words.commentary: From Latin considerare, to observe the stars. CO (together) + SIDUS (star). Something considerable deserves the same careful study once given to reading the stars.
words.tip: CONSIDER + ABLE.
words.example: There was considerable damage to the roof.

words.tip: CONSIST + ENCY. Ends in ENCY.
words.example: Consistency is key to success.

words.commentary: From Latin consistere, to stand firm. CO (together) + SISTERE (to stand). Consistency is standing firm in the same place — reliability through unchanging position.
words.tip: Remember: conSISTent - SIST means to stand, so it stands with 'ent' ending
words.example: Her performance has been consistent throughout the entire season.

words.commentary: From French consommer, to complete or concentrate. A clarified broth. Double m, accent on the final e. The soup is 'completed' through a careful clarification process.
words.tip: Think 'consume' but double the m and add accent: consoMMÉ
words.example: The chef served a crystal-clear consommé as the first course of the tasting menu.

words.commentary: From Latin conspicuus, visible. CON (thoroughly) + SPECERE (to look). Something conspicuous is thoroughly visible — impossible to overlook.
words.tip: Remember SPIC in conSPICuous - like spick and span, thoroughly visible!
words.example: The bright red sports car was conspicuous in the parking lot full of beige sedans.

words.commentary: From Latin conspirare, to breathe together. CON (together) + SPIRARE (to breathe). Conspirators literally breathe as one — unity in secrecy.
words.tip: CONSPIR + ACY. Think: conspire + acy.
words.example: The conspiracy theory spread online.

words.commentary: From Latin constellatio, a set of stars. CON (together) + STELLA (star). A constellation is a group of stars seen together — patterns humanity projects onto the sky.
words.tip: Two L's. CONSTELLAT + ION.
words.example: Orion is a famous constellation.

words.commentary: From Latin consternatio, alarm. Consternation is dismay that paralyzes — shock so complete it freezes the mind.
words.tip: Remember: conSTERNation - stern alarm that freezes you with shock
words.example: The sudden announcement of layoffs caused widespread consternation among the employees.

words.commentary: From Latin constituere, to set up. CON (together) + STATUERE (to set). To constitute is to set things together into a whole — building from parts.
words.tip: Remember: CON-STI-TUTE - 'stitute' like 'institute' but with CON before it
words.example: These twelve members constitute the entire board of directors.

words.commentary: From Latin constitutio, an arrangement. CO (together) + STATUERE (to set up). A constitution is what is set up together — the fundamental arrangement a society agrees upon.
words.tip: CONSTITU + TION. Think: constitute.
words.example: The constitution protects free speech.

words.commentary: From Latin constringere, to bind together. CON (together) + STRINGERE (to bind). A constraint binds your options — limits tightened around freedom.
words.tip: Think 'string' inside - constraint contains STRING to bind you tight.
words.example: The tight budget was a major constraint on our vacation plans.

words.tip: CONSTRUCT + ION.
words.example: The construction project took two years.

words.commentary: From Latin consultare, to deliberate. CO (together) + a root related to counsel. A consultant is someone you deliberate with — expertise offered through conversation.
words.tip: Think 'consul' + 'tant' - like a Roman consul who gives advice
words.example: The marketing consultant recommended a complete rebranding strategy for the startup.

words.commentary: From Latin consummare, to complete. CON (together) + SUMMA (sum). To consummate is to bring to completion — totaling everything into a finished whole.
words.tip: Remember: CON-SUM-MATE - double M like 'sum' reaching the maximum
words.example: The wedding ceremony will consummate their lifelong commitment to each other.

words.tip: CONSUMP + TION. Think: consume.
words.example: Excessive consumption of sugar is unhealthy.

words.commentary: From Latin contagio, a touching together. CON (together) + TANGERE (to touch). Disease spreads by contact — contagion is literally touching together.
words.tip: Remember: conTAGious has TAG in it - diseases TAG along by touching together
words.example: Her laughter was so contagious that soon the entire room was giggling uncontrollably.

words.commentary: From Latin contaminare, to make impure. CO (together) + TANGERE (to touch). To contaminate is to touch with corruption — purity lost through unwanted contact.
words.tip: Remember: conTAMinate has TAMIN in the middle, like vitamin but with TAM
words.example: The oil spill threatens to contaminate the entire water supply.

words.commentary: From Latin contemplari, to gaze at. CON (with) + TEMPLUM (temple). To contemplate was originally to gaze at a sacred space — meditation as temple-watching.
words.tip: Remember: contemplate has TEMPLE inside - like gazing at a sacred temple.
words.example: She would sit by the window each morning to contemplate the meaning of her dreams.

words.commentary: From Latin contemporarius, of the same time. CO (together) + TEMPUS (time). Something contemporary shares your time — existing in the same moment of history.
words.tip: CON + TEMPOR + ARY.
words.example: She enjoys contemporary art.

words.commentary: From Latin contemnere, to scorn. CON plus TEMPT.
words.tip: From Latin contemnere, to scorn.
words.example: He was held in contempt of court.

words.commentary: From Latin contiguus, bordering upon. CON (together) + TANGERE (to touch). Things contiguous touch each other — adjacency without gap.
words.tip: Remember TANGERE (to touch) - keep the U after G like 'tangible'
words.example: The wildfire spread rapidly across three contiguous counties before firefighters could contain it.

words.commentary: From Latin contingere, to touch together. CON (together) + TANGERE (to touch). Something contingent depends on contact with something else — outcome touching cause.
words.tip: Remember: con-TIN-gent has TIN in the middle, like a container that holds possibilities
words.example: The outdoor concert is contingent on favorable weather conditions.

words.commentary: From Italian contrabbando, against the ban. CONTRA (against) + BANNUM (decree). Contraband defies the decree — goods that exist in violation of authority.
words.tip: Remember: CONTRA-BAND defies the ban, so it needs the full 'band' ending
words.example: The customs officers discovered contraband cigarettes hidden in the truck's cargo compartment.

words.commentary: From Latin contradicere, to speak against. CONTRA (against) + DICERE (to speak). To contradict is to speak in opposition — words set against words.
words.tip: Remember CONTRA-DICT: speaking against, like a dictionary speaks words
words.example: The witness began to contradict his earlier testimony during cross-examination.

words.commentary: From Latin contravenire, to come against. CONTRA (against) + VENIRE (to come). To contravene is to come against a rule — violation through opposition.
words.tip: Remember CONTRA-VENE: against + come. The 'a' comes after 'contr' like in 'contrary'.
words.example: The new policy would contravene several existing labor laws.

words.commentary: From Latin contribuere, to bring together. CON (together) + TRIBUERE (to assign). To contribute is to bring your portion to the collective pool.
words.tip: Remember: CON-TRIB-UTE - tribute goes to the collective cause
words.example: She decided to contribute her savings to the community garden project.

words.tip: CONTRIBU + TION. Think: contribute.
words.example: Your contribution to the project was invaluable.

words.commentary: From Latin contritus, ground to pieces. CON (thoroughly) + TERERE (to rub). Someone contrite has been ground down by guilt — remorse that wears away pride.
words.tip: Remember: CON-TRITE rhymes with 'spite' - guilt grinds you down completely.
words.example: After breaking his mother's vase, the boy gave a contrite apology.

words.commentary: From Latin controversia, a turning against. CONTRA (against) + VERSUS (turned). A controversy is a turning against — opposing positions facing each other.
words.tip: CONTROVERS + IAL. Think: controversy.
words.example: The decision was controversial.

words.commentary: From Latin controversia, a turning against. CONTRA (against) + VERTERE (to turn). A controversy is a turning of opinions against each other — debate where positions face off.
words.tip: Remember CONTRO-VERS-Y: against turning - vers like reverse, not -var-
words.example: The new policy sparked controversy among faculty members.

words.commentary: Origin obscure, possibly mock-Latin from Oxford. A conundrum is a riddle that resists solution — a puzzle whose answer seems always just out of reach.
words.tip: Remember: con-UN-drum (like a drum you can't figure out how to play)
words.example: The detective faced a conundrum: all three suspects had perfect alibis.

words.commentary: From Latin convalescere, to grow strong. CON (together) + VALESCERE (to grow strong). A convalescent is growing strong again — recovery gathering momentum.
words.tip: Remember: conVALescent - growing strong with VALor, not VELor
words.example: The convalescent home provided excellent care during her recovery from surgery.

words.commentary: ENCE not ANCE. The word literally means coming together, from Latin convenire. When everything comes together conveniently, you've got convenience.
words.tip: CONVENI + ENCE. Think: convenient.
words.example: The store is open for your convenience.

words.commentary: From Latin convenire, to come together. CO (together) + VENIRE (to come). Something convenient comes together easily — it fits without effort or strain.
words.tip: Remember: conVENient - the VEN comes together smoothly, no extra I needed
words.example: The convenient location of the grocery store made weekly shopping much easier.

words.commentary: From Latin conventio, a coming together. CO (together) + VENIRE (to come). Something conventional is what people have come together to agree upon — shared norms.
words.tip: Remember: people VENT together at CONVENtions - double 'n' in conventional.
words.example: She challenged the conventional wisdom that success requires working 80-hour weeks.

words.tip: CONVERS + ATION. Think: converse.
words.example: We had an interesting conversation.

words.commentary: From Latin convincere, to conquer completely. CO (intensive) + VINCERE (to conquer). A conviction conquers doubt — whether a legal verdict or a deeply held belief.
words.tip: Remember: conVICtion - the C conquers doubt completely, just like in conVINCE.
words.example: Her conviction that education could change lives drove her to become a teacher.

words.commentary: From Latin convivialis, of a feast. CON (together) + VIVERE (to live). Something convivial has the spirit of a feast — living together joyfully.
words.tip: Remember: CON + VIVIAL like 'vivid' - living together vividly at a feast
words.example: The pub's convivial atmosphere made strangers feel like old friends within minutes.

words.commentary: From Latin cooperari, to work together. CO (together) + OPERARI (to work). Cooperation is literally working as one — individual efforts united.
words.tip: CO + OPERAT + ION. Think: operate together.
words.example: International cooperation is needed.

words.commentary: From Latin cooperari, to work together. CO (together) + OPERARI (to work). A cooperative works together — shared labor organized for mutual benefit.
words.tip: CO-OPERATE: together we operate with one 'r' in each part
words.example: The local farmers formed a cooperative to share equipment and reduce costs.

words.commentary: From Latin coordinare, to arrange together. CO (together) + ORDINARE (to arrange). To coordinate is to bring separate things into ordered arrangement — harmony through organization.
words.tip: Remember CO-ORDINATE: CO (together) + ORDINATE (arrange) - no hyphen needed in American English
words.example: The project manager will coordinate the efforts of all three departments to meet the deadline.

words.commentary: From Latin copiosus, plentiful. COPIA (abundance). Something copious overflows with abundance — plenty that exceeds what was expected.
words.tip: Think COPIA (abundance) + -ous: the 'i' makes it copious, not copus
words.example: She took copious notes during the fascinating lecture on ancient history.

words.commentary: From English copy + right. The right to copy — legal ownership of the ability to reproduce creative work. A modern word for a modern concept of intellectual property.
words.tip: COPY + RIGHT, not WRITE. It's about your right to copy.
words.example: The image is protected by copyright.

words.commentary: From Latin cordialis, of the heart. COR (heart). Something cordial comes from the heart — warmth that is genuine, not performed.
words.tip: Remember COR (heart) + DIAL - cordial warmth comes from the heart.
words.example: The host gave us a cordial welcome with warm smiles and refreshments.

words.commentary: From Latin coronatio, a crowning. CORONA (crown or wreath). A coronation places the crown — the moment when authority is symbolically transferred through a circle of gold.
words.tip: Remember CORONA (crown) + ATION (action) - the action of crowning.
words.example: The coronation of King Charles III was watched by millions worldwide.

words.commentary: From Latin corporalis, of the body. CORPUS (body). Corporal relates to the physical body — punishment or rank, both rooted in flesh.
words.tip: Remember CORP-oral: think of a military CORP with oral commands for discipline.
words.example: The sergeant administered corporal punishment to the disobedient recruit.

words.commentary: From Latin corporare, to form into a body. CORPUS (body). A corporation is a legal body — an abstract entity given the rights of a physical person.
words.tip: CORPORAT + ION. Think: corporate.
words.example: The corporation has offices worldwide.

words.commentary: From Latin correlatio, a mutual relation. COR (together) + RELATIO (relation). Correlation describes things that relate to each other — moving in tandem.
words.tip: Two R's. CORRELAT + ION.
words.example: There is a strong correlation between the two.

words.commentary: From Latin correspondere, to answer together. COR (together) + RESPONDERE (to answer). Correspondence is mutual answering — ideas exchanged across distance.
words.tip: Remember: COR + RESPOND (answer together) - keep the E in respond!
words.example: The pen pals correspond regularly, sharing stories about their different cultures.

words.commentary: Two R's and ENCE at the end. The word shares its root with respond — correspondence is a back-and-forth response between people.
words.tip: Two R's, ends in ENCE. CORRESPOND + ENCE.
words.example: Please send all correspondence to this address.

words.commentary: From Latin correspondere, to answer together. CO (together) + RESPONDERE (to answer). A correspondent answers back — communication as a mutual exchange across distance.
words.tip: Remember: correspondent ends in -ENT (like agent), not -ANT
words.example: The war correspondent filed her report from the front lines.

words.commentary: From Italian corridore, a running place. Two R's. A corridor is literally a place for running — a passageway designed for movement.
words.tip: Remember: corridore (Italian runner) - two R's, one D, ends in OR
words.example: The hospital corridor echoed with the sound of hurried footsteps.

words.commentary: From Latin corroborare, to strengthen. CON (together) + ROBORARE (to make strong). To corroborate is to make evidence stronger — confirmation that reinforces.
words.tip: Remember: corROBorate - think 'robust' to make evidence stronger together
words.example: The witness testimony will corroborate the defendant's alibi for that evening.

words.commentary: From Greek kosmopolites, citizen of the world. KOSMOS (world) + POLITES (citizen). As a cocktail, popularized by Sex and the City. The full spelling trips people on the 'opo' in the middle.
words.tip: Remember: COSMO-POLI-TAN - cosmos + polites (citizen), keep the double 'o' intact
words.example: The cosmopolitan city attracted people from every corner of the globe.

words.commentary: From Old French contrefait, made in opposition. CONTRE (against) + FAIT (made). Something counterfeit is made against the original — imitation designed to deceive.
words.tip: COUNTER + FEIT. EI not IE.
words.example: The police seized counterfeit money.

words.commentary: From Old French corage, heart. Latin COR (heart). Courage comes from the heart — bravery understood as a quality of the chest, not the mind.
words.tip: Keep the E from courage. COURAGE + OUS.
words.example: It was a courageous act of bravery.

words.commentary: From French courge, gourd, plus ette. The British term for zucchini.
words.tip: From French courge, gourd, plus ette.
words.example: She sliced the courgette thinly for the salad.

words.commentary: From Old French corteis, having court manners. The E before OUS is essential. Courteous behavior originally meant acting as one would at court.
words.tip: COURTE + OUS. Keep the E.
words.example: The staff were courteous and helpful.

words.commentary: A compound of COURT + YARD. Courts originally meant enclosed spaces. A courtyard is an enclosed open area — a paradox of architecture that somehow makes sense.
words.tip: COURT + YARD.
words.example: The courtyard had a beautiful fountain.

words.commentary: From Berber/Arabic kuskus, pounded small. Steamed semolina granules. The repeating 'cous' pattern: c-o-u-s twice. Not 'cuscus' or 'cooscoose'.
words.tip: Remember: it's 'cous-cous' - the Arabic word repeated twice, like the dish's tiny doubled grains.
words.example: She served fluffy couscous with roasted vegetables and harissa sauce.

words.commentary: From Latin credentialis, giving authority. CREDERE (to believe). Credentials make others believe in your authority — trust documented on paper.
words.tip: Remember CRED-ent-ial: like CREDible, it's about belief and trust.
words.example: She presented her teaching credential to the school administrator.

words.commentary: From Latin credentialis, giving authority to believe. CREDERE (to believe). Credentials are what give others reason to believe in you — trust made portable through documentation.
words.tip: Remember CRED-EN-TIALS: you need them to be credible, so don't lose the 'i'
words.example: The job applicant's impressive credentials included a PhD from Harvard and five years of research experience.

words.commentary: From Latin credibilis, worthy of belief. CREDERE (to believe). Something credible earns belief — trustworthy enough to accept without further proof.
words.tip: Remember CREDIBLE has -ible ending like other Latin words: visible, terrible, possible.
words.example: The witness gave a credible account of what happened that night.

words.commentary: From French creme (cream) + de (of) + menthe (mint). CREME DE MENTHE. Three separate words. The accent on creme is often dropped in English. Not 'cream de mint' or 'creme de menth'.
words.tip: French for 'cream of mint' - keep the French spellings: crEme and menthE
words.example: The bartender added a splash of crème de menthe to give the cocktail its distinctive green color.

words.commentary: From Italian crescendo, growing. CRESCERE (to grow). A crescendo is sound growing — volume rising toward its peak.
words.tip: Remember Italian 'crescere' (to grow) - CRESC-endo with 'sc' like 'crescit'
words.example: The orchestra built to a powerful crescendo in the final movement.

words.commentary: From Old French crevace, a crack. The ICE ending replaces the French -ACE. A crevice is a narrow crack — the word sounds as tight as its meaning.
words.tip: Remember: French -ace becomes English -ice in crevice
words.example: The lizard squeezed through a narrow crevice in the rock wall.

words.commentary: From Greek kriterion, a means of judging. The singular is criterion; the plural is criteria. Mixing up the forms is one of English's most common grammatical errors.
words.tip: CRITER + ION. Plural: criteria.
words.example: The main criterion for selection is experience.

words.commentary: No Z, despite the pronunciation. The ISM suffix is always ISM, never IZM. The word comes from Greek kritikos, meaning able to judge.
words.tip: CRITIC + ISM. Two I's separated by C's.
words.example: Constructive criticism helps us improve.

words.commentary: From French croissant, crescent. CROITRE (to grow). Named for its crescent moon shape. The oi makes a 'wah' sound, the ss is soft, and the final t is silent. A minefield of French phonetics.
words.tip: Think 'crescent moon' - CROI-SS-ANT with silent T, like French croître (to grow).
words.example: She savored the buttery croissant with her morning coffee at the Parisian café.

words.commentary: From Latin crucibulum, a night lamp. The -IBLE ending. A crucible tests materials through extreme heat — metaphorically, any severe trial.
words.tip: Remember the -IBLE ending like other testing words: terrible, horrible, incredible.
words.example: The startup's first year was a crucible that tested the founders' resolve.

words.commentary: From French crudité, rawness. CRUDUS (Latin, raw). Raw vegetables served as appetizers. The accent on the e is important — without it, the pronunciation shifts.
words.tip: Think 'crude' plus 'ités' - raw vegetables need that French accent aigu
words.example: The party platter featured colorful crudités with three different dipping sauces.

words.commentary: From Greek kryptos, hidden, plus currency. CRYPTO plus CURRENCY.
words.tip: From Greek kryptos, hidden, plus currency.
words.example: He invested a small amount in cryptocurrency.

words.commentary: From French cuire, to cook. CUI plus SINE.
words.tip: From French cuire, to cook.
words.example: French cuisine is renowned worldwide.

words.commentary: From Latin culminare, to reach the highest point. CULMEN (summit). To culminate is to reach the summit — everything building toward one peak moment.
words.tip: Remember CULMEN (summit) - it's CUL-MIN-ATE, like climbing to the minimum peak.
words.example: Years of training will culminate in her performance at the Olympics.

words.commentary: From Latin cultivare, to till. The word began with soil and expanded to minds — we cultivate gardens and cultivate knowledge using the same metaphor.
words.tip: CULTIVAT + ION. Think: cultivate.
words.example: The cultivation of rice requires water.

words.commentary: From Latin cumulare, to heap. CUMULUS (heap). Something cumulative builds by heaping — each addition sitting atop the last, growing upward.
words.tip: Think CUMULUS clouds heaping up: cum-u-la-tive, not cum-m-u-la-tive
words.example: The cumulative effect of daily exercise became apparent after three months of training.

words.commentary: Named after the Caribbean island of Curacao, where bitter oranges grow. CU-RA-CAO. Often written with a cedilla under the c (Curacao). Not 'curacow' or 'curaso'.
words.tip: Think Caribbean island: CU-RA-CAO with three syllables, not 'cow' ending
words.example: The bartender added blue curacao to give the cocktail its tropical color.

words.commentary: Drop the U from curious to get curiosity. It seems counterintuitive, but English often simplifies vowel combinations when adding suffixes.
words.tip: CURIOS + ITY. Drop the U from curious.
words.example: Curiosity killed the cat.

words.commentary: Origin uncertain, possibly from cur (dog) + mudgeon (grimace). A curmudgeon grumbles at everything — grumpiness elevated to a personality trait.
words.tip: Remember the 'cur' dog and 'mudge' - a grumpy cur that won't budge.
words.example: The old curmudgeon complained loudly about kids walking across his perfectly manicured lawn.

words.commentary: From Latin curriculum, a running course. CURRERE (to run). A curriculum is a course to be run — education as a path with a beginning, direction, and destination.
words.tip: Two R's. CURRIC + ULUM.
words.example: The school updated its curriculum.

words.commentary: From Latin cursorius, of running. CURSUS (a running). A cursory glance runs over the surface — speed sacrificing depth for coverage.
words.tip: Think 'cursor' running across screen - cursory glance runs quickly over surface.
words.example: She gave the contract only a cursory review before signing it.

words.commentary: From Greek kybernetes plus security. CYBER plus SECURITY.
words.tip: From Greek kybernetes plus security.
words.example: Cybersecurity threats are increasing every year.

words.commentary: From Greek kylindros, a roller. The Y in the first syllable is Greek. A cylinder rolls — the geometry is in the etymology.
words.tip: CYL + INDER. Y not I.
words.example: The engine has four cylinders.

words.commentary: From Greek kynikos, dog-like. The Cynics were ancient philosophers who lived like dogs — simply and without shame. The Y is Greek heritage.
words.tip: Remember the Y from Greek - like the Cynic philosophers who lived dog-like
words.example: After years of broken promises, she became cynical about politicians' motives.

words.commentary: From Daiquiri, a beach near Santiago de Cuba where this rum cocktail was reportedly invented. DAI-QUI-RI. The 'qui' in the middle trips everyone up. Not 'daquiri' or 'daquerri'. Think: I QUIt after one daiquiri.
words.tip: Remember the Cuban beach: DAI-QUI-RI keeps the 'qui' from quitting
words.example: She ordered a frozen strawberry daiquiri at the beachside bar.

words.commentary: A modern compound: DATA + BASE. Coined in the 1960s as computing created the need to name organized collections of information.
words.tip: DATA + BASE.
words.example: The database stores all customer records.

words.commentary: From Middle English derthe, costliness. A dearth is scarcity — shortage that drives up the price of what remains.
words.tip: Remember 'dear' + 'th' - when things are scarce, they become dear (expensive).
words.example: The drought caused a dearth of fresh vegetables in the local markets.

words.commentary: From French debacle, an unleashing. DE (un) + BACLER (to bar). A debacle is barriers unleashed — collapse when what held things together suddenly fails.
words.tip: Remember: DE-BAC-LE - barriers unleashed, not 'baukle' or 'backle'
words.example: The software launch became a complete debacle when the servers crashed during the presentation.

words.commentary: From French debris, broken pieces. DE (down) + BRISER (to break). Debris is what remains after breaking down — fragments scattered by destruction.
words.tip: Remember: DE-BRIS (like 'brisk' without the 'k') - broken pieces scattered down.
words.example: The cleanup crew worked for hours to clear the debris from the collapsed building.

words.commentary: From de plus bug. Grace Hopper found an actual moth in a computer in 1947.
words.tip: From de plus bug.
words.example: He spent hours debugging the code.

words.commentary: From Latin decadentia, a falling away. DECA (ten) relates to falling in tens — decline measured in decades. The -ENCE ending is Latin standard.
words.tip: Remember: deca-DENCE (not dance) - think 'dense' with falling away
words.example: The opulent palace parties symbolized the decadence of the aristocracy before the revolution.

words.commentary: From Latin decadentia, falling away. DE (down) + CADERE (to fall). Decadence is falling from standards — luxury that corrupts as it indulges.
words.tip: Remember it's deca-DENT like a dent from falling down, not -DANT
words.example: The decadent chocolate cake was rich beyond all reason.

words.commentary: From French decanter, to pour off. DE-CAN-TER. One c, one t. Not 'decantor' or 'decantir'. The vessel you pour wine into to let it breathe.
words.tip: Remember: decaNTer ends in -nter, like center or enter.
words.example: She carefully poured the vintage Bordeaux into a crystal decanter to let it breathe before dinner.

words.commentary: EI after C, following the I-before-E rule. From Old French deceite. The word is related to deceive, and both follow the same EI pattern after C.
words.tip: Remember: deCEIt - EI after C, like receive and conceive
words.example: His elaborate story was nothing but deceit designed to hide the truth.

words.commentary: From Latin decipere, to catch or ensnare. DE (from) + CAPERE (to take). Deception takes you away from truth — a trap baited with falsehood.
words.tip: I before E except after C. deCEIve.
words.example: Appearances can deceive you.

words.commentary: From Latin decipere, to ensnare. DE (from) + CAPERE (to take). Something deceptive takes you away from truth — misdirection as a form of capture.
words.tip: Remember CAPERE (to take) - deceptIVE takes you from truth, not makes you receptive
words.example: The calm surface of the lake was deceptive, hiding dangerous currents beneath.

words.commentary: From DE (reverse) + CIPHER (code). To decipher is to reverse the code — reading what was deliberately made unreadable.
words.tip: Remember: DE + CIPHER. Keep the PH from cipher when you decipher.
words.example: The archaeologist worked for months to decipher the ancient hieroglyphics on the tomb wall.

words.commentary: SION not TION. From Latin decisio, meaning a cutting off. When you make a decision, you're cutting off other options.
words.tip: DECI + SION. One S only.
words.example: Making the right decision takes courage.

words.commentary: From Latin decidere, to cut off. DE (off) + CAEDERE (to cut). A decisive moment cuts off alternatives — choice that eliminates other paths.
words.tip: Think 'decide' + 'ive' — cuts off doubt with one 's'
words.example: Her decisive action during the crisis saved the company from bankruptcy.

words.commentary: From Latin declarare, to make clear. DE (fully) + CLARARE (to make clear). A declaration makes something fully clear — truth announced without ambiguity.
words.tip: DECLARAT + ION. Think: declare.
words.example: The declaration was signed.

words.tip: DECOR + ATE. Think: decor.
words.example: They hired someone to decorate the house.

words.commentary: From Latin decorare, to beautify. DECUS (ornament). Decoration adds beauty to the plain — ornament that transforms the ordinary.
words.tip: Remember DECOR + ATION - one R in decor, just like decorating a room.
words.example: The holiday decoration on the mantelpiece caught everyone's attention with its sparkling lights.

words.commentary: From Latin decorum, that which is fitting. DECOR (beauty). Decorum is behaving beautifully — conduct that fits the occasion with grace.
words.tip: Remember DECOR + UM: beautiful conduct ends with -um like Latin words
words.example: The ambassador maintained perfect decorum throughout the tense diplomatic meeting.

words.commentary: From Latin deducere, to lead away. DE (away) + DUCERE (to lead). A deduction leads away from the general to the specific — reasoning that narrows toward truth.
words.tip: Remember: DE-DUC-TION. One D, then DUC (like duck), then TION.
words.example: After reviewing all the evidence, her deduction about the cause of the fire proved correct.

words.commentary: From Latin defamare, to spread evil report. DE (away) + FAMA (fame). Defamation takes fame away — reputation destroyed through false speech.
words.tip: Remember: DE-FAME-ATION destroys fame, not deforms it
words.example: The newspaper issued an apology after the celebrity sued for defamation.

words.commentary: The C in defence follows French spelling conventions, while the S in defense follows Latin more directly. Both are pronounced identically — the difference is purely orthographic.
words.tip: US: defense (with S). UK/AU/NZ: defence (with C).
words.example: The team's defence was outstanding.

words.tip: Ends in ANT not ENT. DEFEND + ANT.
words.example: The defendant pleaded not guilty.

words.commentary: From Latin defensus via French defence. Webster switched to SE to match the verb defend.
words.tip: US uses SE. UK, CA, AU, NZ use CE.
words.example: The team played strong defense all game.

words.commentary: From Latin deferre, to carry away. DE (away) + FERRE (to carry). Deference carries your will away in favor of another's — yielding from respect.
words.tip: One F, two Rs, two Es: defer + ence = deference
words.example: She showed deference to her mentor by carefully considering his advice before making any decisions.

words.commentary: From DE (reverse) + FIBRILLATION (quivering). A device that reverses heart fibrillation. Double-l before the -ator ending. The 'brill' section is often misspelled.
words.tip: Remember: DE-FIBRILLATOR keeps the double-L from fibrillation, ends with -ATOR not -ATER
words.example: The paramedic quickly retrieved the defibrillator to restore the patient's normal heart rhythm.

words.commentary: From Latin deficere, to fail. DE (away) + FACERE (to do). A deficiency is a failing away from what is needed — a gap between requirement and reality.
words.tip: DEFICI + ENCY. Think: deficient.
words.example: Vitamin D deficiency is common in winter.

words.commentary: No A anywhere in this word, despite how many people write 'definate.' The root is the Latin finire, meaning to limit or end — definite means with clear limits.
words.tip: Think 'finite' - definite has clear finite limits, no A needed
words.example: The teacher gave us a definite deadline for the project submission.

words.tip: FINITE is in the middle: deFINITEly. No A anywhere.
words.example: I will definitely be there by noon.

words.commentary: From Latin defunctus, finished. DE (completely) + FUNGI (to perform). Something defunct has completely finished performing — existence concluded, purpose spent.
words.tip: Remember: DE-FUNCT like FUNCTION - something that no longer functions
words.example: The defunct factory still loomed over the abandoned neighborhood.

words.commentary: From Latin degradare, to reduce in rank. DE (down) + GRADUS (step). Degradation is stepping down — loss of quality, status, or dignity measured in descending stairs.
words.tip: Remember: deGRADation has GRAD (step) - you step down gradually
words.example: The environmental degradation of the wetlands accelerated after the factory opened upstream.

words.commentary: DE (remove) + HYDRATE (water). Greek hydor means water. To dehydrate is to remove water — the word is pure Greek-Latin hybrid.
words.tip: Remember: DE + HYDRATE. Think 'hydro' for water - no extra 'e' after 'r'.
words.example: Athletes need to drink plenty of fluids or they will dehydrate quickly during intense exercise.

words.commentary: From Latin delegare, to send away. DE (away) + LEGARE (to send). A delegate is sent on behalf of others — an authorized representative.
words.tip: Remember: DE-LEG-ATE. Think 'leg' in the middle - delegates walk to represent you.
words.example: The union chose Maria as their delegate to negotiate with management.

words.commentary: From Greek deleterios, destructive. DELEISTHAI (to hurt). Something deleterious causes harm — damage that accumulates quietly until the toll becomes visible.
words.tip: Remember: DELETE + RIOUS, not 'deletorious' - it's about harm, not deletion
words.example: The deleterious effects of prolonged stress on cardiovascular health became apparent after months of overwork.

words.commentary: From Latin deliberare, to weigh carefully. DE (thoroughly) + LIBRARE (to weigh). To deliberate is to weigh thoroughly — careful consideration before commitment.
words.tip: DELIBER + ATE. Think: a deliberate act.
words.example: It was a deliberate attempt to mislead.

words.commentary: From Latin delicatus, pleasing. DELICERE (to allure). A delicacy is something that allures — food so refined it seduces the palate.
words.tip: Remember: deli-CAC-y has 'cac' in the middle, like cactus needs delicate care.
words.example: The chef's chocolate soufflé was considered a true delicacy at the restaurant.

words.tip: DELIC + IOUS. One L.
words.example: The cake was absolutely delicious.

words.commentary: From Latin delineare, to sketch out. DE (completely) + LINEA (line). To delineate is to draw the complete outline — making boundaries visible.
words.tip: Remember: DE-LINE-ATE - you draw a LINE to delineate boundaries
words.example: The survey will delineate the exact property boundaries between the two lots.

words.commentary: From Latin delinquere, to fail or offend. DE (away) + LINQUERE (to leave). A delinquent leaves duty behind — the QU combination is Latin.
words.tip: Remember the QU combo from Latin linquere - delinquent leaves duty behind
words.example: The delinquent borrower failed to make payments for three consecutive months.

words.commentary: From Latin delirare, to go off the furrow. DE (away) + LIRA (furrow). Delirium was originally a farming metaphor — a plow gone astray.
words.tip: Remember the furrow: deLIRious comes from Latin LIRA (furrow) - don't go astray!
words.example: The fever made him delirious, and he spoke incoherently through the night.

words.commentary: From Latin diluvium, a flood. Something that deluges overwhelms with volume — water, information, or demands pouring in faster than you can absorb.
words.tip: Remember 'deluge' ends in -uge like 'huge' - a huge flood overwhelms.
words.example: The heavy rains brought a deluge that flooded the entire valley.

words.commentary: From Old French demener, to conduct oneself. DE (completely) + MENER (to lead). Your demeanor is how you lead yourself — outward conduct reflecting inner state.
words.tip: Remember MEAN in the middle - your demeanor shows what you MEAN by your behavior.
words.example: Despite the criticism, she maintained a calm and professional demeanor throughout the meeting.

words.commentary: From Latin dimittere, to send away. DE (away) + MITTERE (to send). Demise is being sent away from life — departure that admits no return.
words.tip: Remember: DE + MISE like 'dismiss' - sending away from life
words.example: The demise of the old theater marked the end of an era.

words.commentary: From Greek demos (people) + kratos (rule). The word literally means rule by the people. The RACY ending sometimes gets swapped with RASY.
words.tip: DEMO + CRACY. People rule.
words.example: Democracy depends on voter participation.

words.commentary: From Latin demoliri, to tear down. DE (down) + MOLIRI (to build). To demolish is to un-build — construction reversed, structures returned to rubble.
words.tip: Remember: DEMOLish ends like 'abolish' - both tear things down completely.
words.example: The city council voted to demolish the abandoned warehouse next month.

words.commentary: From Latin demonstrare, to point out. DE (completely) + MONSTRARE (to show). A demonstration shows completely — proof made visible and undeniable.
words.tip: DEMONSTR + ATE. Think: demon + strate.
words.example: Let me demonstrate how it works.

words.commentary: From Latin demonstrare, to point out fully. DE (fully) + MONSTRARE (to show). A demonstration shows something completely — whether in protest or in proof.
words.tip: DEMONSTRAT + ION. Think: demonstrate.
words.example: The demonstration drew thousands.

words.commentary: From Latin denigrare, to blacken. DE (completely) + NIGER (black). To denigrate is to blacken someone's character — reputation darkened through criticism.
words.tip: Remember: DE-NIGR-ATE (like 'niger' meaning black in Latin) - to blacken reputation
words.example: The rival candidate tried to denigrate her opponent's environmental record during the debate.

words.commentary: From Latin denominare, to name. DE (completely) + NOMINARE (to name). A denomination gives a name to a category — identity through naming.
words.tip: Remember NOMIN (name) in the middle - denomination names categories
words.example: The $20 bill is the most commonly used denomination of currency.

words.commentary: From Latin denominare, to name. DE (completely) + NOMINARE (to name). The denominator names what kind of parts we are counting — it gives the fraction its identity.
words.tip: Remember: denomin-ATOR (not -ater) — it's the part that NAMES the fraction
words.example: In the fraction 3/8, the denominator 8 tells us we're working with eighths.

words.commentary: From French denouer, to untie. DE (un) + NOUER (to tie). The denouement unties the plot's knots — resolution that loosens every tangled thread.
words.tip: Remember: DE-NOUE-MENT (untie-ment). Think 'untie' with French flair - no 'w' needed.
words.example: The mystery's denouement revealed that the butler had been innocent all along.

words.commentary: From Old French departir, to divide or separate. A departure is a separating — leaving one place to go to another.
words.tip: DEPART + URE.
words.example: The departure was delayed.

words.tip: DEPEND + ABLE. Ends in ABLE.
words.example: She is a dependable employee.

words.commentary: From Latin dependere, to hang from. DE (from) + PENDERE (to hang). Something dependent hangs from its support — remove the hook and it falls.
words.tip: Think 'depend-ENT' — like an agent who depends on someone else.
words.example: The success of the project is dependent on securing adequate funding.

words.commentary: From Latin deplorare, to weep for. DE (completely) + PLORARE (to weep). Something deplorable deserves complete weeping — conditions so bad they demand tears.
words.tip: Remember: DE-PLOR-ABLE - think 'completely weep-able' from Latin plorare (to weep)
words.example: The deplorable conditions in the refugee camp prompted immediate international aid.

words.commentary: From Latin deponere, to lay down. DE (down) + PONERE (to place). A deposit places something down for safekeeping — value entrusted to another's care.
words.tip: Remember: depo-SIT, not depo-SITE - you SIT money down in the bank.
words.example: She made a deposit of $500 into her savings account before leaving for vacation.

words.commentary: From Latin deponere, to put down. DEP plus O plus SIT plus ION.
words.tip: From Latin deponere, to put down.
words.example: Her deposition lasted three hours.

words.commentary: From Latin deprecari, to pray against. DE (against) + PRECARI (to pray). To deprecate is to pray against — expressing disapproval through solemn objection.
words.tip: Deprecate has one 'i' - you pray against it, not reduce its value
words.example: The coding standards committee decided to deprecate the old function in favor of a more secure alternative.

words.commentary: From Latin deprimere, to press down. DE (down) + PREMERE (to press). Depression presses the spirit downward — weight bearing on the mind.
words.tip: Two S's. DEPRESS + ION.
words.example: Depression is a serious condition.

words.commentary: From Latin deprivare, to strip away. DE (away) + PRIVARE (to rob). Deprivation is being robbed of something essential — loss that diminishes life.
words.tip: Remember: deprIvation means being prIved of something essential - keep the 'i'
words.example: The study examined the effects of sleep deprivation on cognitive performance.

words.commentary: From Latin derelictus, abandoned. DE (completely) + RELINQUERE (to leave). Something derelict has been completely left behind — abandoned to decay.
words.tip: Remember: deRELICt - think 'relic' left behind to decay
words.example: The derelict warehouse stood empty for decades, its broken windows and crumbling walls telling the story of industrial decline.

words.commentary: From Latin derivare, to draw off water. DE (from) + RIVUS (stream). Something derivative flows from a source — drawn from another's stream rather than springing from its own.
words.tip: Remember: derive + ative. Think of a river flowing from its source.
words.example: Her painting style was dismissed as derivative of the Impressionist masters.

words.commentary: From Greek derma (skin) + itis (inflammation). Skin inflammation. The 'erm' beginning is sometimes misspelled 'derm' without the a that follows.
words.tip: Remember: derma (skin) + titis - the 'a' in derma stays!
words.example: The doctor diagnosed her with contact dermatitis caused by the new soap.

words.commentary: From Latin descendere, to climb down. DE (down) + SCANDERE (to climb). A descendant climbs down the family tree — each generation a step further from the origin.
words.tip: Remember: descendANT climbs down like an ANT going down the family tree.
words.example: She discovered that she was a descendant of Irish immigrants who arrived in the 1800s.

words.tip: DESCRIP + TION. Not DIS.
words.example: The description matched the suspect.

words.commentary: Desert is the dry landscape with one S. Dessert is the sweet course with two S.
words.tip: Desert is the dry landscape with one S.
words.example: The Sahara is the largest hot desert in the world.

words.tip: Drop the E from desire. DESIR + ABLE.
words.example: A quiet location makes the house more desirable.

words.commentary: From Latin desolare, to leave alone. DE (completely) + SOLUS (alone). A desolate place is completely alone — emptied of company, comfort, and life.
words.tip: Remember SOLUS (alone) - deSOLate places are completely alone and empty.
words.example: The abandoned farmhouse stood desolate against the winter sky.

words.commentary: The second syllable is PER not PAR. Desperation comes from Latin desperare, to be without hope. The spelling preserves that Latin E.
words.tip: PER in the middle, not PAR. DESPER + ATE.
words.example: They were desperate for help.

words.commentary: From Latin despondere, to give up. DE (away) + SPONDERE (to promise). Someone despondent has given away their promise — hope surrendered, spirit deflated.
words.tip: Remember: despondENT ends like 'sent away' - hope has been sent away.
words.example: After failing the exam for the third time, Maria felt utterly despondent about her chances of becoming a doctor.

words.commentary: Dessert has two S because you always want seconds. Desert has one S.
words.tip: Dessert has two S because you always want seconds.
words.example: She ordered chocolate cake for dessert.

words.commentary: From Latin destinare, to make firm. DE (completely) + STANARE (to stand). A destination is where you firmly stand at journey's end.
words.tip: DESTINAT + ION. Think: destine.
words.example: We arrived at our destination.

words.commentary: From Latin destitutus, abandoned. DE (away) + STATUERE (to set). Someone destitute has been set away from resources — poverty as a form of abandonment.
words.tip: Think 'stitute' like 'stitched' - someone whose resources have been stitched away
words.example: After losing his job and home, he found himself completely destitute with nowhere to turn.

words.tip: DESTRUCT + ION. Not DIS.
words.example: The hurricane caused massive destruction.

words.commentary: From Latin deterrere, to frighten away. DE (away) + TERRERE (to frighten). To deter is to frighten away from action — prevention through fear of consequences.
words.tip: Remember: DE-TER like 'terror' - one R in the middle, one at the end
words.example: The security cameras were installed to deter potential thieves from breaking in.

words.commentary: From Latin deteriorare, to make worse. DETERIOR (worse). To deteriorate is to become worse — decline measured in the growing distance from what was.
words.tip: DETERIOR + ATE.
words.example: The building began to deteriorate.

words.commentary: From Latin determinare, to set bounds. DE (completely) + TERMINARE (to limit). Determination sets firm boundaries around a goal — resolve that refuses to expand or retreat.
words.tip: DETERMIN + ATION. Think: determine.
words.example: Her determination inspired the whole team.

words.commentary: From Latin detrimentum, a wearing away. DE (away) + TERERE (to rub). Something detrimental rubs away — harm through gradual erosion.
words.tip: Remember: deTRImental has TRI like 'rub away in three ways'
words.example: The oil spill had a detrimental effect on marine wildlife in the area.

words.commentary: From Latin devastare, to lay waste. DE (completely) + VASTARE (to waste). To devastate is to waste completely — destruction so thorough it empties the landscape.
words.tip: Remember: DE-VAST-ATE — think 'vast destruction' with DE prefix
words.example: The hurricane will devastate the coastal communities with winds exceeding 150 mph.

words.tip: No E at the end. DEVELOP, not develope.
words.example: They plan to develop the software further.

words.tip: DEVELOP + MENT. No E before MENT.
words.example: The new housing development is nearly complete.

words.commentary: From Latin dexter, right-handed. Right-handedness was associated with skill. Dexterity preserves the ancient bias toward the right hand.
words.tip: Remember 'dexter' (right-handed) + '-ity' - think dexterous hands with skill
words.example: The surgeon's exceptional dexterity allowed her to perform the delicate procedure flawlessly.

words.commentary: From Latin dexter, right-handed. Dexterity was originally right-handedness — skill attributed to the favored hand, now meaning any nimble ability.
words.tip: Remember 'dexter' (right) + 'ous' - like Dexter's right hand being skillful
words.example: The surgeon's dexterous movements saved precious time during the delicate operation.

words.commentary: From Greek diabetes, a siphon. DIA (through) + BAINEIN (to go). The disease where sugar passes through the body. Three syllables, but people often misspell the ending as '-etes' or '-itis'.
words.tip: Remember: diabeTES not diabeTIS - it goes through, not inflamed
words.example: She was diagnosed with type 2 diabetes and had to change her diet significantly.

words.commentary: From Greek diagnosis, discernment. DIA (through) + GNOSIS (knowing). Knowing through examination. Same 'gnos' root as prognosis — knowing runs through medicine.
words.tip: DIAGN + OSIS. Think: know through.
words.example: The diagnosis was confirmed.

words.commentary: From Greek dialektike, the art of debate. DIA (through) + LEGEIN (to speak). Dialectic is speaking through opposing views — truth discovered in the collision of ideas.
words.tip: Remember: DIA + LECTIC - speaking through ideas, not 'dialectical' the adjective
words.example: The philosopher used dialectic to explore the tension between freedom and security.

words.commentary: From Greek dialogos. Both spellings are common in American English.
words.tip: US drops UE. UK, CA, AU, NZ keep it.
words.example: The film had sharp, witty dialog.

words.commentary: From Greek dialogos, a conversation. DIA (through) + LOGOS (speech). A dialogue is speech flowing through two people — meaning created in the space between speakers.
words.tip: US often: dialog. UK: dialogue.
words.example: The dialogue between the characters was sharp.

words.commentary: From Greek dialysis, separation. DIA (apart) + LYSIS (loosening). Separating waste from blood. The y in the middle is commonly replaced with an i.
words.tip: Remember DIA-LY-SIS: apart + loosening + sis, keep the Y in the middle
words.example: The patient undergoes dialysis three times a week to filter toxins from their blood.

words.commentary: From Greek diametros, measuring through. DIA (through) + METRON (measure). The diameter measures through the center — the longest straight line a circle can contain.
words.tip: Remember: DIA-METER measures through - one 'm', ends in -ter like thermometer
words.example: The telescope's lens has a diameter of 8 inches.

words.commentary: The silent G before M is Greek in origin. From Greek diaphragma, meaning a partition. The PH and the GM are both Greek spelling conventions.
words.tip: DIA + PHRAGM. The PH makes an F sound.
words.example: Singers learn to breathe using their diaphragm.

words.commentary: From Greek diarrhoia, flowing through. DIA (through) + RHEIN (to flow). Double-r, then h — the 'rrh' combination is rare in English. British spelling: diarrhoea.
words.tip: Two R's, then H. Dash In A Rush, Run Hard, Else Accident.
words.example: The patient was treated for diarrhea.

words.commentary: From Greek dichotomia, a cutting in two. DICHA (in two) + TEMNEIN (to cut). A dichotomy cuts reality into two parts — division that clarifies by separating.
words.tip: Remember: diCHOtomy cuts in TWO - CH like 'chop' and O like 'two'
words.example: The false dichotomy between work and life balance ignores the complexity of modern careers.

words.commentary: From Latin dictionarium, a collection of words. DICTIO (a saying). A dictionary collects sayings — every word a small act of speech preserved and defined.
words.tip: DICTION + ARY. Ends in ARY.
words.example: Look it up in the dictionary.

words.tip: Two F's, ends in ENCE. DIFFER + ENCE.
words.example: Can you spot the difference?

words.commentary: From Latin differre, to carry apart. DIS (apart) + FERRE (to carry). A differential measures what's carried apart — the gap between two values.
words.tip: Remember: differ-ENT-ial - think 'different' with ENT in the middle
words.example: The salary differential between entry-level and senior positions was quite substantial.

words.commentary: From Latin diffidere, to distrust. DIS (away) + FIDERE (to trust). Someone diffident trusts away from themselves — self-doubt that manifests as shyness.
words.tip: DIS + FIDERE: trust away from yourself, so double-F double-D for self-doubt
words.example: The diffident student rarely spoke up in class despite knowing the answers.

words.commentary: From Latin digressio, a going away. DIS (away) + GRADI (to step). A digression steps away from the path — a detour in thought or speech.
words.tip: Remember: DI-GRES-SION - one G, double S like stepping away
words.example: The professor's digression about his childhood lasted twenty minutes before he returned to the lecture topic.

words.commentary: Two M's, and the word is spelled with an E in the second syllable, not an A. A dilemma originally meant a double proposition in logic — two equally unpleasant options.
words.tip: Two M's, not N. Think: Emma has a dilemma.
words.example: He faced a dilemma between two job offers.

words.commentary: From Latin diligens, careful. DIS (apart) + LEGERE (to choose). A diligent person chooses carefully among options — thoroughness born from selective attention.
words.tip: Remember: diLIgent has one L and ends in -gent (like agent)
words.example: The diligent student reviewed her notes every evening before the exam.

words.commentary: From Latin dimensio, a measuring. DIS (apart) + METIRI (to measure). A dimension is a direction you can measure apart from others — each axis independent.
words.tip: DIMENS + ION. S not T.
words.example: The room has large dimensions.

words.commentary: From Latin diminuere, to make smaller. DE (away) + MINUERE (to lessen). To diminish is to make less — reduction that takes something toward nothing.
words.tip: Remember: one M, two I's - think 'mini' in the middle to make smaller
words.example: The storm's intensity began to diminish as it moved inland.

words.tip: DINO + SAUR. Think: terrible lizard.
words.example: The museum has a dinosaur exhibit.

words.commentary: From Greek diphthera, leather. The disease creates a leather-like membrane in the throat. The 'phth' cluster (as in ophthalmologist) is the spelling nightmare.
words.tip: Remember the Greek 'phth' - like leather (diphthera), it's tough to pronounce but essential.
words.example: The child received a vaccination to prevent diphtheria and other serious diseases.

words.commentary: From Greek diploma, a folded document. DIPLOUS (double). Diplomacy originated with folded documents bearing official seals — negotiation conducted through carefully crafted papers.
words.tip: Remember DIPLOMA + CY: folded documents (diplomas) need diplomacy
words.example: The ambassador's skilled diplomacy helped prevent the trade war between the two nations.

words.commentary: From Greek diploma, a folded document. Diplomats originally carried folded letters of credential. The word evolved from the document to the person carrying it.
words.tip: DIPLOM + AT. Think: diploma.
words.example: The diplomat negotiated peace.

words.commentary: From DIS (not) + ABILITY. A disability names what the standard framework does not accommodate — the word defines by negation, which is why many prefer identity-first alternatives.
words.tip: DIS + ABIL + ITY.
words.example: The law protects people with disabilities.

words.commentary: From DIS (not) + ADVANTAGE. A disadvantage is the absence of advantage — starting from a position where others have more favorable conditions.
words.tip: DIS + ADVANTAGE.
words.example: The weather was a disadvantage.

words.commentary: One S, two P's. Think DIS + APPEAR. The prefix DIS means away, and APPEAR has two P's on its own.
words.tip: DIS + APPEAR: keep the double P from APPEAR, single S from DIS
words.example: The magician made the rabbit disappear in a cloud of smoke.

words.tip: DIS + APPEAR + ANCE. Two P's.
words.example: The disappearance remains a mystery.

words.commentary: One S, two P's. Same pattern as disappear: DIS + APPOINT. The word originally meant to remove from an appointed position.
words.tip: One S, two P's. DIS + APPOINT.
words.example: I hope the results don't disappoint you.

words.tip: DIS + ASTER. Think: bad star (from astrology).
words.example: The oil spill was an environmental disaster.

words.commentary: DISASTER + OUS, dropping the E. No E between T and R. A disaster was originally a bad star alignment — DIS (bad) + ASTRO (star).
words.tip: Remember: disaster drops the E before adding -ous, like the stars aligned badly
words.example: The hurricane had disastrous effects on the coastal communities.

words.commentary: From Latin discernere, to separate. DIS (apart) + CERNERE (to sift). Discernment sifts apart — judgment that separates the valuable from the worthless.
words.tip: Think CERN like the physics lab - disCERNment sifts apart truth from falsehood.
words.example: Her discernment in choosing business partners helped the company avoid several costly mistakes.

words.commentary: From Latin disciplina, instruction. DISCIPULUS (a learner). Discipline is what a disciple follows — the word connects learning, order, and devoted practice.
words.tip: DISC + IPLINE. Think: disciple.
words.example: Success requires discipline.

words.commentary: From Latin discontinuare, to break off. DIS (apart) + CONTINUARE (to continue). To discontinue is to break the thread of continuation.
words.tip: DIS-CONTINUE: breaks apart continuing, so keep the full 'continue' intact
words.example: The company decided to discontinue the popular product line due to supply chain issues.

words.commentary: From Latin discursus, a running about. DIS (apart) + CURRERE (to run). Discourse is thought running in many directions — conversation that explores rather than concludes.
words.tip: Remember: DIS + COURSE - thought running its course in conversation.
words.example: The professor encouraged open discourse about the controversial topic in class.

words.commentary: From Latin discooperire, to uncover. DIS (opposite) + COOPERIRE (to cover). A discovery uncovers what was hidden — revelation through removal.
words.tip: DIS + COVER + Y.
words.example: The discovery of penicillin saved millions.

words.commentary: From Latin discretus, separated. DIS (apart) + CERNERE (to separate). Someone discreet keeps things properly separated — judgment about what to share and what to withhold.
words.tip: Discreet has two E's together - keep your secrets close together too
words.example: She was discreet about her friend's personal problems, never gossiping with others.

words.commentary: From Latin discrepantia, a discord. DIS (apart) + CREPARE (to rattle). A discrepancy rattles apart — numbers or stories that refuse to harmonize.
words.tip: Think 'apart rattling' - DIS-CREP-ANCY keeps the A in CREPARE (to rattle)
words.example: The audit revealed a significant discrepancy between the reported profits and actual sales figures.

words.commentary: Discrete means individually separate. Discreet means tactfully careful.
words.tip: Discrete means individually separate.
words.example: The system had three discrete components.

words.commentary: From Latin discriminare, to distinguish. DIS (apart) + CERNERE (to separate). To discriminate is to separate apart — originally a neutral act of distinction, now carrying the weight of unjust separation.
words.tip: DIS-CRIMIN-ATE: Remember the Latin CRIMIN root meaning 'to separate' - keep all letters intact.
words.example: The hiring manager was trained not to discriminate based on age or background.

words.commentary: From Latin discriminare, to separate. DISCRIMEN (distinction). Discrimination separates things into categories — the word is neutral, the application often isn't.
words.tip: DISCRIMIN + ATION. Not DESCRIM.
words.example: Discrimination in the workplace is illegal.

words.tip: Two S's. DISCUSS + ION.
words.example: The discussion lasted two hours.

words.commentary: From Latin dedignari, to consider unworthy. DE (away) + DIGNUS (worthy). Disdain judges unworthy — contempt that looks down from a great height.
words.tip: Remember: DIS-DAIN - it's 'dis' plus 'dain' like 'deign' to consider worthy
words.example: She looked at the cheap imitation jewelry with obvious disdain.

words.commentary: From Old French desguiser, to change costume. DIS (away) + GUISE (appearance). The GU combination is French. A disguise takes you away from your true appearance.
words.tip: DIS + GUISE. Think: a guise that disguises.
words.example: He wore a clever disguise.

words.commentary: From DIS (away) + ILLUSION. To disillusion is to strip away illusions — revealing reality by removing the comfortable fictions that obscured it.
words.tip: DIS + ILLUSION: strip away illusions with double L, single S
words.example: The scandal served to disillusion many voters about their trusted politician.

words.commentary: From Old French desmanteler, to tear down fortifications. DIS (un) + MANTEL (cloak). To dismantle is to uncloak — stripping away layers until nothing remains.
words.tip: Remember the MANTLE - like a cloak being removed, ending in -LE
words.example: The workers began to dismantle the old playground equipment piece by piece.

words.commentary: From Old French desparagier, to marry unequally. DIS (away) + PARAGE (equal rank). To disparage originally meant to lower in rank — now it means to lower in estimation through words.
words.tip: Remember: DIS + PAR + AGE - think 'apart from equal age/rank'
words.example: The critic's review seemed designed to disparage the young author's debut novel.

words.commentary: From Latin disparitas, inequality. DIS (apart) + PAR (equal). Disparity is the distance between equals — the gap that shouldn't exist but does.
words.tip: Remember PAR in golf - disparity shows the distance from PAR (equal)
words.example: The study revealed a growing disparity in wages between different departments.

words.commentary: From Latin dispositio, arrangement. DIS (apart) + PONERE (to place). Disposition is how things are placed apart — both temperament and arrangement, character as internal organization.
words.tip: Remember: DIS-PO-SI-TION - place (position) things apart with an 'i' after 's'
words.example: Her cheerful disposition made her popular with colleagues and customers alike.

words.commentary: From Latin disseminare, to scatter seed. DIS (widely) + SEMINARE (to sow). To disseminate is to scatter seeds widely — information sown across many minds.
words.tip: Remember: DIS + SEMINATE (like semen/seed) - double S, single M
words.example: The organization will disseminate the research findings to universities worldwide.

words.commentary: From Latin dissolutio, a loosening. DIS (apart) + SOLVERE (to loosen). Dissolution loosens bonds apart — the undoing of what was bound together.
words.tip: DISSOLUT + ION. Two S's.
words.example: The dissolution of the partnership was final.

words.commentary: From Latin dissolvere, to loosen apart. DIS (apart) + SOLVERE (to loosen). Two S's. Dissolving breaks bonds — the double S marks the forceful separation.
words.tip: Two S's to separate: DIS-SOLVE, like scissors cutting bonds apart.
words.example: The sugar will dissolve completely in hot water within minutes.

words.commentary: From Latin distinguere, to separate by pricking. DIS (apart) + STINGUERE (to prick). A distinction is a point of separation — what sets things apart.
words.tip: DISTINCT + ION.
words.example: She graduated with distinction.

words.commentary: From Latin distinguere, to separate by marks. DIS (apart) + STINGUERE (to prick). To distinguish is to mark apart — identifying differences through careful observation.
words.tip: DISTINGU + ISH. Think: distinct.
words.example: Can you distinguish between the two?

words.commentary: From Latin distribuere, to divide among. DIS (apart) + TRIBUERE (to assign). Distribution divides and assigns — portions allocated across many hands.
words.tip: Remember: DIS-TRIB-UTE - the TRIB tribe distributes fairly among all members.
words.example: The charity will distribute food packages to families in need throughout the community.

words.commentary: From Latin distribuere, to deal out. DIS (apart) + TRIBUERE (to assign). Distribution deals out shares — dividing a whole into portions assigned to many.
words.tip: DISTRIBU + TION. Think: distribute.
words.example: The distribution of goods was delayed.

words.commentary: From Latin disturbare, to throw into disorder. DIS (apart) + TURBARE (to confuse). A disturbance throws order apart — disruption that scatters what was arranged.
words.tip: Remember TURB from Latin 'to confuse' — like turbulent waters causing disturbance.
words.example: The loud construction noise created a significant disturbance in the neighborhood.

words.commentary: From Latin divergere, to go in different directions. DIS (apart) + VERGERE (to incline). Divergent paths incline apart — separation that increases with each step.
words.tip: Remember: paths diVERGE apart, keeping the E from Latin vergere (to incline)
words.example: The scientist's divergent theories sparked heated debates among her colleagues.

words.commentary: From Latin docilis, easily taught. DOCERE (to teach). Someone docile is easily taught — receptiveness that welcomes instruction.
words.tip: Think 'docile' ends like 'fragile' - both have the -ile ending
words.example: The docile lamb followed the shepherd without resistance.

words.commentary: From Latin documentum, a lesson or proof. DOCERE (to teach). A documentary teaches through evidence — facts assembled into narrative that instructs.
words.tip: DOCUMENT + ARY. Ends in ARY.
words.example: The documentary won an award.

words.commentary: From Greek dogma, opinion or decree. DOKEIN (to seem). A dogma is what seems true to a group — belief hardened into institutional certainty.
words.tip: Remember Greek DOKEIN (to seem) - what seems true becomes DOGMA
words.example: The committee refused to challenge the old dogma about working from home.

words.commentary: From Latin dolere, to grieve. Something doleful is full of grief — sadness that permeates everything it touches.
words.tip: Remember: dole + ful = doleful (like dole out grief)
words.example: The doleful sound of the church bells echoed through the empty streets.

words.commentary: From Latin domesticus, belonging to the house. DOMUS (house). Domestic affairs belong to the home — the private sphere of household life.
words.tip: DOMEST + IC. Think: domus (home).
words.example: Domestic flights are cheaper.

words.commentary: From Latin domicilium, a dwelling. DOMUS (house). Your domicile is your home — the word carries the weight of Roman household law.
words.tip: Remember DOMUS (house) + CILE: your domicile has the same root as domestic.
words.example: After years of traveling, she finally established her legal domicile in California.

words.commentary: From Latin dominare, to rule. DOMINUS (master). The dominant force rules over others — mastery expressed through power or influence.
words.tip: DOMIN + ANT. Ends in ANT.
words.example: Blue is the dominant color.

words.commentary: From Latin dominare, to rule. DOMINUS (master). To dominate is to master — control exercised from a position of authority or power.
words.tip: Think DOMINUS (master) - DOM-IN-ATE, not DOM-IN-ENT like 'prominent'
words.example: The tall skyscraper dominates the city's skyline.

words.commentary: From German Doppelganger, double walker — a paranormal double of a living person. The umlaut over A is often dropped in English.
words.tip: From German Doppel (double) + Ganger (walker). Your ghostly double.
words.example: She was startled to meet her doppelganger at the conference.

words.commentary: From Latin dormire, to sleep. Something dormant is sleeping — alive but inactive, waiting for the signal to wake.
words.tip: Remember 'dorm-ant' like a dormitory where students sleep - keep the 'a'
words.example: The volcano had been dormant for centuries before suddenly erupting.

words.commentary: From Latin dormitorium, a sleeping place. DORMIRE (to sleep). A dormitory is designed for sleeping — architecture organized around the most basic human need for rest.
words.tip: Remember DORM + ITORY: where you sleep in a dorm, not 'dormatory'
words.example: The college dormitory housed over 200 students across four floors.

words.commentary: From Greek dramatikos, relating to action. DRAMA (action, deed). Something dramatic pertains to action — intense, vivid, and emotionally charged.
words.tip: DRAMAT + IC. Think: drama.
words.example: The change was dramatic.

words.commentary: From Scottish Gaelic dram buidheach, the drink that satisfies. DRAM-BU-IE. A Scotch whisky-based liqueur. Not 'drambuee' or 'drambouie'. The 'ie' ending, not 'ee'.
words.tip: Remember the Scottish Gaelic 'buidheach' - it ends in 'ie' like a Scottish surname.
words.example: After dinner, he savored a glass of Drambuie while watching the Highland sunset.

words.commentary: OUGHT without the usual OUGH sound patterns. This word rhymes with out, making it unique among OUGHT words (thought, bought, brought).
words.tip: Ends in OUGHT, like thought and bought.
words.example: The drought lasted for three months.

words.commentary: From Latin dubius, wavering. DUBITARE (to doubt). Something dubious makes you waver — uncertainty that resists resolution.
words.tip: Think DUB-ious: doubt has a 'b', so does dubious with 'ub'
words.example: The witness gave a dubious account that contradicted the security footage.

words.commentary: From Latin duplicare, to double. DUO (two) + PLICARE (to fold). A duplicate is a second fold — the original folded over to create an identical copy.
words.tip: DUPLIC + ATE. One P.
words.example: Please duplicate this key for me.

words.commentary: From Latin duplicitas, doubleness. DUPLEX (twofold). Duplicity is being twofold — presenting one face while hiding another.
words.tip: Remember DUPLEX + CITY: two-faced like a duplex with double lives
words.example: The politician's duplicity was exposed when his private emails contradicted his public statements.

words.commentary: From Latin durare, to last. DURUS (hard). Duration is lasting — time measured by endurance, persistence through the hard passage of hours.
words.tip: Remember DURE (endure) + ATION - one R, like enduring through time
words.example: The duration of the storm was much longer than meteorologists had predicted.

words.commentary: From Latin duritia, hardness. DURUS (hard). Duress is hardness imposed — pressure so severe it removes the freedom to choose.
words.tip: Remember DURUS (hard) - one R in the middle, double S at end
words.example: The confession was obtained under duress and therefore inadmissible in court.

words.commentary: From Old English dwinan, to waste away. The -LE ending was added in the 16th century. Dwindle is waste that continues — a slow disappearance.
words.tip: Think 'twin' in the middle - dw-IN-dle, like something slowly twinning away to nothing
words.example: As winter approached, their food supplies began to dwindle rapidly.

words.commentary: From Greek dynasteia, power or lordship. DYNASTES (ruler). The Y is Greek. A dynasty is a line of rulers — power passed through blood.
words.tip: Remember the Greek Y: DYN-A-STY (power-line-rule)
words.example: The Ming dynasty ruled China for nearly three centuries.

words.commentary: From Latin ebullire, to bubble up. E (out) + BULLIRE (to boil). Someone ebullient bubbles over with enthusiasm — joy that cannot be contained.
words.tip: Remember: e-BULL-ient - like a bull that bubbles with energy!
words.example: The ebullient crowd cheered wildly as their team scored the winning goal.

words.commentary: From Greek ekkentros, out of center. EK (out) + KENTRON (center). Someone eccentric orbits off-center — individuality that deviates from the norm.
words.tip: Double C like going off-center twice: eC-Centric orbits away from normal.
words.example: The eccentric professor wore mismatched socks and collected vintage typewriters.

words.commentary: From Greek ekkentros, out of center. EK (out) + KENTRON (center). Eccentricity is being off-center — deviation from the expected orbit, whether planetary or personal.
words.tip: Double C, double T: ECC-entric people need double letters to be off-center
words.example: The professor's eccentricity was evident in his habit of lecturing while wearing mismatched socks and a bowler hat.

words.commentary: From Greek eklektikos, selective. EK (out) + LEGEIN (to choose). Someone eclectic chooses from many sources — taste that refuses to be confined to one style.
words.tip: Think 'elect' - you ELECT choices from many sources, so ec-LECT-ic
words.example: Her eclectic taste in music ranged from classical symphonies to underground hip-hop.

words.commentary: From Greek oikonomikos, of household management. OIKOS (house) + NOMOS (law). Being economical follows the laws of the household — managing resources as carefully as a home budget.
words.tip: Remember: ECO-NOM-IC-AL - house laws need to be practical, not magical (-ical not -icle)
words.example: The hybrid car is more economical than the gas-guzzling SUV for daily commuting.

words.commentary: From Greek oikos (house) + systema (organized whole). An ecosystem is a household system — nature organizing itself into communities of mutual dependence.
words.tip: ECO + SYSTEM.
words.example: The ecosystem is delicate.

words.commentary: From Greek ekzema, something thrown out. EK (out) + ZEIN (to boil). Skin that looks like it is boiling. The 'cz' combination is almost unique to this word in English.
words.tip: Remember: EC-ZE-MA - the 'cz' makes a 'z' sound, like skin boiling out
words.example: The dermatologist prescribed a topical cream to treat her persistent eczema flare-ups.

words.commentary: From Japanese eda (stem) + mame (bean). Stem beans — young soybeans served in the pod. Four syllables: e-da-MA-me. Not 'edamami' or 'endamame'.
words.tip: Remember: EDA (stem) + MAME (bean) - two Japanese words joined together.
words.example: The restaurant served warm edamame as a complimentary appetizer before our sushi order.

words.commentary: From Latin aedificium, a building. AEDIS (dwelling) + FACERE (to make). An edifice is something made to dwell in — a structure imposing enough to earn its own name.
words.tip: Remember: eDIfice - dwelling (aedis) made (facere) with D-I in middle
words.example: The gleaming corporate edifice dominated the downtown skyline with its imposing glass facade.

words.tip: EDUC + ATION. Think: educate.
words.example: Education is the key to opportunity.

words.commentary: From Latin effervescere, to boil up. EX (out) + FERVESCERE (to begin to boil). Something effervescent boils over with bubbles — energy that fizzes and sparkles.
words.tip: Remember: double F, double S - like bubbles fizzing in pairs
words.example: Her effervescent personality lit up the entire room with infectious enthusiasm.

words.commentary: From Latin efficacia, effectiveness. EX (out) + FACERE (to do). Efficacy is doing that produces results — effectiveness measured by outcomes.
words.tip: Think 'effective' with -acy ending: effi-CAC-y keeps the C from efficacious
words.example: The new medication's efficacy was proven through rigorous clinical trials.

words.commentary: From Latin efficientia, an accomplishing. EX (out) + FACERE (to make). Efficiency is making the most out of what you have — maximum result from minimum input.
words.tip: Two F's, ends in ENCY. EFFICI + ENCY.
words.example: We need to improve energy efficiency.

words.commentary: From Latin effrons, barefaced. EX (out) + FRONS (forehead). Effrontery is bare-faced nerve — audacity so bold it shows on the forehead.
words.tip: Remember the double F: ef-FRONT-ery, like showing your forehead boldly in front
words.example: She had the effrontery to ask for a raise after showing up late every day this week.

words.commentary: From French egalitaire, from egal (equal). An egalitarian believes in equality — the conviction that no person is born above another.
words.tip: Remember 'equal' becomes 'egal' in French - drop the 'qu' and add 'itarian'
words.example: The egalitarian society ensured that all citizens had equal access to education and healthcare.

words.commentary: Two H's and a TH at the end. The word is just EIGHT + H, but the consecutive consonants make it feel more complex than it is.
words.tip: EIGHT + H. Just add H to eight.
words.example: She finished in eighth place.

words.commentary: From Latin elaborare, to work out. E (out) + LABORARE (to work). Something elaborate has been worked out thoroughly — detail upon detail, labor made visible.
words.tip: ELABOR + ATE. One L.
words.example: The decorations were elaborate.

words.commentary: From Greek elektron, amber. Rubbing amber produces static charge — the ancient Greeks discovered electricity through a golden fossilized resin, naming a force after a gem.
words.tip: ELECTRIC + ITY.
words.example: The electricity went out during the storm.

words.commentary: From Greek elektron (amber, source of electricity) + kardia (heart) + gramma (writing). An electrical writing of the heart — the ECG or EKG. One of the longest common medical terms.
words.tip: Remember: electro-cardio-gram — electric heart writing, each part flows naturally together
words.example: The doctor ordered an electrocardiogram to check for any irregularities in her heart rhythm.

words.commentary: From Latin elicere, to draw out. E (out) + LACERE (to entice). To elicit is to draw out what was hidden — questions designed to bring answers to the surface.
words.tip: E-LIC-IT draws out; IL-LIC-IT is illegal - different words entirely
words.example: The detective's careful questioning was designed to elicit a confession from the suspect.

words.commentary: From Latin eligere, to choose out. Eligibility is the state of being choosable — meeting the criteria for selection.
words.tip: ELIGIBIL + ITY. Think: eligible.
words.example: Check your eligibility before applying.

words.commentary: IBLE not ABLE. From Latin eligere, to choose out. Someone eligible has been chosen out as suitable — the IBLE preserves the Latin verb class.
words.tip: Ends in IBLE, not ABLE. ELIG + IBLE.
words.example: You must be 18 to be eligible to vote.

words.commentary: From Latin eliminare, to turn out of doors. EX (out) + LIMEN (threshold). To eliminate is to push beyond the threshold — removal by crossing the boundary from inside to outside.
words.tip: ELIMIN + ATE. One L.
words.example: We need to eliminate waste.

words.commentary: From Greek elleipsis, a falling short. Two L's. An ellipse is a circle that falls short of being round — it's a geometric compromise.
