words.commentary: From German Grau (gray) + Burger (citizen). GRAU-BUR-GER. An Alsatian wine grape also known as Pinot Gris. The 'au' makes an 'ow' sound in German.
words.tip: Think German 'grau' (gray) + 'burger' (citizen) - gray citizen wine grape
words.example: The sommelier recommended a crisp Grauburger from the Alsace region to pair with the seafood.

words.commentary: Both spellings are Old English. Americans drifted to A, British to E — no clear etymology winner.
words.tip: US prefers gray. UK, CA, AU, NZ prefer grey.
words.example: The sky turned gray before the storm.

words.commentary: From Latin gregarius, of the flock. GREX (flock). Someone gregarious belongs in the flock — sociability that seeks company as naturally as breathing.
words.tip: Think GREG-ARI-OUS: Greg joins every group, like sheep in a flock (grex).
words.example: The gregarious golden retriever bounded up to every person and dog at the park.

words.commentary: From Italian, a condiment of lemon zest, garlic, and parsley. Not 'gremolada' — the t is correct in Italian. Sometimes spelled 'gremolada' in other languages.
words.tip: Remember the Italian 't' sound: gremola-TA, not gremola-DA like Spanish.
words.example: The chef finished the osso buco with a bright sprinkle of gremolata.

words.commentary: Grey with an E is British, gray with an A is American. A useful mnemonic: E for England, A for America. The word itself comes from Old English graeg.
words.tip: US: grAy (A for America). UK/AU/NZ: grEy (E for England).
words.example: The sky was a dull shade of grey.

words.commentary: From Old French grevance, hardship. GREVER (to burden). A grievance is a burden carried — complaint born from suffering that demands to be heard.
words.tip: I before E. GRIEV + ANCE.
words.example: She filed a formal grievance with HR.

words.commentary: From Old French grimuche, an ugly face. A grimace is the face distorted by pain or disgust — expression that abandons composure.
words.tip: Remember 'grim ace' - a grim face with one 'm' and 'ace' ending
words.example: She couldn't help but grimace when she tasted the bitter medicine.

words.commentary: From Italian grottesca, of a grotto. Grotesque art was originally found in underground caves — the strange and distorted emerging from hidden places.
words.tip: Remember the grotto cave origin: grot-ESQUE ends in -esque like picturesque
words.example: The gargoyles on the cathedral displayed grotesque faces meant to ward off evil spirits.

words.commentary: From Gruyères, a town in Switzerland. A nutty, melting cheese. The accent on the second e is technically correct but often omitted in English. Not 'gruyere' is acceptable, but 'gruyer' is not.
words.tip: Think of Gruyères, the Swiss town - it ends in '-ère' like 'there'
words.example: The fondue recipe called for aged gruyère cheese and a splash of white wine.

words.commentary: From Nahuatl ahuacamolli — AHUACATL (avocado) + MOLLI (sauce). Avocado sauce. The gu makes a 'gw' sound. Often misspelled 'guacomole' or 'guacamoli'.
words.tip: Remember: avocado + molé sauce = guacaMOLE (ends like the Mexican sauce)
words.example: She served fresh tortilla chips with homemade guacamole at the party.

words.commentary: From Old French garantie, protection. Of Germanic origin related to warrant. A guarantee is a promise backed by protection — assurance with teeth.
words.tip: GUAR + ANTEE. U after G.
words.example: We guarantee satisfaction.

words.commentary: From Latin gubernator, a governor. GUBERNARE (to steer). The word sounds nothing like governor because English took the adjective directly from Latin, not through French.
words.tip: Remember GUBERN- from Latin 'to steer', not 'govern'
words.example: The gubernatorial race attracted five candidates for governor.

words.commentary: From Spanish guerrilla, little war — the diminutive of guerra. Entered English during the Peninsular War against Napoleon.
words.tip: From Spanish guerra, war. Double R, double L.
words.example: The guerrilla fighters retreated into the mountains.

words.tip: GUID + ANCE. Ends in ANCE.
words.example: She sought guidance from her mentor.

words.commentary: From Arthur Guinness, founder of the Dublin brewery in 1759. Two n's, two s's — GUINN-ESS. One of the most misspelled brand names in the world. People write 'Guiness' (one n) or 'Guinnes' (one s). Remember: the Irish give generously — double n, double s.
words.tip: Double the Irish generosity: GuiNNess has two N's and two S's
words.example: He ordered a pint of Guinness at the Irish pub downtown.

words.commentary: From GULL, to deceive. Someone gullible is easily deceived — trust so complete it leaves no room for suspicion.
words.tip: Remember: a GULL is a bird easily tricked, so gullIBLE keeps the double-L
words.example: She was so gullible that she believed the email promising free money from a Nigerian prince.

words.commentary: Origin uncertain — possibly named after J.T. Gurney, a cab designer. A wheeled hospital stretcher. Short word but the '-ney' ending is sometimes spelled '-ny'.
words.tip: Remember 'gurney' keeps the 'e' before 'y' like 'journey' does
words.example: The paramedics quickly transferred the patient from the ambulance to the hospital gurney.

words.commentary: From Greek gymnasion, a place to exercise naked. GYMNOS (naked). Greek athletes trained without clothing — the gymnasium was a place of unadorned physical effort.
words.tip: GYMNAS + IUM. Think: gymnast.
words.example: The school has a new gymnasium.

words.commentary: From Japanese gyoza, adapted from Chinese jiaozi (dumplings). No silent letters — what you see is what you say. GY makes a soft g sound.
words.tip: Remember the Japanese origin: GY-O-ZA, like 'guy-oh-zah' with all letters pronounced.
words.example: We ordered a plate of crispy gyoza with soy dipping sauce at the Japanese restaurant.

words.commentary: From Havana, Cuba. HAB plus AN plus ERO.
words.tip: From Havana, Cuba.
words.example: The habanero added intense heat to the dish.

words.commentary: From Latin habitualis, of habit. HABERE (to have). Something habitual is had repeatedly — behavior that owns you as much as you own it.
words.tip: Remember HABIT + UAL: your habits are something you habitually have.
words.example: Her habitual morning coffee routine helped her start each day with focus.

words.commentary: From Scots, possibly from Old Norse hoggva (to chop). A Scottish dish of sheep organs in a stomach casing. Double g, ending in is. Not 'hagis'.
words.tip: Remember: haggis has double G like 'chop' in Old Norse hoggva
words.example: The chef served traditional haggis with neeps and tatties at the Burns Night dinner.

words.commentary: From Greek halkyon, the kingfisher bird. Legend said kingfishers calmed the seas during nesting. Halcyon days are calm days — peace borrowed from a bird's myth.
words.tip: Remember the Y from Greek halkYon - the mythical kingfisher that calmed seas.
words.example: She looked back on those halcyon days of childhood with deep nostalgia.

words.commentary: From Latin alucinari, to wander in mind. A hallucination is the mind wandering away from reality — perception untethered from what actually exists.
words.tip: Remember: double L, then U-C-I, like 'lucid' but you're not lucid!
words.example: The fever caused her to experience a vivid hallucination of dancing butterflies in the hospital room.

words.commentary: A compound of HAND + KERCHIEF, where kerchief itself means head-covering (from French couvre-chef). The D and K meeting in the middle creates the pronunciation challenge.
words.tip: HAND + KERCHIEF. A hand cloth (kerchief).
words.example: He offered her his handkerchief.

words.tip: HAPPY drops the Y, adds INESS. HAPPI + NESS.
words.example: Happiness is a state of mind.

words.commentary: One R, two S's. The word may come from Old French harer, to set a dog on someone. The single R is the key — harassment has one R as well.
words.tip: One R, two S's. Think: one Rude person causeS Stress.
words.example: It is illegal to harass someone at work.

words.commentary: From French harasser, to tire out. Harassment exhausts its target through repetition — wearing down resistance through persistent unwanted attention.
words.tip: One R, two S's. HARASS + MENT.
words.example: Workplace harassment is unacceptable.

words.commentary: From Old French herbergeor, one who provides lodging. A harbinger goes ahead to prepare — the advance signal that something greater follows.
words.tip: Remember: harb-INGER (like ginger) - one who brings tidings ahead.
words.example: The first robin was a harbinger of spring's arrival.

words.commentary: From Old English herebeorg, army shelter. The U crept in via French influence.
words.tip: US drops the U. UK, CA, AU, NZ keep it.
words.example: The boats sheltered in the harbor overnight.

words.commentary: From Old English herebeorg, army shelter. The U crept in via French influence.
words.tip: US drops the U. UK, CA, AU, NZ keep it.
words.example: The boats sheltered in the harbor overnight.

words.commentary: From Arabic al-zahr, the die. Hazard originally meant a dice game — something hazardous carries the unpredictability of a thrown die.
words.tip: Think of dice: HAZ-ARD-OUS keeps the Arabic 'zahr' (die) at its heart.
words.example: The cleanup crew wore protective gear when handling hazardous chemicals.

words.commentary: From German Hefe (yeast) + Weizen (wheat). HEFE-WEIZEN. An unfiltered wheat beer. The 'ei' makes an 'eye' sound, the 'ei' in weizen follows the same German rule. Not 'hefeweisen' or 'heffeweizen'.
words.tip: Remember: German 'ei' sounds like 'eye' - HEFE-WEIZEN (yeast-wheat)
words.example: The brewery's hefeweizen had a cloudy appearance and refreshing citrus notes.

words.commentary: Ends in EIGHT like weight, but starts with H-E-I, not H-I-E. The word comes from Old English, and the spelling has been irregular for centuries.
words.tip: No TH at the end. HEIGHT, not heighth. EI not IE.
words.example: What is the height of that building?

words.commentary: Named after Gerard Adriaan Heineken, who founded the brewery in 1864. HEI-NE-KEN. Not 'hieneken' or 'heiniken'. The 'ei' appears once, then 'e' twice.
words.tip: Remember the Dutch founder: HEI-NE-KEN with 'ei' once, then two e's
words.example: The bar had Heineken on tap along with several local craft beers.

words.commentary: From Old French haineus, hateful. HAINE (hatred). Something heinous is hateful in the extreme — evil so concentrated it provokes visceral disgust.
words.tip: Remember: HAINE (hatred) + -ous. The 'ei' makes the 'ay' sound in 'heinous'.
words.example: The war crimes tribunal prosecuted him for his heinous acts against civilians.

words.tip: HELI + COPTER. Think: helix + wing.
words.example: The helicopter landed on the roof.

words.commentary: From Greek haima (blood) + -oma (tumor, swelling). A solid swelling of clotted blood. British spelling: haematoma. The single-m after he- is counterintuitive.
words.tip: Remember: HEM-A-TOMA (single 'm' after hema, like in hematology)
words.example: The MRI revealed a large hematoma in the patient's brain tissue.

words.commentary: From Greek hemisphairion, half a sphere. HEMI (half) + SPHAIRA (sphere). A hemisphere is half a sphere — the earth divided into matching halves by an imaginary line.
words.tip: Remember HEMI (half) + SPHERE - just add an 'i' between them
words.example: The Northern Hemisphere experiences winter while the Southern Hemisphere enjoys summer.

words.commentary: From Greek haimorrhagia, bleeding. HAIMA (blood) + RHEGNYNAI (to burst). The silent h at the start and the -rrhage ending trip up most spellers. British spelling: haemorrhage.
words.tip: Two R's in the middle. HEMO + RRHAGE.
words.example: The patient suffered a hemorrhage during surgery.

words.commentary: From Greek haimorrhagikos. The adjective form of hemorrhage. Same double-r and silent-h patterns, with an added -ic suffix.
words.tip: Remember: double-r like 'hemorrhage' plus Greek -ic ending
words.example: The patient suffered a hemorrhagic stroke caused by a ruptured blood vessel.

words.commentary: From Greek haimorrhois, bleeding vein. HAIMA (blood) + RHEIN (to flow). Same root as hemorrhage. The silent h and double-r pattern repeat. British spelling: haemorrhoid.
words.tip: Remember Greek roots: HAIMA (blood) + RHEIN (flow) = hemorrhoid with double-r
words.example: The doctor recommended a high-fiber diet to prevent hemorrhoid formation.

words.commentary: From Latin herba (plant) + vorare (to devour). An herbivore devours plants — the word combines garden and appetite.
words.tip: Remember: HERB + i + VORE (like carnivore) - plants need an 'i' to be devoured
words.example: The giant panda is a herbivore that feeds almost exclusively on bamboo shoots and leaves.

words.commentary: From Latin hereditarius, of inheritance. HERES (heir). Something hereditary passes through inheritance — traits, titles, or conditions flowing from parent to child.
words.tip: Remember HEIR + EDIT + ARY - an heir edits what they inherit
words.example: The hereditary monarchy passed the crown from generation to generation.

words.commentary: From Greek hairesis, a taking or choosing. HAIREIN (to take). Heresy is choosing differently — belief that diverges from the accepted doctrine.
words.tip: Remember: heresy ends in -esy, not -say like hearsay
words.example: The medieval church considered his scientific theories to be dangerous heresy.

words.commentary: From Old French heritage, inheritance. HERES (heir). Heritage is what an heir receives — the cultural and material legacy passed down through generations.
words.tip: HERIT + AGE. Think: inherit.
words.example: We must protect our cultural heritage.

words.commentary: From Greek heros, a demigod or champion. Heroism is the quality of the hero — extraordinary courage displayed when ordinary response would be understandable.
words.tip: Remember: HERO + ISM (not -izm). The Greek hero keeps the 'o'.
words.example: The firefighter's heroism saved three families from the burning building.

words.commentary: From Latin haesitare, to stick fast. HAERERE (to stick). Hesitation is getting stuck — the mind catching on uncertainty like cloth on a thorn.
words.tip: HESIT + ATE. Think: hesitation.
words.example: Don't hesitate to ask for help.

words.commentary: From Latin haesitare, to stick fast. HAERERE (to stick). Hesitation is being stuck — the mind adhering to uncertainty, unable to move in either direction.
words.tip: Remember: hesit-ATION (not hesit-IATION) - stick to one 'i' like Latin haesitare
words.example: After a moment's hesitation, she knocked on the door.

words.commentary: From Greek heterogenes, of different kinds. HETEROS (other) + GENOS (kind). The opposite of homogeneous — a mix of different elements.
words.tip: Remember: HETERO-GEN-E-OUS. Think 'generate' - it has the E after GEN.
words.example: The city's heterogeneous population includes people from dozens of different countries.

words.commentary: From Greek heuriskein, to find or discover. A heuristic is a discovery method — finding solutions through exploration rather than formula.
words.tip: Remember 'heur-IS-TIC' - the 'IS' helps you discover the right spelling
words.example: The detective used a heuristic approach, following hunches rather than strict procedures.

words.commentary: IE not EI in the first syllable. The word means sacred rule, from Greek hieros (sacred) + archein (to rule). The sacred part keeps its IE.
words.tip: HIER + ARCHY. Think: sacred rule.
words.example: The company has a strict hierarchy.

words.commentary: From railroad signals — a raised ball meant full speed ahead, like a quick drink. HIGH-BALL. Two common words. Not 'hiball' or 'highbawl'. A tall glass with spirit and mixer.
words.tip: Think railroad signal: HIGH ball means full speed, like a quick drink
words.example: The bartender served him a whiskey highball with plenty of ice and soda water.

words.commentary: From Greek historia, inquiry or knowledge. A historian is one who inquires — the original meaning was not about the past specifically, but about systematic investigation.
words.tip: HISTORI + AN. Think: history.
words.example: The historian studied ancient Rome.

words.commentary: From French hollandaise, Dutch-style. A sauce of egg yolks, butter, and lemon. The aise ending is French for 'in the style of'. Not 'hollandaze' or 'holindaise'.
words.tip: Remember: Holland + -aise (French for 'style of') = hollandaise sauce
words.example: The eggs Benedict were topped with a rich, creamy hollandaise sauce.

words.commentary: From Greek homogenes, of the same kind. HOMOS (same) + GENOS (kind). Something homogeneous is all the same kind — uniformity without variation.
words.tip: Remember: homoGENEous has GENE in it - same genes, same kind
words.example: The cake batter should be stirred until it becomes completely homogeneous with no lumps.

words.commentary: From Latin honor — the U was added in French and kept by British English. Webster removed it.
words.tip: US drops the U. UK, CA, AU, NZ keep it.
words.example: It was an honor to receive the award.

words.commentary: Latin honor became Old French honour, and English kept both spellings alive for centuries. The split only became formal when Webster's dictionary standardized the shorter American form.
words.tip: US: honor. UK/AU/NZ: honour. Both are correct in their regions.
words.example: It is an honour to meet you.

words.commentary: From Greek horizein, to bound or limit. HOROS (boundary). The horizon is the boundary of sight — the line where earth and sky agree to meet.
words.tip: Remember the Greek HOROS (boundary) - horizon ends with -on like the boundary line.
words.example: The sun slowly disappeared behind the distant horizon.

words.commentary: From Latin horizon, the boundary line. HOROS (boundary). The horizontal line marks the boundary between earth and sky.
words.tip: HORIZON + TAL. Think: horizon.
words.example: Draw a horizontal line across the page.

words.commentary: One of the most feared spellings in English, borrowed wholesale from French. It literally means outside the work — food served outside the main meal. The apostrophe and silent letters make it a gauntlet of difficulty.
words.tip: It's French. HORS (outside) + D'OEUVRE (the work). Appetizers served outside the main meal.
words.example: They served hors d'oeuvres before dinner.

words.commentary: From French hors d'oeuvre, outside the work — food served outside the main meal. The plural adds an s that is silent. Almost every letter behaves unexpectedly for English speakers. Many give up and write 'ordurves'.
words.tip: Think 'horse Dover' - silent letters galore in this fancy French import
words.example: The wedding reception featured an elegant spread of hors d'oeuvres before dinner.

words.commentary: From Latin hospitalis, of a guest. HOSPES (host/guest). Hospitable and hospital share a root — both involve caring for those who arrive in need.
words.tip: Think 'hospital' + able - both care for guests in need
words.example: The hospitable family welcomed travelers with warm meals and comfortable beds.

words.tip: HOSPIT + AL. Ends in AL.
words.example: She was admitted to the hospital.

words.commentary: From Latin hospitalitas, friendliness to guests. HOSPES (guest or host). Hospitality treats strangers as guests — the same root gives us hospital, a place where the suffering are hosted.
words.tip: Remember: one T in hospital becomes hospitality - host guests with one T
words.example: The hotel's warm hospitality made all the guests feel like they were visiting old friends.

words.commentary: From Latin hostilitas, enmity. HOSTIS (enemy). Hostility is the stance toward an enemy — aggression that treats the other as a threat to be opposed.
words.tip: Remember HOSTILE + ITY - from Latin HOSTIS meaning enemy
words.example: The diplomat worked to reduce hostility between the two nations.

words.commentary: From Latin humanitas, human nature. HUMANUS (human). A humanitarian acts from human nature at its best — compassion extended universally, without condition.
words.tip: Remember: HUMAN + -itarian (like vegetarian). Keep the 'a' after 'human'.
words.example: The humanitarian organization delivered food and medical supplies to the disaster-stricken region.

words.commentary: From Latin humanitas, human nature. The word carries a double meaning: the quality of being human, and compassion toward other humans.
words.tip: HUMAN + ITY.
words.example: We must show humanity to others.

words.commentary: From Latin humiliare, to make low. HUMILIS (low, from humus, earth). To humiliate is to bring someone down to the ground �� to earth them.
words.tip: HUMILI + ATE. One L.
words.example: He didn't want to humiliate her.

words.commentary: From Arabic hummus, chickpeas. Double m, ending in us. Also spelled 'houmous' (British) or 'humus' (though that also means decomposed soil). The double m distinguishes the food from dirt.
words.tip: Double M for the Middle Eastern dip, unlike humus which is soil.
words.example: She spread creamy hummus on warm pita bread with olive oil drizzled on top.

words.commentary: From Latin humor, fluid — one of the four bodily humours thought to govern temperament.
words.tip: US drops the U. UK, CA, AU, NZ keep it.
words.example: She had a great sense of humor.

words.commentary: The O drops from humour/humor when adding OUS. It's one of the few cases where the root vowel changes with the suffix.
words.tip: US: humorous. UK/AU/NZ: humourous (with U).
words.example: The speech was both informative and humorous.

words.commentary: From Latin humor, fluid — one of the four bodily humours thought to govern temperament.
words.tip: US drops the U. UK, CA, AU, NZ keep it.
words.example: She had a great sense of humor.

words.commentary: From Taino hurakan, god of the storm. The word traveled from Caribbean indigenous language through Spanish to English, carrying the name of a deity into meteorology.
words.tip: Two R's. HURR + ICANE.
words.example: The hurricane caused severe damage.

words.commentary: From Greek hydraulikos, relating to a water pipe. HYDOR (water) + AULOS (pipe). Hydraulic systems use water (or fluid) to transmit force.
words.tip: HYDR + AULIC. Think: water power.
words.example: The hydraulic press was powerful.

words.commentary: The IE and the silent final E make this word look stranger than it sounds. Named after Hygieia, the Greek goddess of health, the spelling preserves the Greek roots.
words.tip: HYG + IENE. Think: Hygieia (Greek goddess of health).
words.example: Good hygiene prevents illness.

words.commentary: From Greek hyper, over, plus link. HYPER plus LINK.
words.tip: From Greek hyper, over, plus link.
words.example: Click the hyperlink to visit the page.

words.commentary: From Greek hyper (over) + Latin tensio (stretching). High blood pressure — the blood vessels are over-stretched. The 'hyper' prefix is sometimes confused with 'hypo' (under).
words.tip: Remember: HYPER-TEN-SION - think 'tension' with 'hyper' prefix meaning over-stretched blood vessels.
words.example: The doctor prescribed medication to help control her hypertension after multiple high blood pressure readings.

words.commentary: From Greek hypo plus chondros. Ancient seat of melancholy.
words.tip: From Greek hypo plus chondros.
words.example: His hypochondria led him to visit the doctor weekly.

words.commentary: From Greek hypo (under) + glykys (sweet) + haima (blood). Low blood sugar. The 'glyc' middle section comes from the Greek word for sweet — same root as glycerin.
words.tip: Remember: hypo-GLYCemia has Y in GLYCerin, both mean sweet
words.example: The diabetic patient experienced dizziness and confusion due to severe hypoglycemia.

words.commentary: From Greek hypothesis, a foundation. HYPO (under) + THESIS (placing). A hypothesis is what you place underneath your investigation — the assumed foundation you then test.
words.tip: HYPO + THESIS. Think: under + theory.
words.example: The hypothesis was tested in the lab.

words.commentary: From Greek hypothesis, a foundation. HYPO (under) + THESIS (placing). Something hypothetical is placed underneath as a foundation for reasoning — assumption before evidence.
words.tip: Remember HYPO-THETICAL: hypo (under) + thetical (placing) - foundation under reasoning.
words.example: The scientist presented a hypothetical scenario to test the theory.

words.commentary: A coined pharmaceutical name from isobutylphenylpropionic acid. The chemical roots were compressed into a pronounceable trademark. No silent letters — just an unusual combination.
words.tip: Remember: ibu-PRO-fen (like 'professional' with 'fen' at the end)
words.example: She took two ibuprofen tablets to reduce the inflammation in her knee.

words.commentary: From Greek eikonoklastes, image-breaker. EIKON (image) + KLAN (to break). An iconoclast breaks images — destroying the sacred symbols that others worship.
words.tip: Remember: ICON + CLAST (breaker) - the image-breaker has one 'a' in icon
words.example: The young artist was an iconoclast who challenged traditional painting techniques.

words.commentary: From Latin identicus, the same. IDEM (the same). Identical things are so alike they might as well be the same thing — the word allows no difference.
words.tip: IDENTIC + AL. Think: identity.
words.example: The twins look identical.

words.commentary: ENTI in the middle. From Latin idem, meaning the same. To identify is to establish that something is the same as what's claimed.
words.tip: IDENT + IFY. Think: ID + ENTITY.
words.example: Can you identify the suspect?

words.commentary: From French ideologie, the study of ideas. IDEA + LOGOS (study). An ideology is a system of ideas — a framework through which someone interprets the world.
words.tip: IDEO + LOGY. Study of ideas.
words.example: Political ideology shapes policy.

words.commentary: From Greek idiosynkrasia, a peculiar temperament. IDIOS (one's own) + SYNKRASIS (mixture). An idiosyncrasy is your own unique mixture — the quirk that belongs only to you.
words.tip: Remember: IDIO-SYN-CRASY. Your own (idio) mixture (syn-crasy) with an 's' not 'c'.
words.example: His idiosyncrasy of wearing mismatched socks became his trademark at the office.

words.commentary: From Latin ignominia, loss of good name. IN (not) + NOMEN (name). Something ignominious erases your good name — shame that replaces reputation.
words.tip: Remember: igNOMINious erases your good NAME (Latin nomen = name)
words.example: The CEO's ignominious departure after the scandal left the company's reputation in tatters.

words.commentary: ANCE not ENCE. Related to ignore, the word keeps the O from its root but changes the ending pattern. Think: you can't ignore ignorance.
words.tip: IGNOR + ANCE. Ends in ANCE.
words.example: Ignorance is not an excuse.

words.commentary: IL + LEGITIMATE. The double L comes from the prefix IN becoming IL before L (like illegal, illogical). The word means not legitimate — outside the law or accepted norms.
words.tip: Two L's. IL + LEGITIMATE.
words.example: The court ruled the contract illegitimate.

words.commentary: From Latin illuminare, to light up. IN (in) + LUMEN (light). To illuminate is to bring light into darkness — whether physical or intellectual.
words.tip: Remember: double L, single M - like shedding light with two Ls.
words.example: The museum's spotlights illuminate the ancient artifacts in stunning detail.

words.commentary: From Latin illustrare, to make bright. IN (in) + LUSTRARE (to purify by light). An illustration brightens understanding — clarity through imagery.
words.tip: Two L's. ILL + USTR + ATE.
words.example: Let me illustrate with an example.

words.commentary: From Latin illustrare, to light up. IN (in) + LUSTRARE (to brighten). An illustration lights up meaning — visual brightness that makes the abstract visible.
words.tip: Remember: it ILLUMINATES meaning - double L, then STRation like demonstration
words.example: The biology textbook included a detailed illustration of the human circulatory system.

words.commentary: From Latin illustris, bright or distinguished. An illustrious career shines — the word connects achievement with light.
words.tip: Two L's. ILLUSTRI + OUS.
words.example: She had an illustrious career.

words.commentary: From Latin imaginarius, existing in imagination. IMAGO (image). Something imaginary exists only as an image in the mind — real in thought, absent in matter.
words.tip: IMAGIN + ARY. Ends in ARY.
words.example: The child had an imaginary friend.

words.tip: IMAGIN + ATION. Think: imagine.
words.example: Use your imagination to solve the puzzle.

words.commentary: From Latin imbuere, to wet or saturate. To imbue is to saturate thoroughly — filling something with a quality until it permeates every part.
words.tip: Remember: im-BUE (like 'blue' but with 'bue') - saturate with Latin roots
words.example: The teacher tried to imbue her students with a love of literature through passionate discussions.

words.commentary: From Latin imitari, to copy. An imitation reproduces what already exists — the copy acknowledging the original by trying to become it.
words.tip: Remember: one M, two T's - you imitate with one mouth, two teeth showing
words.example: The artist's imitation of Monet's style was so convincing that experts had to examine it closely.

words.commentary: From Latin immaculatus, without stain. IN (not) + MACULA (spot). Something immaculate has no spots — it's perfectly clean, inside and out.
words.tip: Two M's. IMMACUL + ATE. Think: without stain.
words.example: The house was immaculate.

words.commentary: IATE in the middle is key. The word means without a medium or intervening space — IM + MEDIATE + LY.
words.tip: IMMEDIATE + LY. Don't drop the E.
words.example: Please respond immediately.

words.commentary: Immigrate means to enter and settle in a new country. Emigrate means to leave.
words.tip: Immigrate means to enter and settle in a new country.
words.example: Her family immigrated to Canada in the 1960s.

words.commentary: From Latin immigrare, to move into. IN (into) + MIGRARE (to move). Immigration is moving into a new country — arrival as the defining act, contrasting with emigration's departure.
words.tip: Two M's. IMMIGR + ATION.
words.example: Immigration policy is a major debate topic.

words.commentary: Imminent means about to happen. Eminent means distinguished or prominent.
words.tip: Imminent means about to happen.
words.example: A storm was imminent according to the forecast.

words.commentary: From Latin immortalis, not subject to death. IN (not) + MORTALIS (subject to death). An immortal being escapes death — the word negates our most fundamental limitation.
words.tip: Two M's. IM + MORTAL.
words.example: The legend became immortal.

words.commentary: From Latin immunitas, exemption. IN (not) + MUNUS (service or duty). Immunity is freedom from duty — whether exemption from disease, prosecution, or obligation.
words.tip: Remember: IM-MUN-ITY has double M like 'immune' plus -ity ending
words.example: The vaccine provided long-lasting immunity against the virus.

words.commentary: From Latin immutabilis, unchangeable. IN (not) + MUTARE (to change). Something immutable cannot be changed — permanence that resists all forces of alteration.
words.tip: Remember: IM-MUT-ABLE - double M, MUT like 'mutate', ends in -ABLE not -IBLE
words.example: The laws of physics are considered immutable truths that govern our universe.

words.commentary: From IN (not) + PARTIAL. An impartial person takes no part — judgment delivered without favoritism, fairness achieved through deliberate detachment.
words.tip: Remember: imPARTial means taking no PART in the dispute
words.example: The judge remained impartial throughout the controversial trial.

words.commentary: From French impasse, a dead end. IM (not) + PASSER (to pass). An impasse is a place you cannot pass — negotiation or path that has reached its end.
words.tip: Remember: IM-PASSE with double S, like the French word for 'no passing'
words.example: The budget negotiations reached an impasse when neither side would compromise on spending cuts.

words.tip: IM + PATIENT. Think: not patient.
words.example: The crowd grew impatient.

words.commentary: From Latin impeccabilis, without sin. IN (not) + PECCARE (to sin). Something impeccable is without fault — perfection that admits no error.
words.tip: Remember: double C like 'accident' - sins require confession with two C's
words.example: Her impeccable timing and flawless presentation impressed the entire board of directors.

words.commentary: From Latin impedire, to shackle the feet. IN (in) + PES (foot). To impede is to shackle someone's feet — obstruction that slows but does not completely stop.
words.tip: Remember: impede has one 'e' at the end, like 'feet' being shackled.
words.example: The fallen tree branches will impede our progress down the hiking trail.

words.commentary: From Latin impedimentum, a hindrance. IN (in) + PES (foot). An impediment snares the feet — an obstacle that tangles your steps and slows your progress.
words.tip: Remember: your feet get tangled in an impedimENT - think 'impede' plus 'ment'
words.example: His severe stutter was no impediment to becoming a successful public speaker.

words.commentary: From Latin impervius, not passable. IN (not) + PER (through) + VIA (way). Something impervious allows no way through — resistance that blocks all penetration.
words.tip: Remember VIA (way) in the middle - nothing gets through the way
words.example: The raincoat's impervious fabric kept her completely dry during the storm.

words.commentary: From Latin impetuosus, violent. IMPETUS (attack). Someone impetuous attacks without thinking — action that outruns consideration.
words.tip: Remember IMPETUS + OUS: the attack (impetus) becomes impetUOus
words.example: His impetuous decision to quit his job without notice surprised everyone.

words.commentary: From Latin implacabilis, not to be appeased. IN (not) + PLACARE (to soothe). Someone implacable cannot be soothed — anger or resolve that no gesture can soften.
words.tip: Remember PLACARE (to soothe) - it's imPLACAble, not imPLACIble
words.example: Despite numerous apologies, she remained implacable in her refusal to forgive him.

words.commentary: From Latin implementum, a filling up. IN (in) + PLERE (to fill). To implement is to fill in the details — turning plans into reality by completing what was outlined.
words.tip: IMPLE + MENT. Think: implement the plan.
words.example: We need to implement new safety measures.

words.commentary: From Latin implicare, to fold in. IN (in) + PLICARE (to fold). An implication is meaning folded inside a statement — significance hidden within words, requiring unfolding to discover.
words.tip: IMPLIC + ATION. Think: imply.
words.example: The implication of the study was clear.

words.commentary: From Latin implicitus, folded in. IN (in) + PLICARE (to fold). Something implicit is folded inside — meaning contained but not displayed.
words.tip: Remember: implicit has one 'c' then 'it' - think 'folded in it'
words.example: There was an implicit understanding between them that no one would mention the incident.

words.commentary: Imply means to suggest without stating. Infer means to deduce from evidence.
words.tip: Imply means to suggest without stating.
words.example: She implied that she was unhappy with the decision.

words.commentary: From Latin imprimere, to press into. IN (in) + PREMERE (to press). An impression is pressed into memory — experience stamped on the mind like a seal in wax.
words.tip: Two S's. IMPRESS + ION.
words.example: She made a great first impression.

words.commentary: From Latin in promptu, in readiness. Something impromptu is done from readiness — unplanned action drawing on prepared ability.
words.tip: Remember 'prompt' inside - ready to act without delay or preparation
words.example: The pianist gave an impromptu performance after dinner, delighting the surprised guests.

words.tip: IMPROVE + MENT. Keep the E.
words.example: There has been a marked improvement.

words.commentary: From Latin improvisus, unforeseen. IN (not) + PROVISUS (foreseen). To improvise is to act without foresight — creating in the moment because preparation was not possible.
words.tip: Remember: improvise uses 's' not 'z' - think 'not provisioned ahead'
words.example: When the microphone failed, she had to improvise her presentation without any slides.

words.commentary: From Latin impudens, shameless. IN (not) + PUDENS (modest). Someone impudent lacks modesty — boldness that has discarded the restraint of shame.
words.tip: Remember: im-PU-dent has 'dent' at the end, not 'dant'
words.example: The impudent child interrupted the meeting to demand ice cream.

words.commentary: From IN (not) + ADEQUATE. Something inadequate is not equal to the task — falling short of what the situation demands.
words.tip: Remember: IN + ADEQUATE. Keep the E in adequate when adding the prefix.
words.example: The fire escape was inadequate for a building of that size.

words.commentary: From Latin inadvertens, not paying attention. IN (not) + ADVERTERE (to turn toward). Something inadvertent happens when attention turns away — error born from momentary distraction.
words.tip: Remember ADVERT-ent: your attention diverts away from adverting (turning toward).
words.example: The inadvertent deletion of the file caused hours of extra work.

words.commentary: From Latin inaugurare, to take omens from birds. IN (in) + AUGUR (bird-seer). Inaugurations originally required favorable bird signs — beginnings blessed by flight patterns.
words.tip: Think AUGUR (bird-seer): in-AUGUR-ate - the middle has AUGUR, not -ER-
words.example: The new president will inaugurate several policy changes during her first term.

words.commentary: From Latin inaugurare, to take omens from birds. IN (in) + AUGUR (a diviner). An inauguration was originally a reading of bird-flight omens — beginning something by consulting the heavens.
words.tip: Remember AUGUR (bird diviner) in the middle - inaug-UR-ation, not inaug-ER-ation
words.example: The presidential inauguration ceremony drew crowds from across the nation.

words.commentary: From Latin incandescere, to glow white. IN (intensive) + CANDERE (to shine). Something incandescent shines with internal heat — light born from being heated to brilliance.
words.tip: Remember CANDESCENT - like a CANDle that's incanDESCENT with heat
words.example: The incandescent bulb cast a warm, golden glow throughout the cozy reading nook.

words.commentary: From Latin incentivum, setting the tune. IN (in) + CANERE (to sing). An incentive sets the tune for action — motivation that starts the song of effort.
words.tip: Remember: incentive sets the tune - think 'in-CENT-ive' like cents motivate spending
words.example: The company offered a cash bonus as an incentive for employees to work overtime.

words.commentary: From Latin incessans, not ceasing. IN (not) + CESSARE (to cease). Something incessant never stops — continuation that exhausts everyone except itself.
words.tip: Remember: inCESSant contains CESS (cease) - it never ceases, so double S
words.example: The incessant barking from next door kept me awake all night.

words.commentary: From Latin incidere, to fall upon. IN (upon) + CADERE (to fall). Incidence is how often something falls upon a population — frequency measured by impact.
words.tip: Remember: things fall IN upon us, ending in -ENCE likeidence.
words.example: The incidence of flu cases increased dramatically during the winter months.

words.commentary: From Latin incidere, to fall upon. IN (upon) + CADERE (to fall). An incident falls upon you — an event that lands without invitation.
words.tip: Remember: an incident falls IN upon you - keep the 'e' like 'dent'
words.example: The security team filed a report about the incident in the parking garage.

words.commentary: ALLY ending, not LY alone. INCIDENT + AL + LY. Each suffix adds its own layer: incident becomes incidental becomes incidentally.
words.tip: INCIDENTAL + LY. Keep the AL before LY.
words.example: Incidentally, I found your keys on the table.

words.commentary: From Latin incisivus, cutting into. IN (into) + CAEDERE (to cut). Something incisive cuts into the heart of the matter — analysis sharp enough to reach the core.
words.tip: Think 'precise' - both end in -CISE, both mean sharp and exact.
words.example: Her incisive questions exposed the flaws in their business plan.

words.commentary: From Latin inclinare, to lean toward. IN (toward) + CLINARE (to lean). An inclination is a leaning — preference expressed as a tilt toward one option over another.
words.tip: Remember CLIN like 'recline' - you lean back to recline, lean toward an inclination.
words.example: Her inclination toward mathematics led her to pursue a career in engineering.

words.commentary: From Latin incongruus, not agreeing. IN (not) + CONGRUERE (to agree). Something incongruous doesn't agree with its surroundings — the element that doesn't fit.
words.tip: Remember CONGRU- from Latin 'to agree' - it's conGRUent with proper spelling
words.example: The neon pink flamingo looked incongruous in the formal Victorian garden.

words.commentary: From Latin incorporare, to form into a body. IN (into) + CORPUS (body). To incorporate is to bring into one body — separate parts united into a single whole.
words.tip: IN + CORPOR + ATE. Think: body (corpus).
words.example: We plan to incorporate feedback into the design.

words.commentary: From Latin incredibilis, not believable. IN (not) + CREDERE (to believe). Something incredible exceeds what belief can hold — wonder that overflows the vessel of expectation.
words.tip: IN + CRED + IBLE. Think: not credible.
words.example: The view was absolutely incredible.

words.commentary: From Latin incubare, to lie upon. IN (upon) + CUBARE (to lie). Incubation is lying upon eggs until they hatch — warmth and patience transforming potential into life.
words.tip: Remember: CUB like a baby bear lying in its den
words.example: The incubation period for chicken eggs is typically twenty-one days.

words.commentary: From Latin incumbere, to lie upon. IN (upon) + CUBARE (to lie). An incumbent lies upon the office — the current holder whose weight fills the position.
words.tip: Remember: an incumbent lies 'upon' the office - IN-CUM-BENT (like 'bent upon')
words.example: The incumbent mayor decided not to seek reelection after serving two terms.

words.commentary: From Latin independentia, not hanging from. IN (not) + DEPENDERE (to hang from). Independence means not hanging from anything — standing entirely on your own support.
words.tip: INDEPEND + ENCE. Ends in ENCE.
words.example: Independence Day is celebrated on July 4th.

words.commentary: ENT not ANT at the end. The word means not hanging from something else — IN + DEPEND + ENT.
words.tip: Ends in DENT, not DANT. A DENT in dependence.
words.example: She became financially independent at 25.

words.commentary: From Latin indicare, to point out. IN (toward) + DICARE (to proclaim). To indicate is to point and proclaim — direction given through gesture or evidence.
words.tip: Remember: IN-DI-CATE - like pointing your index finger to indicate direction
words.example: The dark clouds indicate that rain is likely this afternoon.

words.commentary: From Latin indicare, to point out. IN (toward) + DICARE (to proclaim). An indication points toward meaning — signs that proclaim what is coming or what is true.
words.tip: INDIC + ATION. Think: indicate.
words.example: There was no indication of foul play.

words.commentary: From Latin indicere, to proclaim. The C is silent.
words.tip: From Latin indicere, to proclaim.
words.example: The grand jury handed down an indictment.

words.commentary: From Latin indigena, a native. INDU (in) + GIGNERE (to beget). Something indigenous was begotten in its place — belonging by birth rather than arrival.
words.tip: Remember: in-DIG-enous - natives DIG deep roots in their homeland
words.example: The indigenous peoples of Australia have lived on the continent for over 60,000 years.

words.commentary: From Latin indignans, considering unworthy. IN (not) + DIGNUS (worthy). Someone indignant judges something unworthy — anger aroused by perceived injustice.
words.tip: Remember: IN-DIGNANT - someone finds something not DIGNIFIED, so keep the DIG
words.example: She was indignant when her proposal was dismissed without consideration.

words.commentary: From Latin indiscriminatus, not separated. IN (not) + DISCRIMINARE (to separate). Something indiscriminate makes no separations — action that treats all targets the same regardless of merit.
words.tip: Remember DISCRIMINATE inside - you can't discriminate if you're indiscriminate
words.example: The bombing campaign was criticized for its indiscriminate targeting of civilian areas.

words.commentary: From IN (not) + DISPENSABLE. Something indispensable cannot be dispensed with — so essential that its removal would collapse the system that depends on it.
words.tip: Remember: indisPENSable - you can't be dispensed from spelling PENS correctly
words.example: Clean water is indispensable for human survival and cannot be replaced by any substitute.

words.commentary: From Latin individuus, indivisible. IN (not) + DIVIDUUS (divisible). An individual cannot be divided further — the smallest unit of humanity.
words.tip: INDIVID + UAL. Think: not divided.
words.example: Every individual has unique talents.

words.commentary: From Latin indolens, without pain. IN (not) + DOLERE (to grieve). Someone indolent avoids the pain of effort — laziness disguised as comfort.
words.tip: Remember: IN-DOLENT rhymes with 'olen' like stolen - lazy people steal time from work.
words.example: The indolent student spent the entire afternoon lounging in bed instead of studying.

words.commentary: From Latin indomitabilis, untameable. IN (not) + DOMARE (to tame). Someone indomitable cannot be tamed — spirit that refuses to submit regardless of opposition.
words.tip: Remember: inDOMitable - think 'DOM'inate, but can't be dominated or tamed
words.example: Her indomitable spirit carried her through years of setbacks and challenges.

words.commentary: From Latin industria, diligence. The word originally meant hard work — industry became the system that organized labor into production.
words.tip: INDUSTRI + AL. Think: industry.
words.example: The industrial revolution changed the world.

words.commentary: From Latin industria, diligence. INDU (within) + STRUERE (to build). An industrious person builds from within — internal drive manifested as sustained productive effort.
words.tip: Remember: inDUSTRious builds from within - DUSTR like construction dust from building.
words.example: The industrious student completed all her assignments ahead of schedule.

words.commentary: From Latin ineptus, unsuitable. IN (not) + APTUS (fit). Someone inept is not fit for the task — inadequacy that shows in every attempt.
words.tip: Remember: IN + APT = inept (not fit for the task)
words.example: The new manager's inept handling of the crisis cost the company several major clients.

words.commentary: From IN (not) + EQUALITY. Inequality is the measurable distance between equal and actual — the gap between what should be balanced and what is not.
words.tip: Remember: IN + EQUALITY = INEQUALITY (keep all letters from equality)
words.example: The study revealed significant income inequality between urban and rural communities.

words.commentary: From Latin iners, unskilled or idle. IN (not) + ARS (skill). Something inert lacks the skill to act — passivity that resists all forces of change.
words.tip: Remember: IN-ERT lacks ARS (skill) - no extra letters needed
words.example: The noble gas argon is chemically inert under normal conditions.

words.commentary: From Latin inevitabilis, unavoidable. IN (not) + EVITARE (to avoid). The inevitable cannot be sidestepped — fate that blocks every escape route.
words.tip: IN + EVIT + ABLE. Think: you can't evict the inevitable.
words.example: Change is inevitable.

words.commentary: From Latin infallibilis, incapable of error. IN (not) + FALLERE (to deceive). Something infallible cannot fail — certainty that admits no possibility of being wrong.
words.tip: Remember: inFALLible - it cannot FALL into error, double L like fall
words.example: The GPS system seemed infallible until it led us straight into a dead end.

words.commentary: From Latin infamis, of bad repute. IN (not) + FAMA (fame). Infamous is fame inverted — being known not for greatness but for terrible acts.
words.tip: Remember: IN-famous keeps the Latin FAMA (fame) - no extra 'e' needed
words.example: The infamous bank robber was finally captured after decades on the run.

words.tip: INFECT + ION. Think: infect.
words.example: The wound developed an infection.

words.commentary: Infer means to conclude from evidence. Imply means to hint or suggest.
words.tip: Infer means to conclude from evidence.
words.example: From his tone she inferred that something was wrong.

words.commentary: From Latin inflammare, to set on fire. IN (into) + FLAMMA (flame). Something inflammatory sets passions on fire — words or actions designed to ignite anger.
words.tip: Remember double M like flames: in-FLAMM-atory ignites anger
words.example: The politician's inflammatory remarks sparked heated debate among voters.

words.commentary: From Latin inflare, to blow into. IN (into) + FLARE (to blow). Inflation blows air into prices — value expanding like a balloon until the currency feels hollow.
words.tip: INFLAT + ION. Think: inflate.
words.example: Inflation has risen this quarter.

words.commentary: ENTIAL ending. From medieval Latin influentia. The word originally described astral power flowing down from the stars to affect human destiny.
words.tip: INFLUENT + IAL. Not ENCIAL.
words.example: She is one of the most influential leaders.

words.commentary: From Italian influenza, influence. Named for the influence of the stars.
words.tip: From Italian influenza, influence.
words.example: The influenza outbreak closed several schools.

words.commentary: INFRA (below) + STRUCTURE. Infrastructure is the structure below — the hidden systems that support visible activity. Roads, pipes, and wires are civilization's skeleton.
words.tip: INFRA + STRUCTURE. Below the structure.
words.example: The infrastructure needs upgrading.

words.commentary: From Latin ingeniosus, of good natural ability. IN (in) + GIGNERE (to beget). Someone ingenious has ability born within them — cleverness that feels innate.
words.tip: Remember: inGENious has GEN like 'generate' - born with talent inside
words.example: Her ingenious solution to the puzzle impressed everyone in the room.

words.commentary: From Latin ingredi, to enter. IN (into) + GRADI (to step). An ingredient steps into the mixture — each component entering the whole to change its character.
words.tip: INGREDI + ENT. Ends in ENT.
words.example: The secret ingredient is cinnamon.

words.commentary: From Latin inhaerere, to stick in. IN (in) + HAERERE (to stick). Something inherent sticks within — a quality inseparable from the thing itself.
words.tip: Remember: something inherent 'sticks in' - spell it like 'in-HER-ent'
words.example: Curiosity is an inherent trait in young children who constantly ask questions.

words.commentary: From Latin inhereditare, to make an heir. IN (in) + HERES (heir). Inheritance is what makes you an heir — wealth, property, or traits passed from one generation to the next.
words.tip: INHERIT + ANCE. Ends in ANCE.
words.example: She received a large inheritance.

words.commentary: From Latin initialis, of the beginning. INITIUM (beginning). Your initials are your beginning — the first letters that start your name.
words.tip: Remember INITIUM - your INITIALs begin with I-N-I-T, then add I-A-L
words.example: Please sign your initial here to approve the contract changes.

words.commentary: From Latin initium, a beginning. IN (into) + IRE (to go). Initiative is going into something first — the willingness to begin when others wait.
words.tip: INITI + ATIVE. Three I's.
words.example: She took the initiative to solve the problem.

words.commentary: From Latin injungere, to enjoin. IN plus JUNC plus TION.
words.tip: From Latin injungere, to enjoin.
words.example: The court issued an injunction against the construction.

words.commentary: IN + JUSTICE. The prefix simply negates justice. Injustice is the absence of fairness — the void where equity should be.
words.tip: IN + JUSTICE.
words.example: They fought against injustice.

words.tip: Two N's, ends in ENCE. INNOC + ENCE.
words.example: He protested his innocence.

words.commentary: From Latin innocuus, harmless. IN (not) + NOCERE (to harm). Something innocuous does no harm — so safe it barely registers.
words.tip: Remember: IN-NOC-UOUS. Think 'no harm' - it's innocent and won't knock you out.
words.example: The seemingly innocuous comment sparked an unexpected debate among the colleagues.

words.commentary: From Latin innovare, to make new. IN (into) + NOVUS (new). Innovation introduces newness into what exists — not creation from nothing, but transformation of what was.
words.tip: Two N's. INNOV + ATION.
words.example: Innovation drives economic growth.

words.commentary: From Latin innuendo, by nodding to. IN (toward) + NUERE (to nod). An innuendo nods toward meaning — suggestion delivered through implication rather than statement.
words.tip: Double N, then U-E-N-D-O: the word 'nods' with two N's toward hidden meaning
words.example: Her comment about his 'interesting' fashion choices was clearly an innuendo about his poor taste.

words.commentary: One N, one C. From Latin inoculare, meaning to graft a bud. Medical inoculation is a metaphor from horticulture — grafting immunity onto a person.
words.tip: One N, one C. IN + OCULATE.
words.example: They plan to inoculate the entire population.

words.commentary: From Latin inoculare, to graft. IN (into) + OCULUS (eye or bud). Inoculation was originally a gardening term — grafting a bud into a plant. Medicine borrowed the metaphor for introducing a weakened pathogen.
words.tip: Remember OCULUS (eye): one C, one L. IN-OCUL-ATION avoids double letters.
words.example: The doctor recommended getting a flu inoculation before the winter season began.

words.tip: US: inquiry (IN). UK: enquiry (EN).
words.example: The police launched an inquiry.

words.commentary: From Latin insatiabilis, that cannot be satisfied. IN (not) + SATIARE (to satisfy). Something insatiable can never be filled — hunger that grows with feeding.
words.tip: Remember SATI- like 'satisfy' - you can't satisfy something insatiable
words.example: Her insatiable curiosity led her to read every book in the library.

words.commentary: From Latin inscribere, to write upon. IN (upon) + SCRIBERE (to write). An inscription is writing placed upon a surface — words carved into permanence.
words.tip: Remember SCRIB from Latin 'to write' - inscription has the same SCRIB as scribble.
words.example: The ancient inscription on the tombstone had weathered over centuries but was still legible.

words.commentary: From Latin insidiosus, cunning. INSIDIAE (ambush). Something insidious ambushes from within — harm that advances so gradually you don't notice until it's too late.
words.tip: Remember INSIDIOUS hides inside like an ambush - keep the 'i' after 'd'
words.example: The insidious effects of sleep deprivation crept up on her over months of working late shifts.

words.commentary: IN + SIGNIFICANT. The prefix removes significance — something insignificant carries no meaningful weight or importance.
words.tip: IN + SIGNIFICANT.
words.example: The difference was insignificant.

words.commentary: From Latin insinuare, to wind one's way in. IN (into) + SINUARE (to curve). To insinuate is to curve your way in — suggestion that enters sideways.
words.tip: Remember: inSINuate has SIN in the middle, like a sneaky suggestion
words.example: She didn't directly accuse him, but managed to insinuate that he was lying.

words.commentary: From Latin insipidus, without taste. IN (not) + SAPIDUS (tasty). Something insipid lacks flavor — blandness so thorough it becomes its own form of offense.
words.tip: Remember: IN-SIPID has one P, like 'sip' - you barely sip something tasteless.
words.example: The restaurant's insipid soup tasted like nothing more than warm water with a few floating vegetables.

words.commentary: From Latin insolens, unaccustomed. IN (not) + SOLERE (to be accustomed). Someone insolent behaves in unaccustomed ways — rudeness that violates expected norms.
words.tip: Remember: insolENT people violate social norms - think 'solEmn' behavior turned upside down.
words.example: The insolent student rolled her eyes and talked back to the teacher during class.

words.commentary: From Latin insomnia, sleeplessness. IN (not) + SOMNUS (sleep). The inability to sleep. The double consonant pattern is sometimes misplaced — it is 'mn' not 'mm'.
words.tip: Remember: in-SOM-nia has 'mn' not 'mm' - think somnus (Latin for sleep)
words.example: Her chronic insomnia left her exhausted during the day despite lying awake all night.

words.commentary: From Latin inspicere, to look into. IN (into) + SPECERE (to look). Inspection looks deeply into something — examination that penetrates the surface.
words.tip: INSPECT + ION. Think: inspect.
words.example: The building passed the safety inspection.

words.commentary: From Latin inspirare, to breathe into. IN (into) + SPIRARE (to breathe). Inspiration is breath entering — ideas arriving like air filling the lungs, vital and involuntary.
words.tip: INSPIR + ATION. Think: inspire.
words.example: Nature is a constant source of inspiration.

words.commentary: From Latin installare, to place in a stall. IN (in) + STALLUM (a standing place). An installation places something in its standing place — whether equipment in a building or art in a gallery.
words.tip: Two L's. INSTALL + ATION.
words.example: The installation of the new system took a week.

words.commentary: From Latin instigare, to goad. IN (toward) + a root meaning to prick. To instigate is to prick someone toward action — provocation as a pointed tool.
words.tip: Remember: IN-STI-GATE - think 'stick' in the middle to prick someone into action
words.example: The activist tried to instigate peaceful protests throughout the city.

words.commentary: From Latin instituere, to set up. IN (in) + STATUERE (to cause to stand). An institution is something caused to stand — a structure designed to persist beyond its founders.
words.tip: INSTITU + TION. Think: institute.
words.example: Harvard is a prestigious institution.

words.commentary: From Latin instruere, to build in. IN (in) + STRUERE (to pile up). Instructions build knowledge into the learner — understanding constructed layer by layer.
words.tip: INSTRUCT + ION. Think: instruct.
words.example: Follow the instruction manual carefully.

words.commentary: From Latin instruere, to build or equip. IN (in) + STRUERE (to build). An instructor builds knowledge into students — education as internal construction.
words.tip: Remember: instructOR builds knowledge, like a constructOR builds buildings
words.example: The yoga instructor demonstrated the proper breathing technique for the relaxation pose.

words.commentary: From Latin insufficiens, not enough. IN (not) + SUB (under) + FACERE (to make). Insufficient means not made to the level needed — falling short of the mark.
words.tip: Two F's. IN + SUFFICI + ENT.
words.example: The evidence was insufficient for a conviction.

words.commentary: From Old French enseurance, assurance. Insurance is a guarantee against loss — the word promises that you will be made sure (secure) again.
words.tip: INSUR + ANCE. Ends in ANCE.
words.example: Health insurance is expensive.

words.commentary: From Latin integrare, to make whole. INTEGER (whole). To integrate is to make whole — combining separate parts into a unified system.
words.tip: INTEGR + ATE. Not INTER.
words.example: We need to integrate the new employees.

words.commentary: From Latin integrare, to make whole. INTEGER (whole or untouched). Integration makes whole what was separated — completeness achieved through combination.
words.tip: Remember INTEGER (whole number) + -ation = integration. No extra 'er' needed.
words.example: The integration of renewable energy sources into the power grid requires careful planning.

words.commentary: From Latin integritas, wholeness. INTEGER (whole). Integrity is wholeness of character — values that remain intact under pressure.
words.tip: Remember INTEGER (whole number) - integrity keeps your character whole and intact.
words.example: The journalist's integrity was never questioned, even when offered bribes for false stories.

words.commentary: From Latin intellectualis, of understanding. INTER (between) + LEGERE (to choose). An intellectual chooses between ideas — the mind exercising its capacity for discernment.
words.tip: Two L's. INTELLECT + UAL.
words.example: The debate was an intellectual challenge.

words.commentary: Two L's and ENCE at the end. From Latin intelligere, meaning to understand or perceive. The double L reflects the Latin root.
words.tip: Two L's. INTELLIG + ENCE.
words.example: Artificial intelligence is advancing rapidly.

words.commentary: From Latin intensivus, stretched or strained. Something intensive is stretched to its full extent — concentrated and thorough.
words.tip: INTENS + IVE. Think: intense.
words.example: The course was intensive.

words.commentary: From Latin intendere, to stretch toward. IN (toward) + TENDERE (to stretch). An intention stretches toward a goal — the mind reaching for what it wants.
words.tip: Remember: inTENTion has TENT - like stretching a tent toward your goal
words.example: Her intention was to finish the project before the deadline.

words.commentary: From INTER (between) + ACTION. An interaction is action between parties — behavior that requires at least two participants to exist.
words.tip: Remember: INTER (between) + ACTION - one R in inter, one T in action.
words.example: The teacher encouraged more interaction between students during group discussions.

words.commentary: From Latin intercipere, to catch between. INTER (between) + CAPERE (to take). To intercept is to catch something in transit — seizing what was meant for someone else.
words.tip: Remember INTER-CEPT: catch between with double C from Latin capere (to take)
words.example: The fighter jets were scrambled to intercept the unidentified aircraft.

words.commentary: Don't drop the E after the first syllable. INTER + EST + ING — all three parts must be present.
words.tip: INTEREST + ING. Don't drop the second E.
words.example: That was an interesting lecture.

words.commentary: From Latin inter plus facies, face. INTER plus FACE.
words.tip: From Latin inter plus facies, face.
words.example: The new interface was much more intuitive.

words.commentary: From Latin interferire, to strike between. INTER (between) + FERIRE (to strike). Interference strikes between two things, disrupting their connection.
words.tip: INTERFER + ENCE. Ends in ENCE.
words.example: There was radio interference.

words.commentary: From Latin interim, meanwhile. INTER (between). An interim is the time between — the gap that separates what was from what will be.
words.tip: Remember INTER (between) + IM: the time between events has an 'i' before 'm'
words.example: She served as interim director while they searched for a permanent replacement.

words.commentary: From Latin intermittere, to leave off. INTER (between) + MITTERE (to send). Something intermittent sends pauses between bursts — activity that stops and starts.
words.tip: Remember: INTER + MITTENT - think 'between sending' with double T
words.example: The intermittent rain made it difficult to plan outdoor activities.

words.commentary: From Latin interpretari, to explain. INTER (between) + a root meaning to negotiate. Interpretation stands between a text and its meaning — bridging the gap between expression and understanding.
words.tip: INTERPRET + ATION. Think: interpret.
words.example: Her interpretation of the data was unique.

words.commentary: From Latin interrumpere, to break apart. INTER (between) + RUMPERE (to break). An interruption breaks between moments — continuity shattered by intrusion.
words.tip: Two R's. INTERRUPT + ION.
words.example: Please excuse the interruption.

words.commentary: From Latin intervenire, to come between. INTER (between) + VENIRE (to come). An intervention comes between a person and their trajectory — redirecting by stepping into the path.
words.tip: Think INTER-VEN-TION: someone comes between (inter) to prevent (ven) action (tion)
words.example: The teacher's timely intervention prevented the argument from escalating into a fight.

words.commentary: From Latin intimidare, to make afraid. IN (into) + TIMIDUS (fearful). To intimidate is to push someone into fear — courage dissolved by the pressure of threat.
words.tip: Remember: IN-TIMID-ATE - push someone INTO being TIMID
words.example: The bully tried to intimidate the younger students during lunch break.

words.commentary: From Latin intoxicare, to poison. IN (in) + TOXICUM (poison). To intoxicate is to put poison in — alcohol understood as a pleasant poison that alters perception.
words.tip: Remember: inTOXicate contains TOXIC - poison that alters your mind
words.example: The bartender was trained to recognize when alcohol might intoxicate patrons beyond safe limits.

words.commentary: From Latin intra (within) + musculus (little mouse — muscles looked like mice under the skin). An injection into the muscle. The 'musc' section reflects the mouse origin.
words.tip: Remember: intra-MUSC-ular - the mouse (musc) is within the muscle
words.example: The nurse administered the vaccine via intramuscular injection into the patient's deltoid muscle.

words.commentary: From Latin intra (within) + vena (vein). Within the veins — how IV fluids are delivered. The 'ven' in the middle comes directly from vein.
words.tip: Remember: intra-VEN-ous has 'ven' from vein, not 'vein' spelled out
words.example: The patient received antibiotics through an intravenous drip in the hospital.

words.commentary: From Latin intrepidus, not alarmed. IN (not) + TREPIDUS (alarmed). Someone intrepid is not alarmed — courage defined by the absence of fear.
words.tip: Remember: IN-TREPID keeps the Latin TREPID root intact - don't drop letters
words.example: The intrepid explorer ventured deep into the uncharted jungle despite the dangers.

words.commentary: From Latin intricare, to entangle. IN (in) + TRICAE (perplexities). Something intricate is entangled in complexity — detail woven so densely it demands close attention.
words.tip: Remember the Latin root: IN-TRIC-ATE, like tangled TRICKS that are intricate.
words.example: The watchmaker carefully examined the intricate mechanism of the antique timepiece.

words.commentary: From Latin intrinsecus, inwardly. INTRA (within). Something intrinsic exists within — value that belongs to the thing itself, not added from outside.
words.tip: Remember INTRA (within) + nsic: intrinsic value comes from within
words.example: The intrinsic beauty of the sunset needed no enhancement or filter.

words.commentary: From Latin introducere, to lead inside. INTRO (inward) + DUCERE (to lead). An introduction leads you inside — whether into a person's acquaintance or a subject's territory.
words.tip: INTRODUCT + ION. Think: introduce.
words.example: The introduction to the book was captivating.

words.commentary: From Latin intueri, to look upon. IN (at) + TUERI (to look). Intuition is looking inward — knowledge that arrives through internal sight rather than external evidence.
words.tip: Remember: inTUItion has TUIT - think 'to look at' from Latin tueri
words.example: Her intuition told her something wasn't right about the situation.

words.commentary: From Latin inundare, to overflow. IN (upon) + UNDA (wave). To inundate is to send waves upon — flooding that overwhelms capacity.
words.tip: Remember UNDA (wave) - one N, then UN like UNder water.
words.example: Heavy rains continued to inundate the coastal town for three days.

words.commentary: From Latin inventorium, a list of things found. INVENIRE (to find). An inventory is a catalog of findings — everything discovered and recorded in a space.
words.tip: INVENT + ORY.
words.example: The store conducted an inventory check.

words.commentary: From Latin investigare, to track. IN (into) + VESTIGIUM (footprint). To investigate is to follow footprints into the truth — detection as tracking.
words.tip: Remember: IN-VEST-I-GATE — you vest time to investigate, with 'i' before 'gate'
words.example: The detective will investigate the mysterious disappearance of the ancient artifact.

words.tip: INVESTIGAT + ION. Think: investigate.
words.example: The investigation is still ongoing.

words.commentary: From Latin investire, to clothe. IN (in) + VESTIS (garment). An investment originally meant to clothe someone with authority. Finance borrowed the metaphor — money dressed in the hope of returns.
words.tip: Remember: you VEST someone with authority - investment keeps the VEST.
words.example: Her investment in renewable energy stocks paid off handsomely last year.

words.commentary: From Latin invincibilis, unconquerable. IN (not) + VINCERE (to conquer). Someone invincible cannot be conquered — strength beyond the reach of defeat.
words.tip: Remember 'invincible' has double 'i' - you're invincible if you can't be 'convinced' to lose
words.example: The superhero seemed invincible until he met his match in the final battle.

words.commentary: From Latin invisibilis, not able to be seen. IN (not) + VISIBILIS (visible). Something invisible exists beyond the reach of sight — present but undetectable by the eye.
words.tip: IN + VIS + IBLE. Ends in IBLE.
words.example: The stain was nearly invisible.

words.tip: INVIT + ATION. Think: invite.
words.example: Did you receive the wedding invitation?

words.tip: INVOLVE + MENT. Keep the E.
words.example: Community involvement makes a difference.

words.commentary: From Greek eironeia, simulated ignorance. An ironist pretends not to know — meaning hiding behind its opposite.
words.tip: Think 'iron-y' - as hard as iron, but with a Y ending
words.example: The irony was that the fire station burned down while the firefighters were out on a call.

words.commentary: Two R's and IBLE not ABLE. The IR prefix means not, so something irresistible cannot be resisted. The double R comes from the prefix meeting the root.
words.tip: Ends in IBLE, not ABLE. IR + RESIST + IBLE.
words.example: The chocolate cake was irresistible.

words.commentary: From Latin irreverens, disrespectful. IR (not) + REVERERI (to stand in awe). Someone irreverent refuses to stand in awe — disrespect as a deliberate stance.
words.tip: IR-REVERENT: not reverent, showing no reverence or respect
words.example: The comedian's irreverent jokes about politics made some audience members uncomfortable.

words.commentary: From Latin irrigare, to water. IN (into) + RIGARE (to wet). Irrigation brings water into dry land — artificial rain directed where nature does not provide enough.
words.tip: Two R's. IRRIG + ATION.
words.example: The farm uses drip irrigation.

words.commentary: From Italian isolato, made into an island. Latin INSULA (island). Isolation makes something into an island — separation that surrounds with emptiness on all sides.
words.tip: ISOLAT + ION. Think: isolate.
words.example: The village was in complete isolation.

words.commentary: From Latin itinerans, traveling. ITER (journey). An itinerant person is always journeying — movement as a way of life rather than a means to a destination.
words.tip: Remember ITER (journey) - itinerant travelers are always journeying with -ant ending
words.example: The itinerant musician traveled from town to town, performing in local venues.

words.commentary: From Latin itinerarium, a road guide. ITER (journey). An itinerary maps a journey — the planned path with its scheduled stops and destinations.
words.tip: ITINER + ARY. Think: I TIN ER ARY.
words.example: What's on the travel itinerary for tomorrow?

words.commentary: Its is the possessive of it with no apostrophe needed.
words.tip: Its is the possessive of it with no apostrophe needed.
words.example: The cat licked its paw.

words.commentary: From German Jager (hunter) + Meister (master). JAGER-MEISTER. The umlaut (a with two dots) is often dropped in English. Not 'jagermiester' — the 'ei' makes an 'eye' sound in German.
words.tip: Remember: German 'ei' sounds like 'eye' - so MEIster not MIEster
words.example: The bartender served shots of Jägermeister with the traditional herbal liqueur ceremony.

words.commentary: From Xalapa (Jalapa), Mexico, where it was cultivated. The ñ makes a 'ny' sound. Without the tilde, it would be 'jalapeno' which is the common English approximation.
words.tip: Remember the Mexican city Xalapa - the ñ makes a 'ny' sound like 'canyon'
words.example: She added diced jalapeño to the salsa for extra heat.

words.commentary: The EALOUS ending is distinctive. The word comes from Old French jalous, and English added the E to distinguish it from other words.
words.tip: JEAL + OUS. Think: ZEAL with a J.
words.example: He was jealous of her success.

words.tip: US: Z. UK: S. JEOPARD + IZE.
words.example: Don't jeopardize your career.

words.commentary: Named after Jeroboam, a biblical king of Israel. JE-RO-BO-AM. A 3-liter wine bottle (or 4.5L for Champagne). Not 'jeroboham' or 'jeraboam'. Four syllables, all vowels clear.
words.tip: Remember the biblical king Jeroboam: JE-RO-BO-AM, four clear syllables like his royal name.
words.example: The sommelier proudly displayed the jeroboam of vintage Bordeaux for the anniversary celebration.

words.commentary: From Old French jouel, plaything. In American English: jewelry (three syllables). In British English: jewellery (four syllables, double l). Neither spelling is wrong — it depends on your audience.
words.tip: US: jewelry. UK: jewellery.
words.example: She wore expensive jewelry.

words.tip: JOURNAL + ISM. Think: journal.
words.example: She studied journalism at university.

words.commentary: From Latin jubilare, to shout for joy. Someone jubilant shouts with joy — celebration so intense it demands vocal expression.
words.tip: Remember 'jubilant' has an 'a' - think 'jubi-LAnt' like shouting 'La!'
words.example: The team was jubilant after scoring the winning goal in overtime.

words.commentary: From Latin jubilare, to shout for joy. Jubilation is joy too large for silence — happiness that demands vocal expression.
words.tip: Remember: JUBILation - like 'jubilee' celebration with -ation ending
words.example: The team erupted in jubilation when they scored the winning goal.

words.tip: US: judgment (no E). UK: judgement (with E).
words.example: The court issued its judgment.

words.commentary: From Latin judicium, judgment. JUDEX (judge). Someone judicious judges well — wisdom applied to decisions with careful discrimination.
words.tip: Remember JUDEX (judge) - it's judi-CI-ous with 'ci' like in judicial
words.example: The judicious use of savings helped them weather the financial crisis.

words.commentary: From Arabic julab, from Persian gulab, rosewater. JU-LEP. Most famous in the mint julep. Not 'julip' or 'jullep'. Five letters, e not i.
words.tip: Think 'JU-LEP' like the Arabic origins - just five letters with E not I
words.example: The bartender carefully muddled fresh mint leaves for the traditional Kentucky Derby julep.

words.commentary: From Latin jurisdictio, administration of justice. JURIS (law) + DICTIO (saying). Jurisdiction is the saying of law — authority defined by where your legal voice carries.
words.tip: Remember: JURIS (law) + DICTION (speaking) = jurisdiction - legal speaking authority
words.example: The federal court had jurisdiction over the interstate commerce dispute.

words.tip: JUST + ICE. Think: just ice.
words.example: Justice must be served.

words.commentary: From Latin justificare, to make just. JUSTUS (just) + FACERE (to make). Justification makes something just — reasoning that transforms questionable into acceptable.
words.tip: JUSTIFIC + ATION. Think: justify.
words.example: There was no justification for his actions.

words.commentary: From Latin juvenilis, youthful. JUVENIS (young). Something juvenile belongs to youth — behavior or characteristics appropriate to the young, sometimes used to criticize adults.
words.tip: JUVEN + ILE. Think: young.
words.example: Juvenile offenders need rehabilitation.

words.commentary: From Latin juxta (beside) + French poser (to place). To juxtapose is to place beside — comparison created through proximity.
words.tip: Remember: juxta (beside) + pose (place) - no extra letters needed
words.example: The photographer chose to juxtapose the modern skyscraper against the historic cathedral.

words.commentary: A Mexican coffee liqueur, the name possibly derived from the Nahuatl language. KAH-LU-A. Not 'kaluha' or 'khalua'. The h comes after the a, not after the l.
words.tip: Remember: KAH-LU-A - the H follows each A, never after L
words.example: She added a splash of Kahlúa to her coffee for a rich, sweet flavor.

words.commentary: From Greek kalos (beautiful) + eidos (form) + skopein (to look). A kaleidoscope shows beautiful forms — beauty created through reflection, symmetry, and rotation of colored fragments.
words.tip: KAL + EIDO + SCOPE. Greek: beautiful form to see.
words.example: The kaleidoscope showed colorful patterns.

words.commentary: From Japanese kara, empty and okesutora, orchestra — empty orchestra, because the singer fills the void. KAR + A + O + KE — four syllables.
words.tip: From Japanese kara (empty) + okesutora (orchestra). Four syllables.
words.example: They ended the night with karaoke at the bar.

words.commentary: From Japanese karate — kara, empty and te, hand. Empty hand — the art of fighting without weapons. Three syllables, the final E is spoken.
words.tip: From Japanese kara (empty) + te (hand). Three syllables: ka-RA-tay.
words.example: He earned his black belt in karate at sixteen.

words.commentary: From Greek keros, wax. Kerosene was named for its waxy residue during refinement — a fuel named after what it leaves behind, not what it does.
words.tip: Remember Greek 'keros' (wax) + 'ene' ending - waxy fuel leaves residue
words.example: The old camping lantern burned kerosene with a steady, bright flame.

words.commentary: From key plus board. The keys were modelled on piano keys.
words.tip: From key plus board.
words.example: She typed quickly on the mechanical keyboard.

words.commentary: From Korean gimchi, salted vegetables. Fermented cabbage. Not 'kimchee' (though that romanization exists). The standard modern spelling is kimchi.
words.tip: Remember: kimchi ends with 'chi' like the martial art, not 'chee'
words.example: The spicy kimchi added a perfect tangy crunch to the rice bowl.

words.commentary: From German Kinder (children) + Garten (garden). A kindergarten is a children's garden — education as cultivation, children growing like plants tended by patient gardeners.
words.tip: KINDER + GARTEN. German for children's garden. T not D.
words.example: Her daughter starts kindergarten next year.

words.commentary: From Greek kinetikos, of motion. KINEIN (to move). Kinetic energy is the energy of motion — physics naming the force contained in moving things.
