words.example: Reading is my favorite pastime.

words.commentary: From Latin paternalis, of a father. PATER (father). Something paternal pertains to fatherhood — authority and protection inherited from the father role.
words.tip: Remember PATER (father) + NAL - paternal authority comes from father
words.example: His paternal grandfather served in the military for twenty years.

words.commentary: From Greek pathos (suffering) + logos (study). Pathology is the study of suffering — disease examined to understand what causes pain.
words.tip: PATH-O-LOGY: the path to studying suffering, with one 'l' in the middle
words.example: The pathology report revealed that the tissue sample showed signs of early-stage cancer.

words.tip: PATI + ENCE. Not PATIENTS (those go to doctors).
words.example: Teaching requires a lot of patience.

words.commentary: From Greek patriotes, fellow countryman. PATRIS (fatherland). Patriotism is love of the fatherland — devotion to the soil that raised you.
words.tip: Remember PATRI- (father) + -OTIC: a patriotic father loves his fatherland
words.example: The patriotic ceremony honored veterans who served their country with distinction.

words.commentary: From Greek patriotes, fellow countryman. PATRIOS (of one's fathers). Patriotism is devotion to the fatherland — loyalty rooted in ancestral connection to place.
words.tip: Remember: PATRI-OT-ISM - father's devotion ends with -ism, not -izm
words.example: Her patriotism was evident in her years of military service and community volunteer work.

words.commentary: From Latin patronus, protector. PATER (father). Patronage is fatherly protection extended to others — support given from a position of power to those with less.
words.tip: Remember PATRON + AGE: a patron's fatherly support through the ages
words.example: The artist relied on the wealthy collector's patronage to fund her ambitious sculpture series.

words.commentary: From Latin patronus, a protector. PATER (father). To patronize is to act as a father-protector — support that can shade into condescension.
words.tip: Remember PATRON + IZE - a patron father protects with Z not S
words.example: The wealthy businessman would patronize local artists by purchasing their work.

words.commentary: From Latin peculiaris, originally meaning of private property (from pecu, cattle — wealth was measured in livestock). Something peculiar was originally just privately owned.
words.tip: PECULI + AR. Ends in AR.
words.example: That's a peculiar coincidence.

words.commentary: From Greek paidagogia, the leading of children. PAIS (child) + AGOGOS (leader). Pedagogy is the art of leading children to knowledge — teaching as guided discovery.
words.tip: Remember: PEDA-GOG-Y - the Greek 'agogos' (leader) keeps the double G
words.example: The professor's innovative pedagogy encouraged students to discover concepts through hands-on experiments.

words.commentary: From Latin pedester, going on foot. PES (foot). A pedestrian travels by foot — the simplest form of transportation, movement powered by the body alone.
words.tip: Remember PED (foot) + ESTRIAN: pedestrians use their feet to walk.
words.example: The pedestrian waited at the crosswalk for the light to change.

words.commentary: From French pencher, to incline. A penchant is an inclination — taste that leans naturally toward a preference.
words.tip: Remember: penchANT like 'ant' - your inclination marches toward what you like
words.example: She has a penchant for vintage jewelry and spends weekends browsing antique shops.

words.commentary: Origin debated — possibly from Welsh pen gwyn (white head) or Portuguese/Spanish origins. The 'gu' combination makes people want to write 'pengiun' or 'pengwin'. Keep the u after the g.
words.tip: Remember: pen-GUIN like 'guilty' - the U follows the G
words.example: The emperor penguin huddled with its chick against the Antarctic wind.

words.commentary: Named after the Penicillium mold by Alexander Fleming. One n, one c, two l's. The double-l follows the Latin diminutive pattern.
words.tip: Remember: pen-i-cill-in - double L like the Latin diminutive, single N at end
words.example: The doctor prescribed penicillin to treat the bacterial infection.

words.commentary: From Latin paeninsula, almost an island. PAENE (almost) + INSULA (island). A peninsula is almost an island — land nearly surrounded by water, connected by a single bridge of earth.
words.tip: PEN + INSULA. Almost an island (insula).
words.example: Florida is a peninsula.

words.commentary: From Italian penna, feather or quill. Tube pasta cut at an angle like a quill pen. Double n, ending in e. Often misspelled 'pene' — which means something entirely different in Italian.
words.tip: Double N like a feather's spine - penne pasta shaped like quills.
words.example: She ordered penne arrabbiata with extra parmesan cheese at the Italian restaurant.

words.commentary: From Latin percipere, to seize entirely. PER (through) + CAPERE (to take). To perceive is to take in completely through the senses.
words.tip: I before E except after C. perCEIve.
words.example: How you perceive the world shapes your reality.

words.tip: PERCENT + AGE.
words.example: What percentage of students passed?

words.commentary: From Latin percipere, to seize thoroughly. PER (through) + CAPERE (to take). Perception is thorough seizing — the mind taking in sensory information and making meaning from it.
words.tip: PER-CEP-TION: through seizing - remember the C before E in the middle
words.example: Her perception of the situation changed dramatically after hearing all the facts.

words.commentary: From Latin perennis, lasting through the year. PER (through) + ANNUS (year). Something perennial lasts through every year — persistence that outlives seasons.
words.tip: Remember: PER-ennial has one R, then double N like aNNual (year)
words.example: The perennial flowers in her garden bloomed faithfully every spring.

words.commentary: From Old French parfournir, to carry out completely. PER (through) + FOURNIR (to provide). A performance provides completely — talent delivered through execution from beginning to end.
words.tip: PERFORM + ANCE. Ends in ANCE.
words.example: The performance was outstanding.

words.commentary: From Latin perfunctorius, careless. PER (through) + FUNGI (to perform). Something perfunctory is performed to get through it — duty discharged without engagement.
words.tip: Remember FUNC like function - you perform the function to get through it
words.example: She gave a perfunctory nod to her colleagues before rushing out of the meeting.

words.commentary: From Greek periphereia, a carrying around. PERI (around) + PHEREIN (to carry). Something peripheral is carried around the edges — present but not central.
words.tip: Remember PERI-PHER-AL: around the edges, like a PHERry going around
words.example: The keyboard and mouse are peripheral devices connected to the computer.

words.commentary: From Latin perjurium, a false oath. PER (destructively) + JURARE (to swear). Perjury is an oath destroyed — truth sworn and then betrayed.
words.tip: Remember 'jury' at the end - perjury destroys what a jury seeks: truth.
words.example: The witness was charged with perjury after lying under oath during the trial.

words.commentary: ENT ending. From Latin permanere, to remain through. The word suggests duration and endurance — something that stays through time.
words.tip: PERMAN + ENT. Ends in ENT.
words.example: The damage is permanent.

words.tip: Two S's. PERMISS + ION.
words.example: You need permission to enter.

words.commentary: From Latin perpetualis, continuous. PER (through) + PETERE (to seek). Something perpetual seeks through time endlessly — duration without terminus.
words.tip: Remember PER-PET-UAL: through time seeking all, like a pet that's always loyal
words.example: The perpetual motion of the grandfather clock's pendulum marked each passing hour.

words.tip: PERSE + CUTE. Not prosecute (that's legal). Persecute means to harass.
words.example: They were persecuted for their beliefs.

words.commentary: From Latin persequi, to follow persistently. PER (through) + SEQUI (to follow). Persecution is relentless following — harassment that pursues without ceasing.
words.tip: Remember PER-SEC-UTION: think 'per second' - relentless following every second
words.example: The journalist faced persecution for exposing government corruption.

words.commentary: ANCE ending. SEVER sits in the middle. The word means to persist strictly, from Latin perseverare. Don't let the VER trick you into writing ENCE.
words.tip: PERSEVERE + ANCE. Sever is in the middle.
words.example: Her perseverance paid off in the end.

words.commentary: From Latin persistere, to continue steadfastly. PER (through) + SISTERE (to stand). Persistence stands through difficulty — the refusal to sit down when standing is hard.
words.tip: Remember: perSISTence keeps the SIST from 'resist' - it SISTs through challenges.
words.example: Her persistence in learning the violin finally paid off when she mastered the difficult piece.

words.tip: PERSONAL + ITY.
words.example: She has a wonderful personality.

words.commentary: Two N's and one L. This French word for staff or people keeps its French double-N. Don't confuse it with personal, which has one N and a different meaning.
words.tip: Two N's, one L. PERSON + NEL.
words.example: The personnel department handles hiring.

words.commentary: From Latin perspicere, to see through. PER (through) + SPECERE (to look). Perspective is looking through — understanding that comes from seeing beyond the surface to what lies behind.
words.tip: PERSPECT + IVE. Think: per + spect (look).
words.example: Try to see it from my perspective.

words.tip: PERS + UADE. Not PUR.
words.example: She tried to persuade him to stay.

words.commentary: From Latin persuadere, to urge thoroughly. PER (thoroughly) + SUADERE (to urge). Persuasion is thorough urging — argument sustained until the listener's mind shifts.
words.tip: Remember SUADE like 'suave' - persuasion needs smooth urging, not force
words.example: Her gentle persuasion was more effective than any aggressive argument could have been.

words.commentary: From Latin pertinens, reaching through. PER (through) + TENERE (to hold). Something pertinent holds through to the topic — relevance that connects directly.
words.tip: Remember: PER-TIN-ENT holds TIN in the middle, like relevant facts hold together.
words.example: The lawyer presented only pertinent evidence that directly supported her client's case.

words.commentary: From Latin pervadere, to go through. PER (through) + VADERE (to go). Something pervasive goes through everything — influence that penetrates every corner.
words.tip: Remember: PER-VAS-IVE with 'vas' like 'vase' - it goes through like water.
words.example: The smell of fresh bread was pervasive throughout the entire house.

words.commentary: From Latin pestis (plague) + caedere (to kill). A pesticide kills pests — chemical warfare waged against organisms that threaten crops or health.
words.tip: Remember: pest + i + cide (like homicide) - it kills pests
words.example: The organic farmer avoided using synthetic pesticide on her vegetables.

words.commentary: From Latin petra (rock) + oleum (oil). Petroleum is rock oil — liquid fuel drawn from deep within the earth's stone layers, ancient sunlight stored as chemistry.
words.tip: Remember: PETRa (rock) + oLEUM (oil) = peTRoLEum - rock oil from ancient stone
words.example: The petroleum industry transformed the global economy in the twentieth century.

words.commentary: From Egyptian per-aa, great house. The -aoh ending is unique in English and frequently misspelled as 'pharoah'. The a comes before the o.
words.tip: Remember: pharAOh - the A comes before the O, like in 'great house'
words.example: Tutankhamun was a young pharaoh who ruled ancient Egypt for about ten years.

words.commentary: From Greek pharmakon, drug. PHARMA (drug) + CEUTICAL (relating to preparation). The 'ph' as 'f' and the length of the word create multiple failure points.
words.tip: PH not F. PHARMA + CEUTICAL.
words.example: The pharmaceutical industry develops life-saving drugs.

words.commentary: From Greek pharynx, throat. Inflammation of the pharynx. The y in place of an expected i and the silent-seeming g create the spelling difficulty.
words.tip: Remember: pharYNX becomes pharYNGitis - keep the Y and add G before -itis
words.example: The doctor diagnosed him with pharyngitis after examining his red, swollen throat.

words.commentary: From Greek phainomenon, a thing appearing. PHAINEIN (to show). A phenomenon is something that shows itself — an observable event that demands explanation.
words.tip: PHENOM + ENON. Greek ending.
words.example: The northern lights are a natural phenomenon.

words.commentary: From Greek philanthropia, love of humanity. PHILOS (loving) + ANTHROPOS (human). Philanthropy is the love of humanity expressed through giving — generosity as an act of species-wide affection.
words.tip: Remember: PHIL (love) + ANTHROP (human) + Y - philanthropy loves humans
words.example: The billionaire's philanthropy focused on education and healthcare initiatives worldwide.

words.commentary: From Greek philosophos, lover of wisdom. PHILOS (loving) + SOPHIA (wisdom). A philosopher loves wisdom — not knowledge, but the deeper understanding beneath it.
words.tip: Remember PHIL-OS-O-PHER: Phil loves (philos) wisdom (sophia) with an O
words.example: The philosopher spent years contemplating the nature of existence and human consciousness.

words.commentary: From Greek philosophia, love of wisdom. PHILOS (loving) + SOPHIA (wisdom). Philosophy is the love of wisdom — not the possession of answers, but the passionate pursuit of understanding.
words.tip: PHILO + SOPHY. Love of wisdom.
words.example: She majored in philosophy.

words.commentary: Deliberate misspelling of fishing. Luring victims like fish.
words.tip: Deliberate misspelling of fishing.
words.example: The phishing email looked like a bank notification.

words.commentary: From Vietnamese pho, possibly from French feu (fire, as in pot-au-feu). A Vietnamese noodle soup. Three letters, one syllable, pronounced 'fuh'. The simplest spelling that still confuses people.
words.tip: Think 'photo' minus 'to' - pho keeps the 'ph' from French origins.
words.example: We ordered steaming bowls of pho at the Vietnamese restaurant downtown.

words.commentary: From Greek photos (light) + graphein (to write). A photograph writes with light — reality captured by allowing light to inscribe an image.
words.tip: PHOTO + GRAPH. Writing with light.
words.example: The photograph captured the moment.

words.commentary: From Greek photos (light) + graphein (to write). Photography is writing with light — images drawn by exposing surfaces to controlled illumination.
words.tip: Remember: PHOTO (light) + GRAPHY (writing) - don't drop the 'h' in graphy!
words.example: She studied photography at art school and now runs her own portrait studio.

words.commentary: From Greek phyllo, leaf. Paper-thin pastry dough. Also spelled 'filo' — both are correct. The ph makes an f sound, the y makes an 'ih' sound. Double l.
words.tip: Think 'PHYLLo' - Greek for leaf, like chloroPHYLL. Double L like a leaf's veins.
words.example: She carefully layered the delicate phyllo sheets to make baklava.

words.tip: PHYSIC + IAN. Think: physics of the body.
words.example: Consult your physician before taking medication.

words.commentary: From Greek physis (nature) + therapeia (healing). Healing through natural means — exercise and movement. Also called physical therapy in the US.
words.tip: Remember: PHYSIcal + THERAPY = physiotherapy (physio- means nature in Greek)
words.example: After her knee surgery, Sarah attended physiotherapy sessions twice a week to regain strength and mobility.

words.commentary: From Pilsen (Plzen), a city in the Czech Republic where this style was first brewed in 1842. PILS-NER. Also spelled 'pilsener'. Not 'pilzner' or 'pilsnar'.
words.tip: Remember Pilsen, Czech Republic: PILS + NER, not pilsnar or pilzner
words.example: The bartender recommended a crisp German pilsner to pair with the bratwurst.

words.commentary: From French pinot, related to pin (pine), because the grape clusters resemble pine cones. PI-NOT. The t is silent. People add letters — 'pinnot' or 'peenot'. Two syllables, four letters that matter.
words.tip: Think 'pine' + 'ot' - grape clusters look like pine cones
words.example: The sommelier recommended a crisp pinot grigio to pair with the seafood.

words.commentary: From French pionnier, a foot soldier who clears the way. Latin PES (foot). A pioneer walks first — clearing the path for others to follow.
words.tip: Remember: a pioneer needs ONE 'n' but TWO 'e's to see ahead clearly
words.example: Marie Curie was a pioneer in radioactivity research, opening new frontiers in science.

words.commentary: From Pisco, a port city in Peru. PIS-CO. A South American grape brandy. Not 'pisca' or 'pisko'. Five letters, two syllables, no nonsense.
words.tip: Remember the Peruvian port city: Pisco ends in -co like Mexico.
words.example: The bartender crafted a perfect pisco sour with lime juice and egg white.

words.commentary: From picture element. PIC plus SEL contracted to PIXEL.
words.tip: From picture element.
words.example: The image was only 100 pixels wide.

words.commentary: From Latin placare, to calm. Someone placated has been calmed — anger soothed by concession or kindness.
words.tip: Think 'place + ate' - one 'c', one 't' like Latin placare
words.example: The manager tried to placate angry customers with discount vouchers.

words.commentary: From Latin placebo, I shall please. A treatment with no therapeutic effect given to please the patient. The 'ceb' middle section is sometimes misspelled 'seb'.
words.tip: Remember 'place' + 'bo' - it's placed to please, with 'ceb' not 'seb'
words.example: The patients in the control group received a placebo instead of the actual medication.

words.commentary: From Latin plagiarius, a kidnapper. Plagiarism kidnaps another's words — stealing intellectual children and claiming them as your own.
words.tip: PLAGI + AR + ISM. Not PLAGER.
words.example: Plagiarism is a serious academic offense.

words.commentary: From Old French plaintif, complaining. PLAIN plus TIFF.
words.tip: From Old French plaintif, complaining.
words.example: The plaintiff sought damages of one million dollars.

words.commentary: From Latin plantatio, a planting. PLANTA (a sprout). A plantation is a large-scale planting — agriculture organized for mass production, with a history that includes the exploitation of human labor.
words.tip: Remember PLANT + ATION: you plant plants on a plantation.
words.example: The old cotton plantation had been converted into a historical museum.

words.commentary: From French plateau, itself from Old French platel, flat object. The AU makes an OH sound, the T is silent — classic French.
words.tip: From French for flat elevated land. The T and X are both silent.
words.example: The road climbed until it reached a wide plateau.

words.commentary: From Latin plausibilis, deserving applause. PLAUDERE (to applaud). Something plausible deserves initial applause — believable enough to earn consideration.
words.tip: Think 'applause' - if it's plausible, it deserves applause for being believable
words.example: Her explanation for being late seemed plausible until we checked the security cameras.

words.commentary: WRIGHT not WRITE. A playwright is a play-wright, someone who wrights (constructs) plays, just as a wheelwright builds wheels.
words.tip: Think wheelwright, not playwrite - a wright builds things, including plays.
words.example: Shakespeare was the most famous playwright in English literature.

words.commentary: From Greek pneumonia, lung disease. PNEUMON (lung). The p is silent at the start, following the Greek convention where pn- represents a single nasal sound that English speakers cannot easily produce.
words.tip: PNEUM + ONIA. Silent P.
words.example: She was hospitalized with pneumonia.

words.commentary: From French poignant, pricking. POINDRE (to prick). Something poignant pricks the emotions — feeling sharp enough to pierce composure.
words.tip: Remember: poiGNant has GN like 'sign' - both prick your attention silently
words.example: The veteran's poignant speech about his fallen comrades moved the entire audience to tears.

words.commentary: From Greek politikos, of citizens. POLIS (city). A politician is concerned with the city — public life managed by those who claim to serve the community.
words.tip: POLITIC + IAN. Think: politics.
words.example: The politician made promises.

words.tip: Two L's. POLLUT + ION.
words.example: Air pollution affects everyone.

words.commentary: From German poltern, to rumble or make noise and Geist, ghost. A poltergeist is literally a noisy ghost — the etymology describes exactly what it is.
words.tip: From German poltern (to rumble) + Geist (ghost). A noisy ghost.
words.example: The family blamed the broken dishes on a poltergeist.

words.commentary: From Latin ponderosus, heavy. PONDUS (weight). Something ponderous carries great weight — heaviness that slows everything it touches.
words.tip: Think POND + ER + OUS: the pond holds heavy water
words.example: The professor's ponderous lecture on medieval taxation put half the class to sleep.

words.tip: POPULAR + ITY.
words.example: The show gained popularity quickly.

words.commentary: From Latin populatio, a people. POPULUS (people). Population is the people themselves counted — humanity measured in numbers.
words.tip: POPULAT + ION. Think: populate.
words.example: The population has grown rapidly.

words.commentary: From Italian portafoglio, carry-leaf. PORTARE (to carry) + FOGLIO (leaf). A portfolio originally carried loose papers — sheets of work gathered but not bound.
words.tip: PORT + FOLIO. Think: carry leaves.
words.example: She presented her design portfolio.

words.commentary: From Latin possessio, an occupation. POSSIDERE (to occupy). Possession is occupation — holding something so firmly it becomes an extension of the self.
words.tip: Two S pairs. POSSESS + ION.
words.example: The stolen goods were in his possession.

words.tip: POSSIBIL + ITY. Two S's.
words.example: There is a strong possibility of rain.

words.commentary: From Latin postponere, to place after. POST (after) + PONERE (to place). To postpone is to place after the present moment — delay as temporal rearrangement.
words.tip: POST + PONE. Think: place after.
words.example: We had to postpone the meeting.

words.commentary: From Dutch potasschen, pot ashes. Potassium was first isolated from the ashes left in cooking pots — a vital element named after the humble residue of domestic fires.
words.tip: Think 'pot ashes' - double S in the middle, IUM ending
words.example: Bananas are an excellent source of potassium for maintaining healthy blood pressure.

words.commentary: The singular has no E, but the plural (potatoes) adds one. This caused a famous political embarrassment when a vice president misspelled the plural on a chalkboard.
words.tip: No E at the end. Plural is POTATOES (with E).
words.example: She baked a potato for dinner.

words.commentary: From two villages in Burgundy: Pouilly and Fuisse. POUIL-LY FU-IS-SE. Double l in Pouilly, double s in Fuisse. A premium Chardonnay. Accents on the e are often dropped in English.
words.tip: Remember the two French villages: Pouilly (double L) and Fuissé (double S)
words.example: The sommelier recommended a crisp pouilly-fuissé to pair with the lobster bisque.

words.tip: PRACTICAL + LY.
words.example: The task is practically impossible.

words.commentary: In British English, practise is the verb and practice is the noun. American English uses practice for both. The split follows the same logic as licence/license and advice/advise.
words.tip: US: practice for both. UK: practise (verb) vs practice (noun).
words.example: You need to practise every day.

words.commentary: From Greek pragmatikos, fit for business. PRAGMA (deed). Someone pragmatic focuses on deeds — practicality that prefers results over theory.
words.tip: Remember PRAGMA (deed) - pragmAtic focuses on Actual results, not theory.
words.example: The CEO took a pragmatic approach, choosing the solution that worked rather than the most elegant one.

words.commentary: Named after César du Plessis-Praslin, a French diplomat whose chef invented sugar-coated almonds. The accent on the e is optional in English. Not 'praline' is also accepted.
words.tip: Think Praslin palace chef - the é gives it French flair
words.example: The chocolate shop specialized in handmade praliné truffles with roasted hazelnuts.

words.commentary: From Latin precarius, obtained by prayer. PREX (prayer). Something precarious depends on prayer — security so uncertain it requires divine intervention.
words.tip: Remember PREC-ARIOUS: like prayer, it needs careful attention to survive.
words.example: The mountain climber found himself in a precarious position on the narrow ledge.

words.commentary: From Latin praecautio, a guarding beforehand. PRAE (before) + CAVERE (to be on guard). A precaution is guarding before danger arrives — safety achieved through anticipation.
words.tip: Think 'caution' with 'pre-' before it - guard with CAUTION beforehand
words.example: As a precaution against rain, she packed an umbrella in her bag.

words.commentary: CEDE not CEED. Only three English words end in CEED (exceed, proceed, succeed). Everything else is CEDE.
words.tip: PRE + CEDE. Not CEED. CEDE means to go.
words.example: Thunder precedes lightning by seconds.

words.commentary: From Latin praecedens, going before. PRAE (before) + CEDERE (to go). A precedent goes before — a past decision that shapes future ones.
words.tip: Remember: precedent goes before with -ent ending, like president and regent.
words.example: The Supreme Court's ruling set a dangerous precedent for future cases.

words.commentary: From Latin praecipitium, a steep fall. PRAE (before) + CAPUT (head). A precipice is where you fall headfirst — the edge beyond which recovery is impossible.
words.tip: Remember: precipice ends in -ice, like the slippery ice that makes you fall.
words.example: The mountain climber carefully avoided the rocky precipice that dropped into the valley below.

words.commentary: From Latin praecox, ripened early. PRAE (before) + COQUERE (to ripen). Someone precocious ripens before their time — ability that arrives ahead of schedule.
words.tip: Remember: precocious has two C's - one before and one in cooking (coquere).
words.example: The precocious five-year-old was already reading chapter books while her classmates learned basic sight words.

words.commentary: From Latin praedator, a plunderer. PRAEDA (prey). A predator takes prey — survival through the capture of others.
words.tip: Remember: predAtor takes prey with an A, not predItor with an I
words.example: The hawk circled overhead, a skilled predator searching for unsuspecting prey below.

words.commentary: From Latin praedecessor, one who goes before. PRAE (before) + DECESSOR (one who departs). A predecessor departed before you arrived — the person who held your position before it was yours.
words.tip: PRE + DE + CESSOR. Think: one who goes before.
words.example: She was more popular than her predecessor.

words.commentary: From Latin praedicamentum, a category. PRAE (before) + DICARE (to proclaim). A predicament was originally a logical category. It came to mean a difficult situation — trouble that defies easy classification.
words.tip: Think PREDICATE + MENT: logical statement plus outcome equals predicament
words.example: The chess player found herself in a predicament when both moves led to checkmate.

words.commentary: From Latin praedominans, ruling before others. PRAE (before) + DOMINARE (to rule). Something predominant rules above all else — the most powerful or common element.
words.tip: Remember DOMINANT - just add PRE before it to rule before others
words.example: The predominant color in the autumn forest was a brilliant golden yellow.

words.commentary: From PRE (before) + FABRICATE. To prefabricate is to build before final assembly — construction completed in a factory, then transported to where it will stand.
words.tip: Remember: PRE + FABRICATE, like fabric in a factory before assembly
words.example: The company decided to prefabricate the wall panels to speed up construction at the building site.

words.commentary: From Latin praeferre, to carry before. PRAE (before) + FERRE (to carry). A preference carries one option before others — priority expressed through choice.
words.tip: PREFER + ENCE. Ends in ENCE.
words.example: Do you have a preference?

words.commentary: From Latin praejudicium, prior judgment. PRAE (before) + JUDICIUM (judgment). Prejudice judges before knowing — the verdict delivered before the trial begins.
words.tip: PRE + JUDICE. Think: pre-judge.
words.example: Prejudice has no place in society.

words.commentary: From Latin praeliminaris, before the threshold. PRAE (before) + LIMEN (threshold). Something preliminary comes before you cross the threshold — preparation that precedes the main event.
words.tip: PRE + LIMIN + ARY. Think: before the threshold.
words.example: The preliminary results look good.

words.commentary: From Latin praeparare, to make ready before. PRAE (before) + PARARE (to make ready). Preparation readies the ground before the work begins.
words.tip: PREPAR + ATION. A not E. Think: prepare.
words.example: Preparation is key to success.

words.commentary: From PRE (before) + REQUISITE. A prerequisite is required before you can proceed — the qualification that must exist before the next step becomes available.
words.tip: PRE-REQUISITE: before what's required. Remember the 'E' in both parts.
words.example: Completing the introductory course is a prerequisite for advanced chemistry.

words.commentary: From Latin praerogativa, previous choice. PRAE (before) + ROGARE (to ask). A prerogative is the right to choose first — privilege that precedes others' options.
words.tip: Think PRAE-ROG-ATIVE: before asking, like a privilege that comes before others can ask.
words.example: As team captain, it's my prerogative to decide the starting lineup.

words.commentary: From Latin praescriptio, a writing beforehand. PRAE (before) + SCRIBERE (to write). Often misspelled 'perscription' — the prefix is PRE, not PER.
words.tip: PRE + SCRIPT + ION. Not PER.
words.example: The doctor wrote a prescription.

words.tip: PRESENTAT + ION. Think: present.
words.example: The presentation lasted an hour.

words.commentary: From Latin praeservare, to guard beforehand. PRAE (before) + SERVARE (to keep). Preservation keeps things safe before they are lost — protection deployed against the erosion of time.
words.tip: PRESERV + ATION. Think: preserve.
words.example: Wildlife preservation is important.

words.tip: PRESID + ENT. Ends in ENT.
words.example: The president addressed the nation.

words.commentary: From Latin praestigiae, tricks or illusions. Prestige originally meant deception — the tricks of a conjurer. Over centuries, it shifted to mean the admiration those tricks inspired.
words.tip: PRESTIG + IOUS. Think: prestige.
words.example: She attended a prestigious university.

words.commentary: From Latin praevalere, to be stronger. PRAE (before) + VALERE (to be strong). Something prevalent is stronger than its alternatives — dominance achieved through widespread presence.
words.tip: PREVAL + ENT. Ends in ENT.
words.example: The disease is prevalent in tropical regions.

words.tip: PREVENT + ION.
words.example: Prevention is better than cure.

words.commentary: From Latin primitivus, first of its kind. PRIMUS (first). Primitive describes what came first — original, foundational, and unrefined.
words.tip: PRIMIT + IVE. I not A.
words.example: The tools were primitive.

words.tip: PRINCIP + AL. The principal is your PAL. Principle is a rule.
words.example: The school principal made an announcement.

words.commentary: From Latin principium, a beginning. PRINCEPS (first). A principle is what comes first — the foundational rule from which all subsequent reasoning follows.
words.tip: PRINCIP + LE. A principle is a ruLE. Principal is a person.
words.example: It's against my principles.

words.tip: PRIOR + ITY. Think: prior.
words.example: Safety is our top priority.

words.commentary: From Latin privilegium, a law for one person. PRIVUS (individual) + LEX (law). A privilege is a law that applies to you alone — special treatment encoded in the rules.
words.tip: PRIVIL + EGE. No D.
words.example: It is a privilege to be here.

words.commentary: From Latin probabilitas, worthiness of approval. PROBARE (to prove). Probability is what can be proved likely — chance measured in degrees of provability.
words.tip: PROBABIL + ITY. Think: probable.
words.example: The probability of success is high.

words.commentary: From Latin probatum, proved. The process of proving a will.
words.tip: From Latin probatum, proved.
words.example: The estate went through probate after her death.

words.commentary: From Latin procedere, to go forward. PRO (forward) + CEDERE (to go). A procedure goes forward step by step — progress organized into sequence.
words.tip: PROCED + URE. One E.
words.example: Follow the standard procedure.

words.commentary: From Latin processio, a moving forward. PRO (forward) + CEDERE (to go). A procession goes forward — a line of people moving in ordered sequence.
words.tip: One C, two S's. PROCESS + ION.
words.example: The wedding procession was beautiful.

words.commentary: From Latin processus, advance. PRO plus CESS plus OR.
words.tip: From Latin processus, advance.
words.example: The new processor was significantly faster.

words.commentary: From Latin procrastinare, to put off until tomorrow. PRO (forward) + CRASTINUS (of tomorrow). To procrastinate is to push tasks into tomorrow — delay that compounds as tomorrows accumulate.
words.tip: Remember CRAS-TIN from Latin 'crastinus' meaning tomorrow - you're putting it off until CRAS-TIN-ate
words.example: She knew she shouldn't procrastinate on her term paper, but Netflix was calling her name.

words.commentary: From Latin producere, to lead forward. PRO (forward) + DUCERE (to lead). Productivity leads things forward — it brings results into existence.
words.tip: PRODUCT + IVE.
words.example: It was a productive meeting.

words.commentary: From Latin producere, to lead forward. PRO (forward) + DUCERE (to lead). Productivity is leading forward — output measured by how much is brought into existence.
words.tip: Remember: DUCT (like a duct that leads things forward) + IV + ITY
words.example: The company's productivity increased significantly after implementing the new workflow system.

words.commentary: Two S's. PROFESS + ION. To profess originally meant to declare publicly ��� a profession is what you publicly declare your expertise in.
words.tip: One F, two S's. PROFESS + ION.
words.example: Teaching is a noble profession.

words.commentary: From Latin professio, a public declaration. PRO (before) + FATERI (to acknowledge). A professional publicly declares their expertise — competence announced and maintained as identity.
words.tip: One F, two S's. PROFESSION + AL.
words.example: She is a true professional.

words.commentary: One F, two S's. From Latin professor, one who publicly declares expertise. The double S comes from the Latin root profiteri.
words.tip: One F, two S's. PRO + FESSOR.
words.example: The professor gave an inspiring lecture.

words.commentary: From Latin proficere, to make progress. PRO (forward) + FACERE (to make). Proficiency is making forward progress — skill advanced to the point of reliable competence.
words.tip: Remember: PRO-FIC-IENCY. Think 'proficient' + 'ency' ending.
words.example: Her proficiency in Spanish helped her land the job at the international firm.

words.commentary: From Latin proficere, to advance. PRO (forward) + FACERE (to make). Something profitable makes forward progress — effort that yields more than it costs.
words.tip: Remember: profit + able = profitable (keep the 't' from profit)
words.example: The new marketing strategy proved highly profitable for the small business.

words.commentary: From Latin profundus, deep. PRO (forward) + FUNDUS (bottom). Something profound reaches the bottom — depth that touches the foundation of understanding.
words.tip: PRO + FOUND.
words.example: The experience had a profound impact.

words.commentary: From Greek prognosis, foreknowledge. PRO (before) + GNOSIS (knowing). Predicting the course of a disease. The 'gn' combination where the g is nearly silent.
words.tip: Remember: PRO-GNOSIS (before-knowing) - one 's' in the middle, silent 'g'
words.example: The doctor gave a cautious prognosis for her recovery from the surgery.

words.commentary: From Greek programma via French programme. Webster trimmed the ME ending.
words.tip: US uses shorter form. UK, CA, AU, NZ use the French spelling.
words.example: The program starts at eight.

words.commentary: British programme retains the French ending for most uses, except in computing where program is universal. The computer sense was established in American English and exported worldwide.
words.tip: US: program. UK/AU/NZ: programme (except for computer programs).
words.example: The evening programme includes a concert.

words.commentary: From programme plus er. PROGRAM plus MER.
words.tip: From programme plus er.
words.example: The programmer fixed the bug in minutes.

words.commentary: From Latin progressio, a going forward. PRO (forward) + GRADI (to step). Progression steps forward steadily — advancement measured one step at a time.
words.tip: Two S's. PROGRESS + ION.
words.example: The disease showed rapid progression.

words.commentary: From Latin prohibere, to hold back. PRO (before) + HABERE (to hold). Prohibition holds back before the act — prevention through forbidding.
words.tip: PROHIBIT + ION. Think: prohibit.
words.example: Prohibition banned alcohol sales.

words.commentary: From Latin proles (offspring) + ferre (to bear). To proliferate is to bear offspring abundantly — rapid multiplication that fills available space.
words.tip: Remember: proli-FER-ate (to BEAR offspring), not prolifor-ate
words.example: Social media platforms allow misinformation to proliferate rapidly across global networks.

words.commentary: From Latin prolificus, generating offspring. PROLES (offspring) + FACERE (to make). Someone prolific makes abundantly — creative output that seems inexhaustible.
words.tip: Remember: PROLES + FACERE = proliFIC (offspring maker ends in -fic)
words.example: The prolific author published twelve novels in just five years.

words.commentary: From Latin prominere, to jut out. PRO (forward) + MINERE (to project). Something prominent projects forward — it cannot be missed because it refuses to recede.
words.tip: PROMIN + ENT. Ends in ENT.
words.example: She held a prominent position.

words.commentary: NUN in the middle, not NOUN. Despite coming from pronounce, the word drops the O. This inconsistency is itself one of the most commonly mispronounced and misspelled words.
words.tip: PRONUNCI + ATION. Not PRONOUNCE + ATION.
words.example: Her pronunciation was perfect.

words.commentary: From Latin propagare, to propagate. PRO (forward) + a root related to fixing or fastening. Propaganda was originally a Vatican committee for spreading the faith. The word now means any systematic spreading of ideas.
words.tip: PROPAG + ANDA. A not O in the second syllable.
words.example: The government spread propaganda.

words.commentary: From Latin propensus, inclined. PRO (forward) + PENDERE (to hang). A propensity hangs forward — natural inclination that leans toward a particular behavior.
words.tip: Think 'propense' + 'ity' - like pensive, it hangs forward with pensity.
words.example: She had a propensity for arriving late to important meetings.

words.commentary: From Greek pro (before) + phylaxis (guarding). Preventive treatment — guarding before disease strikes. Same -phylaxis root as anaphylaxis.
words.tip: Remember: PRO-PHY-LAX-IS - guarding before illness, like relax with PHY
words.example: The dentist recommended fluoride treatments as prophylaxis against tooth decay.

words.commentary: From Latin proportio, a comparative relation. PRO (for) + PORTIO (a share). Proportion is the relationship between shares — balance achieved when parts relate harmoniously to the whole.
words.tip: PROPORT + ION. Think: proportional.
words.example: The room was of generous proportions.

words.commentary: From Latin proponere, to set forth. PRO (forth) + PONERE (to place). A proposition places something forward for consideration.
words.tip: PROPOSIT + ION. Think: propose.
words.example: He made a business proposition.

words.commentary: From Latin pro (before) + exsuctus (sucked out, dried). The sci makes a 'SHOO' sound in Italian. Double t. Often misspelled 'prosciuto' or 'proscuitto'. Remember: sci = sh, double t at the end.
words.tip: Remember: pro-SCI-UT-TO - 'sci' makes 'sh' sound, double T at end
words.example: The antipasto platter featured paper-thin slices of prosciutto wrapped around fresh melon.

words.commentary: From the village of Prosecco near Trieste, Italy. PRO-SEC-CO. Double c, one s. People reverse it — 'prosseco' or 'proseco'. Think: one s, two c's, like the Italian double consonant tradition.
words.tip: One S, two C's - from Prosecco village near Trieste
words.example: We celebrated with a bottle of chilled prosecco on the terrace.

words.commentary: From Latin prosequi, to follow forward. PRO (forward) + SEQUI (to follow). Prosecution follows a case forward — pursuing legal action until resolution.
words.tip: Remember: PRO-SE-CU-TION follows the Latin 'sequi' (follow) with CU not SI
words.example: The prosecution presented compelling evidence to support their case against the defendant.

words.commentary: From Latin prosperitas, good fortune. PRO (for) + SPES (hope). Prosperity is hope fulfilled — fortune that aligns with what was wished for.
words.tip: PROSPER + ITY.
words.example: The region enjoyed economic prosperity.

words.commentary: From Greek prosthesis, an addition. PROS (to) + TITHENAI (to place). Something placed onto the body. The 'sth' cluster in the middle is the spelling challenge. Plural: prostheses.
words.tip: Remember the Greek 'sth' cluster: proSTHesis - Something placed TO the body
words.example: The new leg prosthesis allowed her to walk comfortably again.

words.commentary: From Greek protagonistes, first actor. PROTOS (first) + AGONISTES (actor or competitor). The protagonist is the first actor — the central character around whom the story revolves.
words.tip: Remember: PROT-AG-ON-IST - the first actor needs all four syllables intact.
words.example: Elizabeth Bennet serves as the protagonist in Jane Austen's Pride and Prejudice.

words.tip: PROTECT + ION.
words.example: Sun protection is essential.

words.tip: PROTE + IN. EI not IE.
words.example: Eggs are a great source of protein.

words.commentary: From Latin provincialis, of a province. PROVINCIA (province). Something provincial belongs to the province — narrow perspective shaped by limited exposure.
words.tip: Remember PROVINCE + -IAL: the province has its own special way.
words.example: Her provincial attitude showed when she dismissed all foreign cuisine as 'weird food.'

words.commentary: From Latin provocare, to call forth. PRO (forth) + VOCARE (to call). A provocation calls forth a reaction — stimulus designed to draw response from someone.
words.tip: PROVOCAT + ION. Think: provoke.
words.example: The attack was without provocation.

words.commentary: From Latin prudens, foreseeing. PRO (forward) + VIDERE (to see). Someone prudent sees forward — wisdom that anticipates consequences before acting.
words.tip: Remember: PRUdent sees forward - keep the U after PR, end with -ent
words.example: A prudent investor diversifies their portfolio to minimize risk.

words.commentary: From Greek psora, itch. The silent p at the start follows the Greek ps- pattern (like psychology). A chronic skin condition with an almost unspellable name.
words.tip: Silent P + sore + I + AS + IS: think 'sore' skin condition
words.example: The dermatologist prescribed a new topical treatment for her psoriasis.

words.commentary: From Greek psyche (soul, mind) + iatros (healer). Psychiatry is literally soul-healing — the medical treatment of the mind.
words.tip: PSYCH + IATRIC. Silent P.
words.example: The psychiatric evaluation was thorough.

words.commentary: From Greek psyche (mind) + iatros (healer). A mind healer. The silent p at the start and the 'ch' pronounced as 'k' make this doubly deceptive.
words.tip: Remember: PSYCHe (mind) + iATRist (healer) - silent P, CH sounds like K
words.example: After years of therapy, she decided to become a psychiatrist herself.

words.commentary: From Greek psykhe (mind) + iatreia (healing). Psychiatry is mind-healing — medicine applied to the invisible organ of thought and feeling.
words.tip: Remember: PSYche + iaTRY = psychiatry. The mind tries to heal.
words.example: After years of struggling with anxiety, she finally decided to pursue a career in psychiatry to help others.

words.commentary: From Greek psyche (soul, mind) + logos (study). The study of the mind. Silent p, 'ch' as 'k' — same Greek pattern as psychiatrist.
words.tip: PSYCH + OLOGY. Silent P.
words.example: She studies psychology at university.

words.commentary: From Greek pteron, wing and daktylos, finger — a winged finger. The silent P follows Greek convention shared by psychology and pneumonia.
words.tip: From Greek pteron (wing) + daktylos (finger). Silent P.
words.example: The museum displayed a pterodactyl fossil.

words.commentary: From Latin publicare, to make public. PUBLICUS (of the people). A publication makes something public — private work released into the commons for all to encounter.
words.tip: PUBLIC + ATION.
words.example: The publication was widely read.

words.commentary: No AL before LY. Unlike most -IC words (basically, magically), publicly drops the expected AL. It's one of English's genuine exceptions.
words.tip: PUBLIC + LY. No AL in between.
words.example: She publicly apologized for the mistake.

words.commentary: From Latin pugnax, combative. PUGNUS (fist). Someone pugnacious leads with their fists — combativeness that seeks confrontation.
words.tip: Remember PUGN- from Latin pugnus (fist) - pugnacious fighters use fists
words.example: The pugnacious politician seemed eager to pick fights with anyone who disagreed.

words.commentary: From Italian puntiglio, a fine point. PUNCTUM (point). Someone punctilious attends to every fine point — precision so thorough it borders on obsession.
words.tip: One 'L' only - think 'point' not 'till'
words.example: The punctilious librarian ensured every book was shelved in perfect alphabetical order.

words.commentary: From Latin punctuare, to mark with points. PUNCTUM (a point). Punctuation marks text with points — tiny symbols that organize written language into breathable, comprehensible units.
words.tip: Remember PUNCTUM (point) + ATION - every letter gets its point marked
words.example: The essay's poor punctuation made it difficult to follow the author's arguments.

words.tip: PUNISH + MENT. No E before MENT.
words.example: The punishment fit the crime.

words.commentary: From Latin punitivus, inflicting punishment. POENA (penalty). Something punitive inflicts penalty — action designed to cause suffering as consequence.
words.tip: Remember: punishment ends in -ish, punitive ends in -itive (like positive)
words.example: The company implemented punitive measures against employees who violated safety protocols.

words.tip: PURCH + ASE. Ends in ASE.
words.example: She made a large purchase.

words.commentary: From Italian puttana, a coarse word. A bold pasta sauce of tomatoes, olives, capers, and anchovies. Double t. The name's origin is debated and colorful.
words.tip: Remember the double 't' from Italian puttana - two t's for this bold sauce
words.example: The chef's puttanesca sauce was a perfect balance of salty olives, capers, and anchovies.

words.commentary: From Latin qualificare, to attribute a quality. QUALIS (of what kind) + FACERE (to make). A qualification makes you of a certain kind — credentials that define what you are equipped to do.
words.tip: QUALIFIC + ATION. Think: qualify.
words.example: She has the right qualifications.

words.commentary: Origin uncertain, possibly from Latin quando (when). A quandary is a state of not knowing when or how to act — indecision paralyzed by equal options.
words.tip: Remember 'quan-DAR-y' - sounds like 'wander' but with 'quan' and 'ary'
words.example: She found herself in a quandary when both job offers seemed equally appealing.

words.commentary: From Italian quarantina, forty days. QUARANTA (forty). Quarantine originally lasted forty days — isolation measured by biblical tradition.
words.tip: QUARAN + TINE. From the Italian quaranta (forty days).
words.example: Travelers were placed in quarantine for two weeks.

words.tip: Two R's. QUARR + EL.
words.example: The couple had a quarrel.

words.commentary: From Latin querulus, complaining. QUERI (to complain). Someone querulous complains habitually — dissatisfaction expressed as a constant whine.
words.tip: Think QUERY + ULOUS: questioning complainers are querulous
words.example: The querulous customer demanded to speak with three different managers about the same minor issue.

words.commentary: From Spanish queso, cheese. QUESO (cheese) + ADILLA (diminutive). A cheese-filled tortilla. Double l makes a 'y' sound in Spanish. Not 'quesidilla' or 'quesadila'.
words.tip: Remember: QUESo (cheese) + dillA - it's about cheese, not 'quesidilla'
words.example: She ordered a chicken quesadilla with extra salsa and sour cream.

words.commentary: From French questionnaire, a list of questions. Latin QUAESTIO (a seeking). A questionnaire is a structured seeking — organized curiosity deployed through systematic asking.
words.tip: Two N's. QUESTION + NAIRE.
words.example: Please fill out the questionnaire.

words.commentary: From French queue, tail. Four of the five letters are silent — only the Q is pronounced as expected. The longest common English word where removing four letters still produces the same sound.
words.tip: Remember the French tail: Q-U-E-U-E, like a tail with extra letters
words.example: We waited in a long queue at the movie theater for tickets.

words.commentary: From German Kuchen, cake, adopted through the Alsatian French dialect. The qu makes a 'k' sound, the iche sounds like 'eesh'. Only six letters but frequently misspelled as 'quich' or 'keesh'.
words.tip: Remember the silent 'e' - it's quiche like 'keesh' with a 'qu' start
words.example: She served a delicious spinach and cheese quiche for Sunday brunch.

words.commentary: From Quechua kinwa. An Andean grain-like seed. Three syllables: KEEN-wah. Not 'quinoah' or 'keenwa'. The qu makes a 'k' sound, not 'kw' as in 'queen'.
words.tip: From Quechua 'kinwa' - remember the silent 'u' and no 'h' at end.
words.example: She added protein-rich quinoa to her salad for a nutritious lunch.

words.commentary: From Latin quinta essentia, fifth essence. Medieval philosophers believed a fifth element beyond earth, air, fire, and water defined the purest form of anything.
words.tip: Remember 'quinta' (fifth) + 'essentia' (essence) - keep both double letters: ss and t
words.example: The old bookstore was the quintessential neighborhood gathering place.

words.commentary: From Don Quixote, Cervantes' idealistic knight. Someone quixotic tilts at windmills — idealism so impractical it becomes its own kind of nobility.
words.tip: Remember Don Quixote: X before O, single T - just like the Spanish knight's name
words.example: His quixotic plan to eliminate poverty worldwide was admirable but utterly unrealistic.

words.commentary: From Latin quotare, to number. QUOTUS (how many). A quotation numbers and marks the exact words — precision in attribution.
words.tip: Think QUOTA + TION - you're numbering exact words like a quota
words.example: The professor required a quotation from the primary source to support each argument.

words.tip: RADIAT + ION. Think: radiate.
words.example: The plant leaked radiation.

words.commentary: From Latin radius (ray) + ACTIVE. Something radioactive emits rays — invisible energy radiating from unstable atoms as they seek stability through decay.
words.tip: Remember: RADIO waves are ACTIVE - radio + active = radioactive
words.example: The scientists carefully handled the radioactive materials in the laboratory.

words.commentary: From Turkish raki, possibly from the Arabic word for sweat (araq). RA-KI. An anise-flavored spirit popular across Turkey and the Balkans. Not 'rakki' or 'racki'. Also spelled 'rakia' in the Balkans.
words.tip: Think 'RA-KI' like the Turkish original - one 'k', ends in 'i'
words.example: The waiter served traditional raki alongside the mezze platter.

words.commentary: From Japanese ramen, adapted from Chinese la mian (pulled noodles). Two syllables: RA-men. Not 'rahmen' or 'rayman'. The simplicity of the spelling belies the complexity of the broth.
words.tip: Remember: RA-men rhymes with 'raw men', not 'ray men' or 'raw main'.
words.example: The steaming bowl of ramen warmed her hands as she slurped the rich tonkotsu broth.

words.commentary: From Latin ramificare, to form branches. RAMUS (branch). A ramification is a branching consequence — one decision splitting into many outcomes.
words.tip: Remember RAMUS (branch) + -ification: RAM-I-fication branches out like tree limbs
words.example: The new policy had serious ramifications for small business owners across the state.

words.commentary: From Latin rancor, a stinking sore. Rancor is bitterness that festers — resentment that refuses to heal.
words.tip: Think 'rancid' but with -or ending: festering bitterness needs the Latin -or suffix.
words.example: Years of workplace betrayal had filled him with rancor toward his former colleagues.

words.commentary: From French ratouille, a chunky stew, with rata (army slang for food). Double l, ending in -ille which sounds like 'ee'. The 2007 Pixar film helped people learn the spelling — but many still write 'ratatouile' with one l.
words.tip: Remember the double L in the French -ille ending, like in Bastille
words.example: The chef prepared a traditional Provençal ratatouille with fresh eggplant, zucchini, and tomatoes.

words.commentary: From Latin ratus, fixed. RERI (to think or calculate). Ratification fixes a decision — formal approval that converts a proposal into binding reality.
words.tip: Think 'ratify' + 'ication' — the Latin ratus means fixed, so ratification fixes decisions.
words.example: The treaty awaits ratification by both governments before it becomes legally binding.

words.commentary: From Latin raucus, hoarse. Something raucous is hoarse with noise — disorder loud enough to damage the voice.
words.tip: Remember: rAUcous has AU like the hoarse sound 'AUgh!' it describes
words.example: The raucous laughter from the comedy club could be heard from three blocks away.

words.commentary: The S in realise reflects traditional British practice, though the Z form is also accepted by major British style guides. This is one of the most contested spelling differences between dialects.
words.tip: US: realize (with Z). UK/AU/NZ: realise (with S).
words.example: I didn't realise how late it was.

words.commentary: From REAL + Latin suffix for making. A realization makes something real — whether understanding suddenly crystallizing in the mind or a plan manifesting in the world.
words.tip: US uses Z (realize + ation), UK uses S (realise + ation)
words.example: The realization that she had been walking in the wrong direction hit her suddenly.

words.tip: REASON + ABLE.
words.example: The price was reasonable.

words.commentary: RE (again) + ASSURANCE. Reassurance is being made sure again — having your certainty restored after doubt.
words.tip: RE + ASSUR + ANCE. Two S's.
words.example: She needed reassurance.

words.commentary: From Latin rebellio, a renewed war. RE (again) + BELLUM (war). A rebellion is war renewed — subjects rising against authority, conflict reignited from below.
words.tip: Two L's. REBELL + ION.
words.example: The rebellion was quickly suppressed.

words.commentary: From Anglo-Norman rebuker, to cut down. A rebuke cuts down — sharp criticism that reduces the recipient.
words.tip: Remember: reBUKE ends with -uke, like duke or puke.
words.example: The teacher's sharp rebuke silenced the disruptive student immediately.

words.commentary: From Latin recalcitrare, to kick back. RE (back) + CALCITRARE (to kick). Someone recalcitrant kicks back — defiance expressed through stubborn resistance.
words.tip: Remember: RE-CALC-IT-RANT - like calculating your defiant rant back at authority
words.example: The recalcitrant student refused to follow the dress code despite repeated warnings.

words.commentary: From Anglo-Norman receite, from Latin receptus. The p is silent and was added by scholars in the 16th century to reflect the Latin root. Before that, it was spelled 'receit' — the p has always been decorative.
words.tip: Remember the silent p from Latin - it's decorative but necessary!
words.example: She kept the receipt in case she needed to return the shoes.

words.commentary: EI after C, confirming the old rule. From Latin recipere, meaning to take back. The I-before-E rule works perfectly here.
words.tip: I before E except after C. reCEIve.
words.example: You will receive a confirmation email shortly.

words.commentary: From Latin recipiens, receiving. RE (back) + CAPERE (to take). A recipient takes back — they receive what is given to them.
words.tip: RECIPI + ENT. Think: receive.
words.example: The recipient opened the gift.

words.commentary: From Latin reclusus, shut away. RE (away) + CLAUDERE (to shut). A recluse has shut themselves away — solitude chosen as a permanent condition.
words.tip: Remember: recluse has 'clus' from Latin 'shut' - not 'loose' or 'close'
words.example: The famous author became a recluse after winning the Nobel Prize, rarely leaving his countryside cottage.

words.tip: RE + COGN + ISE/IZE. Think: re-cognition.
words.example: I didn't recognise him with his new haircut.

words.commentary: From Latin recognoscere, to know again. RE (again) + COGNOSCERE (to know). Recognition is knowing again — the moment when something familiar returns to awareness.
words.tip: RECOGNIT + ION. Think: recognize.
words.example: She deserves recognition for her work.

words.commentary: One C, two M's. RECOM + MEND. The word means to commend again, and the double M comes from the Latin root commendare.
words.tip: One C, two M's. Think: I recommend more M&Ms.
words.example: I highly recommend this restaurant.

words.commentary: From Latin recommendare, to commit to another's care. RE (again) + COMMENDARE (to entrust). A recommendation entrusts again — passing your trust to someone else for their consideration.
words.tip: Two M's. RECOMMEND + ATION.
words.example: I followed the doctor's recommendation.

words.commentary: From Latin reconciliare, to bring together again. RE (again) + CONCILIARE (to unite). Reconciliation unites again what was separated — peace rebuilt from the ruins of conflict.
words.tip: RECONCILI + ATION. Think: reconcile.
words.example: Reconciliation was achieved.

words.commentary: From Latin recreare, to create again. RE (again) + CREARE (to create). Recreation re-creates your energy — rest that rebuilds what work has spent.
words.tip: RECREAT + ION. Think: recreate yourself.
words.example: The park is used for recreation.

words.commentary: From Latin rectificare, to make right. RECTUS (right) + FACERE (to make). To rectify is to make right — correction that restores the proper state.
words.tip: Remember: RECT (right) + IFY (to make) - keep the 'rect' straight like a rectangle
words.example: The company promised to rectify the billing errors within 48 hours.

words.commentary: From Latin redundans, overflowing. RE (back) + UNDA (wave). Something redundant waves back over what's already been covered — excess that repeats unnecessarily.
words.tip: Remember UNDA (wave) - redundANT has ANT, not ENT ending
words.example: The editor removed several redundant paragraphs that repeated the same information.

words.commentary: ENCE ending. The word means to carry back, from Latin referre. The double E in the middle is what most people miss.
words.tip: REFER + ENCE. Don't drop the E.
words.example: Can you provide a reference for the job?

words.commentary: From Latin referendum, something to be referred. REFERRE (to carry back). A referendum carries a question back to the people — decision returned to those it most affects.
words.tip: REFER + ENDUM.
words.example: The referendum will decide the issue.

words.commentary: From Latin refrigerare, to make cool again. RE (again) + FRIGUS (cold). A refrigerator makes things cold again — artificial winter contained in a kitchen appliance.
words.tip: No D. REFRIGER + ATOR.
words.example: Put the milk in the refrigerator.

words.commentary: From Latin registrum, list. RE (back) + GERERE (to carry). Registration carries your information back into the official record.
words.tip: REGISTRAT + ION. Think: register.
words.example: Online registration opens tomorrow.

words.commentary: From Latin regulare, to direct. REGULA (rule). Regulation directs behavior through rules — order imposed from above.
words.tip: REGULAT + ION. Think: regulate.
words.example: New regulations took effect.

words.commentary: From Latin rehabilitare, to make fit again. RE (again) + HABILITARE (to make able). Rehabilitation makes able again — restoration of capacity that was lost to injury, illness, or circumstance.
words.tip: REHABILIT + ATION. Think: rehabilitate.
words.example: Rehabilitation took several months.

words.commentary: From Latin relatio, a bringing back. RE (back) + LATUS (carried). A relationship carries something back and forth between people — connection sustained through exchange.
words.tip: RELATION + SHIP.
words.example: They have a good working relationship.

words.commentary: ANT not ENT at the end. The word means bearing upon the matter at hand, from Latin relevare, to raise up.
words.tip: RELEV + ANT. Ends in ANT.
words.example: That information is not relevant.

words.tip: RELIGI + OUS. I before O.
words.example: They observed the religious holiday.

words.commentary: From Latin relinquere, to leave behind. RE (behind) + LINQUERE (to leave). To relinquish is to leave behind — surrender of what you once held.
words.tip: Think 'RE-LINQU-ISH' - leave behind with LINQU like 'linguist' but drop the 't'
words.example: The CEO was forced to relinquish control of the company after the scandal.

words.commentary: From Latin reluctari, to struggle against. RE (against) + LUCTARI (to wrestle). Reluctance wrestles against action — the mind fighting its own forward motion.
words.tip: Remember LUCTARI (to wrestle) - reluctance has 'luct' not 'lunct'
words.example: Despite her talent, she showed reluctance to perform in front of the large audience.

words.commentary: From Latin reluctari, to struggle against. RE (against) + LUCTARI (to struggle). Reluctance is an internal struggle — resistance within yourself.
words.tip: RELUCT + ANT. Ends in ANT.
words.example: She was reluctant to leave.

words.commentary: From French remarquer, to mark again. RE (again) + MARQUER (to mark). Something remarkable deserves a second mark — noteworthy enough to be flagged twice.
words.tip: REMARK + ABLE.
words.example: She made a remarkable recovery.

words.tip: RE + MEM + BER. MEMBER is inside it.
words.example: Do you remember where we parked?

words.commentary: From Latin reminisci, to remember. RE (again) + MENS (mind). To reminisce is to mind again — memory revisited with the warmth of distance.
words.tip: Remember: RE-MIN-ISCE. Think 'mind' in the middle, 'isce' ending like 'science'.
words.example: Grandparents love to reminisce about the good old days over Sunday dinner.

words.commentary: From Latin reminisci, to remember. RE (again) + a root related to MENS (mind). Reminiscence is the mind going back again — memory revisited with deliberate attention.
words.tip: REMINISC + ENCE. Two I's.
words.example: The song brought back reminiscences.

words.commentary: From Latin remordere, to bite again. RE (again) + MORDERE (to bite). Remorse bites again — guilt that returns to gnaw at the conscience.
words.tip: Remember: remorse bites again with -morse, like Morse code's repeated signals.
words.example: She felt deep remorse after realizing her harsh words had hurt her friend's feelings.

words.commentary: From French renaissance, rebirth. RE (again) + NASCERE (to be born). A renaissance is being born again — cultural revival that breathes new life into old forms.
words.tip: REN + AISS + ANCE. French: rebirth.
words.example: The Renaissance changed art forever.

words.commentary: From French rendez-vous, present yourself. The Z and S are both silent — almost every letter behaves unexpectedly.
words.tip: From French rendre (to render) + vous (you). Pronounced ron-day-VOO.
words.example: They arranged a rendezvous at the cafe.

words.commentary: From Latin renovare, to make new again. RE (again) + NOVUS (new). Renovation makes new again — restoring what time has worn by returning it to freshness.
words.tip: RENOVAT + ION. Think: renovate.
words.example: The renovation took six months.

words.commentary: From Latin repercussio, a rebounding. RE (back) + PERCUTERE (to strike). A repercussion strikes back — consequence that returns to the source of action.
words.tip: Remember PER like 'percussion' - it strikes back with consequences
words.example: The company didn't anticipate the negative repercussion of their controversial advertising campaign.

words.commentary: From Latin repetere, to seek again. RE (again) + PETERE (to seek). Repetition is seeking again — returning to the same action, word, or thought until it becomes embedded.
words.tip: REPETI + TION. Think: REPEAT becomes REPETI.
words.example: Repetition is the key to learning.

words.commentary: From Latin repositorium, storehouse. RE plus POSIT plus ORY.
words.tip: From Latin repositorium, storehouse.
words.example: The code was stored in a public repository.

words.commentary: From Latin reprehensibilis, blameworthy. RE (back) + PREHENDERE (to seize). Something reprehensible seizes back your respect — behavior that grabs and reverses good opinion.
words.tip: Think 'reprehensIBLE' - if behavior is blameworthy, it's 'I-B-L-E' not 'A-B-L-E'
words.example: The politician's reprehensible lies about the charity funds shocked even his supporters.

words.commentary: From Latin repraesentare, to make present again. RE (again) + PRAESENTARE (to present). Representation makes something present again — standing in for what cannot be there directly.
words.tip: REPRESENTAT + ION. Think: represent.
words.example: Legal representation is important.

words.commentary: From Latin repraesentare, to make present again. RE (again) + PRAESENTARE (to present). A representative makes you present where you cannot be.
words.tip: Remember: RE-present-ative. You RE-present someone, making them present again.
words.example: The sales representative explained the new product features to potential customers.

words.commentary: From RE (again) + PRODUCTION. Reproduction is producing again — whether biology creating new life or technology creating copies of existing work.
words.tip: Remember: RE-PRODUCTION keeps the full word PRODUCTION intact, no letters dropped
words.example: The museum's reproduction of the ancient manuscript was incredibly detailed.

words.commentary: From Latin reputare, to think over. RE (again) + PUTARE (to think). Reputation is what others think over about you — your character calculated by collective opinion.
words.tip: REPUTAT + ION. Think: repute.
words.example: She has an excellent reputation.

words.commentary: From Latin requirere, to seek again. RE (again) + QUAERERE (to seek). A requirement seeks something it won't stop asking for — demand that persists until met.
words.tip: REQUIRE + MENT. Keep the E.
words.example: Meeting the requirement is mandatory.

words.tip: RE + SEARCH. Search again.
words.example: More research is needed.

words.commentary: From French ressembler, to seem like again. RE (again) + SEMBLER (to seem). A resemblance is seeming like something else — similarity that makes you look twice.
words.tip: Remember SEMBLE (seem) in the middle - you resemble when you seem alike
words.example: The family resemblance between the twins was so strong that even their teachers confused them.

words.commentary: From French reserver, to keep back. A reservoir keeps water back — it stores what would otherwise flow away. The OIR ending is characteristically French.
words.tip: RESERV + OIR. French ending.
words.example: The reservoir supplies water to the city.

words.commentary: From Latin resignare, to unseal or cancel. RE (back) + SIGNARE (to sign). Resignation is signing back — returning the authority you were given, whether from a position or from hope.
words.tip: RESIGNAT + ION. Think: resign.
words.example: She handed in her resignation.

words.commentary: From Latin resilire, to spring back. RE (back) + SALIRE (to jump). Someone resilient springs back — recovery that returns to original form after being compressed.
words.tip: Remember: reSILient springs back like a SILly ball - keep the 'i' before 'ent'
words.example: The oak tree proved remarkably resilient, standing tall despite decades of harsh storms.

words.commentary: ANCE ending. From Latin resistere, to stand back. Resistance is the act of standing firm against pressure.
words.tip: RESIST + ANCE. Ends in ANCE.
words.example: There was strong resistance to the plan.

words.commentary: From Latin resolutus, loosened. RE (intensely) + SOLVERE (to loosen). Someone resolute has intensely loosened doubt — determination freed from hesitation.
words.tip: Think 'resolve' + 'ute' ending - one S, one L, silent E
words.example: Despite the setbacks, she remained resolute in her commitment to finish the project.

words.commentary: From Latin resolvere, to loosen again. RE (again) + SOLVERE (to loosen). A resolution loosens what was tangled — problems untied or commitments set firmly in place.
words.tip: RESOLUT + ION. Think: resolve.
words.example: Her New Year's resolution was to exercise more.

words.tip: RE + SOURCE.
words.example: Water is a precious resource.

words.commentary: From Latin respirare, to breathe again. RE (again) + SPIRARE (to breathe). Every breath is a re-breathing — an inhalation that follows an exhalation in endless cycle.
words.tip: RESPIRAT + ORY. Think: respire.
words.example: She has a respiratory infection.

words.commentary: From Latin respondere, to answer. RE (back) + SPONDERE (to pledge). Responsibility is pledging back — answering for your actions with the weight of personal accountability.
words.tip: RESPONSIBIL + ITY. I not A.
words.example: It's my responsibility to finish this.

words.commentary: From French restaurant, restoring. RESTAURER (to restore). An establishment that restores you with food. The 'au' combination and silent letters make this one of the most commonly misspelled words globally.
words.tip: REST + AURANT. AU in the middle.
words.example: We ate at an Italian restaurant.

words.commentary: From Latin restitutio, a restoring. RE (again) + STATUERE (to set up). Restitution sets things up again — restoration that returns what was taken.
words.tip: Remember STATUTE in the middle - restitution restores by statute
words.example: The court ordered full restitution to compensate the victims for their losses.

words.commentary: From Latin restaurare, to rebuild. RE (again) + STAURARE (to set up). Restoration sets up again — returning something to its original condition through careful reconstruction.
words.tip: RESTORAT + ION. Think: restore.
words.example: The restoration of the painting took years.

words.commentary: From Latin restringere, to draw back tightly. RE (back) + STRINGERE (to bind). A restriction binds back — freedom limited by rules that pull behavior within defined boundaries.
words.tip: Remember the C-T pattern: restriCTion (like consTRiCTion - both squeeze tight)
words.example: The new parking restriction limits visitors to two hours maximum.

words.commentary: From Latin resurrectio, a rising again. RE (again) + SURGERE (to rise). Resurrection is rising again — return from death or dormancy, life reclaimed from what seemed final.
words.tip: Double R like RE-rising, single S like SURGE upward again.
words.example: The garden showed signs of resurrection after the long winter drought ended.

words.commentary: From Latin resuscitare, to revive. RE (again) + SUSCITARE (to raise). Raising someone back to life. The 'sci' in the middle pronounced as 'si' misleads spellers.
words.tip: Remember: RE-SUS-CI-TA-TION. Think 'SUS' then 'CI' like 'see', not 'si'.
words.example: The paramedics performed cardiopulmonary resuscitation to save the drowning victim's life.

words.commentary: From Latin reticens, keeping silent. RE (intensely) + TACERE (to be silent). Someone reticent keeps intensely silent — reserve that speaks through what it withholds.
words.tip: Remember TACERE (silent): reTICEnt keeps the 'tice' from being silent about silence.
words.example: The usually talkative professor became reticent when asked about the controversial research findings.

words.commentary: From Latin revelare, to unveil. RE (back) + VELARE (to veil). A revelation pulls back the veil — hidden truth exposed by removing what concealed it.
words.tip: REVELAT + ION. Think: reveal.
words.example: The revelation shocked everyone.

words.commentary: From Latin reverentia, awe. RE (back) + VERERI (to fear). Reverence is awe that steps back — respect so deep it creates distance.
words.tip: Remember: revere + ence = reverence. The E before NCE stays.
words.example: The students showed deep reverence for their elderly professor's wisdom.

words.commentary: From Latin revolutio, a turning back. RE (back) + VOLVERE (to roll). A revolution is a complete rolling — society turned over, the bottom rising to replace the top.
words.tip: REVOLUT + ION. Think: revolve.
words.example: The revolution changed the country.

words.commentary: From Greek rhetorike, the art of speaking. RHETOR (speaker). Rhetoric is the speaker's art — language crafted to persuade, move, and convince.
words.tip: Remember the H: RHETO-RIC, like the Greek RHETOR (speaker) who masters this art
words.example: The politician's fiery rhetoric stirred the crowd, but critics questioned whether substance matched style.

words.commentary: From Greek rhetorikos, of an orator. RHETOR (speaker). Something rhetorical belongs to the art of speaking — language deployed for persuasion rather than information.
words.tip: Remember the Greek RHETOR (speaker) - it's rhetOR-ical, not rhetoric-le
words.example: The politician's rhetorical flourishes impressed the crowd but revealed little substance.

words.commentary: From Greek rheuma, flowing. RHEIN (to flow). Ancient Greeks believed the disease was caused by flowing humors in the body. The 'rheu' beginning is the main spelling trap.
words.tip: RHEUM + ATISM. Silent H.
words.example: She suffers from rheumatism.

words.commentary: From Greek rheuma (flowing) + eidos (form). Rheumatoid arthritis — disease that resembles the flowing humor condition. Same 'rheu' trap as rheumatism.
words.tip: Think 'rheu-MAT-oid' - the flowing (rheu) condition takes the form (oid) of a mat
words.example: Her rheumatoid arthritis symptoms improved significantly after starting the new medication.

words.commentary: From Greek rhythmos, measured flow. No vowel between the consonant clusters — just r-h-y-t-h-m. One of the longest common English words without a standard vowel (a, e, i, o, u).
words.tip: Rhythm Has Your Two Hips Moving.
words.example: The drummer kept a steady rhythm.

words.commentary: Named after Colonel Joe Rickey, a lobbyist who popularized the drink in 1880s Washington DC. RICK-EY. Not 'ricky' or 'rickie'. The -ey ending, like a proper name.
words.tip: Colonel Rickey's drink: -ey ending like a surname, not -ie like a nickname.
words.example: The bartender served a refreshing gin rickey with fresh lime and soda water.

words.commentary: From Latin ridiculosus, laughable. RIDERE (to laugh). Something ridiculous invites laughter — absurdity that refuses to take itself seriously.
words.tip: RIDICUL + OUS. I not E.
words.example: The idea was absolutely ridiculous.

words.commentary: From the German grape variety, possibly named after the Rhine region. RIES-LING. The 'ie' follows the German pattern (opposite of English 'i before e'). Not 'reisling' — the i comes first.
words.tip: Remember the German rule: 'ie' not 'ei' - Rhine wine has 'ie'
words.example: The crisp Riesling paired perfectly with the spicy Asian cuisine.

words.commentary: From Italian rigato, lined or ridged. RIGARE (to draw lines). Tube pasta with ridges on the outside. One g, one t — straightforward compared to its pasta cousins.
words.tip: Think 'rigid lines' - one G for ridged pasta tubes.
words.example: The rigatoni held the chunky tomato sauce perfectly in its ridged tubes.

words.commentary: From Latin rigorosus, stiff. RIGOR (stiffness). Something rigorous is stiff with standards — thoroughness that bends for nothing.
words.tip: Remember RIGOR + OUS: the stiff Latin root needs no extra letters
words.example: The scientist followed rigorous protocols to ensure accurate results.

words.commentary: From Latin rudimentum, a first attempt. RUDIS (rough). Something rudimentary is rough and basic — the first sketch before refinement begins.
words.tip: Remember RUDE + MENT + ARY: rough beginnings need mental preparation
words.example: The students learned rudimentary Spanish before their trip to Mexico.

words.commentary: From French sabot, a wooden shoe. Workers allegedly threw their wooden shoes into machinery to disrupt production — resistance expressed through deliberate destruction.
words.tip: Remember the French wooden shoe 'sabot' - SABOTage workers throwing shoes into machines
words.example: The cyber attack was an act of sabotage designed to cripple the power grid.

words.commentary: From Latin sacrificium, sacred offering. SACER (sacred) + FACERE (to make). A sacrifice makes something sacred — the ordinary transformed through the act of giving up.
words.tip: SACRI + FICE. Think: sacred.
words.example: He made a great sacrifice.

words.commentary: From Arabic safara, to travel — via Swahili safari, journey. Entered English through the era of African expeditions in the 19th century.
words.tip: From Arabic safara, to travel. Single F, ends in I.
words.example: They booked a safari in the Serengeti.

words.commentary: From Latin sagax, of keen perception. SAGIRE (to perceive keenly). Someone sagacious perceives keenly — wisdom sharpened by exceptional insight.
words.tip: Remember SAGE wisdom: sag-A-cious keeps the 'a' from Latin sagax
words.example: The sagacious judge quickly identified the key inconsistency in the witness testimony.
