words.commentary: From Japanese sake, meaning alcoholic beverage generally. SA-KE. Two syllables in Japanese (sah-keh), though English speakers often say 'sah-kee'. Not 'saki' — that is a different word (a type of monkey).
words.tip: Remember: sake ends in -e, not -i (saki is a monkey!)
words.example: We warmed the sake in a traditional ceramic flask before serving.

words.commentary: From Latin sambucus, elderberry. SAM-BU-CA. An Italian anise-flavored liqueur. Not 'sambuka' or 'sambucca'. One c, and the ending is -ca not -ka.
words.tip: Remember Latin sambucus: one 'c' and Italian '-ca' ending, not Germanic '-ka'.
words.example: The bartender set the sambuca on fire before serving it with three coffee beans.

words.commentary: From Japanese saburai, one who serves — from the verb saburau, to serve a master. Three syllables, not two.
words.tip: From Japanese saburai, to serve. Three syllables: SAM-u-rye.
words.example: The samurai code valued honour above all else.

words.commentary: From Latin sanctimonia, holiness. SANCTUS (holy). Someone sanctimonious performs holiness — piety displayed for others rather than practiced in private.
words.tip: Remember SANCTI-MONIOUS: holy (sancti) + testimony (monious) - performing piety for show
words.example: His sanctimonious lecture about charity rang hollow given his own selfish behavior.

words.commentary: From Latin sanctuarium, a holy place. SANCTUS (holy). A sanctuary is a holy place — space set apart from the ordinary, offering safety through sacredness.
words.tip: SANCTU + ARY. Think: sacred place.
words.example: The wildlife sanctuary protects endangered species.

words.commentary: Named after the Earl of Sandwich, who allegedly asked for meat between bread so he could keep playing cards. The D before W is preserved from the place name.
words.tip: SAND + WICH. Named after the Earl of Sandwich.
words.example: I had a turkey sandwich for lunch.

words.commentary: From Spanish sangria, bleeding, from sangre (blood) — named for its red color. SAN-GRI-A. Not 'sangrea' or 'sangera'. The g and r stay together.
words.tip: Think 'blood' in Spanish (sangre) + ia ending - keep the gr together
words.example: We served traditional red sangria with fresh fruit at the summer party.

words.commentary: From Latin sanitas, health. SANUS (healthy). Sanitation maintains health — the systems and practices that keep communities clean enough to survive together.
words.tip: Remember SANE + -ITATION: sane people create sanitation systems for health.
words.example: The city invested millions in improving sanitation infrastructure to prevent disease outbreaks.

words.commentary: From Greek sarkophagos, flesh eating — named for the limestone used, which was believed to decompose bodies quickly. SAR + CO + PHA + GUS.
words.tip: From Greek sarx (flesh) + phagein (to eat). PH makes F sound.
words.example: The archaeologists discovered an intact sarcophagus.

words.commentary: From Japanese sashi (pierced) + mi (body/flesh). Sliced raw fish. Four syllables: sa-SHEE-mee. Not 'sashami' or 'sahshimi'.
words.tip: Remember: saSHImi has 'shi' like 'she' - pierced flesh in Japanese.
words.example: The chef prepared fresh tuna sashimi with wasabi and pickled ginger.

words.commentary: From Latin satelles, an attendant. A satellite attends a larger body — whether a moon orbiting a planet or a device orbiting the earth, both following a greater gravitational pull.
words.tip: One T, two L's. SAT + ELL + ITE.
words.example: The satellite orbits the Earth.

words.commentary: From Latin satisfacere, to do enough. SATIS (enough) + FACERE (to do). Satisfaction is doing enough — the feeling that arrives when effort meets its sufficient conclusion.
words.tip: SATISFACT + ION. Think: satisfy.
words.example: Customer satisfaction is our priority.

words.commentary: From Latin satisfacere, to do enough. SATIS (enough) + FACERE (to do). Satisfactory means enough has been done — not exceptional, but sufficient.
words.tip: SATISFACT + ORY. Think: satisfy.
words.example: The results were satisfactory.

words.commentary: From German sauer (sour) + Kraut (cabbage). Fermented cabbage. The au makes an 'ow' sound in German. Not 'sourkrout' or 'saurkraut'. Keep the e in sauer.
words.tip: Remember: sauer (sour) + kraut (cabbage) - keep the 'e' in German 'sauer'
words.example: The traditional German sauerkraut paired perfectly with the bratwurst at the Oktoberfest celebration.

words.commentary: From Sauternes, a commune in Bordeaux. SAU-TERNES. A sweet French wine. Not 'sauterne' (though this variant exists) or 'soterns'. The 'au' and the final 's' matter.
words.tip: Remember the French town: SAU-TERNES with 'au' like 'auto' and keep the final 's'
words.example: The dessert wine paired beautifully with the foie gras, a classic Sauternes from Château d'Yquem.

words.commentary: From French sauvage, wild. SAUVIGNON. The French 'au' and 'ignon' combinations are foreign to English spellers. Not 'savignon' or 'sauvingon'. The au is crucial.
words.tip: Remember 'savage' (sauvage) wine: SAUVignon with AU like 'sausage'
words.example: The sommelier recommended a crisp Sauvignon Blanc from New Zealand.

words.commentary: From Latin scalpellum, small knife. SCALPERE (to cut, scrape). A surgeon's cutting tool. The 'sc' beginning where both letters are pronounced is unusual.
words.tip: Remember: SCALP + EL - think of cutting the scalp with this surgical tool.
words.example: The surgeon carefully made the first incision with a sharp scalpel.

words.commentary: From Greek skandalon, a snare or stumbling block. A scandal is something that trips people up — a moral stumbling block that catches public attention.
words.tip: SCAND + AL. Ends in AL.
words.example: The political scandal dominated the news.

words.commentary: From Italian scenario, a sketch of a play. In Italian SC before E or I makes a SH sound — scenario is often mispronounced as well as misspelled.
words.tip: From Italian scena, scene. SC makes a SH sound in Italian.
words.example: In the worst case scenario, the project would be delayed by a month.

words.commentary: British sceptic preserves the Greek skeptikos more faithfully, while American skeptic swapped the C for K. Both spellings have coexisted since the 16th century.
words.tip: US: skeptic (SK). UK/AU/NZ: sceptic (SC).
words.example: He remains a sceptic about climate predictions.

words.commentary: From German Schaden, harm and Freude, joy. Pleasure derived from others misfortune — English borrowed it because English had no word for the concept.
words.tip: From German Schaden (harm) + Freude (joy). Pronounced SHA-den-froy-duh.
words.example: She felt a twinge of schadenfreude when her rival missed the flight.

words.commentary: Pronounced differently in American (SKED) and British (SHED) English, but spelled the same either way. The SCH comes from Greek through Latin.
words.tip: SCHED + ULE. SCH at the start.
words.example: Check the train schedule.

words.commentary: From Greek schizein (to split) + phren (mind). A split mind — though the disease is not split personality. The 'sch' as 'sk' and 'phr' as 'fr' are both Greek patterns.
words.tip: Remember: SCH sounds like 'sk', PHR sounds like 'fr' - both Greek patterns
words.example: The psychiatrist explained that schizophrenia involves hallucinations and delusions, not multiple personalities.

words.commentary: From German Schnaps, a gulp. SCHNAPPS. Double p, ends in s. The 'sch' opening is German. Not 'shnaps' or 'schnaps'. Two p's seal the deal.
words.tip: Remember the German origin: SCH-napps with double P like 'snapping' twice
words.example: After dinner, grandfather poured everyone a small glass of peach schnapps.

words.commentary: From German Schnitzel, a cutlet, from schnitzen (to carve). Breaded and fried meat. The sch makes a 'sh' sound in German. The tz makes a 'ts' sound.
words.tip: Remember German 'sch' makes 'sh' sound, and 'tz' makes 'ts' - like carved meat cuts
words.example: The restaurant's Wiener schnitzel was perfectly golden and crispy.

words.commentary: From SCHOLAR + SHIP (state of being). Scholarship is the state of being a scholar — both the pursuit of knowledge and the financial support that makes pursuit possible.
words.tip: SCHOLAR + SHIP.
words.example: She won a full scholarship.

words.commentary: From Latin sciaticus, relating to the hip. ISCHION (hip joint). Pain along the sciatic nerve. The 'sci' beginning pronounced as 'sy' is counterintuitive.
words.tip: Remember: SCIence becomes SCI-atica, despite the 'sy' sound pronunciation
words.example: The doctor explained that her lower back pain was actually sciatica radiating down her leg.

words.commentary: From Latin cisorium, cutting instrument. The sc- beginning looks wrong but is correct. Medieval scribes added the c to reflect a false Latin etymology. The word has survived with its misleading spelling ever since.
words.tip: SC + ISSORS. SC at the start.
words.example: Pass me the scissors.

words.commentary: From Greek skoliosis, crookedness. SKOLIOS (crooked). Abnormal curvature of the spine. The 'oli' middle section is often misspelled 'olio' or 'olo'.
words.tip: Remember 'scoliosis' has 'oli' in the middle, like 'olive' - spine curved like an olive branch
words.example: The doctor diagnosed her with mild scoliosis during her teenage growth spurt.

words.commentary: From screen plus shot. A photograph of what is on screen.
words.tip: From screen plus shot.
words.example: She took a screenshot of the error message.

words.commentary: From Latin sculpere, to carve. A sculpture is carved from raw material — the art of removing what doesn't belong to reveal the form within.
words.tip: SCULPT + URE.
words.example: The sculpture was carved from marble.

words.commentary: From Latin secretarius, one entrusted with secrets. SECRETUM (secret). A secretary was originally a keeper of secrets — the most trusted person in the room.
words.tip: SECRET + ARY. A secret in secretary.
words.example: The secretary scheduled the meeting.

words.tip: SECUR + ITY. Think: secure.
words.example: Airport security has been increased.

words.tip: SEM + ESTER. Think: six months.
words.example: The semester begins in September.

words.commentary: From semi plus conductor. SEMI plus CONDUCTOR.
words.tip: From semi plus conductor.
words.example: Silicon is the most common semiconductor material.

words.tip: SENSAT + ION. Think: sense.
words.example: The song became an overnight sensation.

words.tip: SENSIT + IVE. I not A.
words.example: He is sensitive about his height.

words.commentary: PAR in the middle, not PER. There's A RAT in separate — that mnemonic has saved millions of students on spelling tests.
words.tip: There's A RAT in sepARAte.
words.example: Please separate the whites from the colors.

words.tip: SEPARAT + ION. A not E. Think: separate.
words.example: The separation was amicable.

words.commentary: The EAR in the middle makes no phonetic sense, which is why this military rank is so hard to spell. The spelling preserves an Old French form that English pronunciation has abandoned.
words.tip: SER + GEANT. The G before E is unusual.
words.example: The sergeant led the training exercise.

words.commentary: From Turkish cevirme, turning. Meat cooked on a vertical rotisserie. Not 'schwarma' or 'shwarma'. The a after the sh matters.
words.tip: Remember the 'a' after 'sh' - it's sha-WAR-ma, like 'war' in the middle
words.example: The fragrant lamb shawarma was carved fresh from the rotating spit at the Mediterranean restaurant.

words.commentary: Named after the city of Shiraz in Iran, though the connection to the grape is debated. SHI-RAZ. Same grape as Syrah in France. Not 'sheraz' or 'shiras'. The i is short, the z is firm.
words.tip: Think of the Iranian city Shiraz: SHI-RAZ with short 'i' and hard 'z'.
words.example: The Australian shiraz had bold, peppery flavors that paired perfectly with the grilled lamb.

words.commentary: IE not EI, because there's no C before it. The word means to sit upon, from Latin sedere. Medieval sieges involved sitting outside castle walls and waiting.
words.tip: Remember: I before E in siege - think 'I sit' outside the castle walls
words.example: The medieval castle withstood a three-month siege before finally surrendering.

words.commentary: From Spanish siesta, itself from Latin sexta hora, the sixth hour — noon, when Romans rested. The tradition predates the word.
words.tip: From Spanish sexta hora, the sixth hour — midday rest.
words.example: The whole town shut down for the afternoon siesta.

words.commentary: From Latin signatura, a marking. SIGNARE (to mark). Your signature is your mark — the written symbol that represents your identity and consent.
words.tip: SIGN + ATURE. Think: sign.
words.example: Please add your signature here.

words.commentary: From Latin significare, to make signs. SIGNUM (sign) + FACERE (to make). Significance is what makes signs — meaning that signals its own importance through observable effect.
words.tip: SIGNIFIC + ANCE. Ends in ANCE.
words.example: The significance of the discovery was immense.

words.commentary: From Latin significare, to indicate. SIGNUM (sign) + FACERE (to make). Something significant makes a sign — importance that signals itself.
words.tip: Remember: significant makes a SIGN - keep the 'i' after 'sign' and end with 'ant'
words.example: The discovery of water on Mars represents a significant breakthrough in space exploration.

words.commentary: Named after Etienne de Silhouette, a French finance minister known for his shadow-portrait hobby. The word preserves his French surname exactly.
words.tip: Named after Etienne de Silhouette. SILHOU + ETTE.
words.example: She could see his silhouette in the doorway.

words.commentary: AR ending, not ER. From Latin similis, meaning like or resembling. The word shares its root with simulate and simultaneous.
words.tip: SIMIL + AR. Ends in AR, not ER.
words.example: The two paintings look very similar.

words.commentary: From Latin simul, at the same time. Something simultaneous happens at the same time as something else — parallel events sharing a moment.
words.tip: SIMULTANE + OUS. Think: same time.
words.example: The events were simultaneous.

words.commentary: CERE in the middle. From Latin sincerus, meaning clean or pure. Despite the folk etymology about wax-free marble, the true origin simply means genuinely pure.
words.tip: SINCERE + LY. Keep the E.
words.example: Yours sincerely, John.

words.tip: SITUAT + ION. Think: situate.
words.example: The situation is under control.

words.tip: SKEL + ETON. One L.
words.example: The skeleton was found in the cave.

words.commentary: US spelling starts with SK; British spelling (sceptic) starts with SC. Both come from Greek skeptikos, meaning inquiring or reflective.
words.tip: Think 'SK' for skeptical Americans, 'SC' for sceptical Brits - both from Greek skeptikos.
words.example: The skeptic demanded more evidence before accepting the extraordinary claim.

words.commentary: From soft plus ware. Contrasted with hardware. Coined in 1953.
words.tip: From soft plus ware.
words.example: The software update fixed several security vulnerabilities.

words.commentary: From Korean soju, burned liquor. SO-JU. Korea's most popular spirit, made from rice or other starches. Not 'sojou' or 'sogu'. Four letters, as clean as the drink.
words.tip: Korean soju: just SO-JU, four letters like the simple rice spirit itself.
words.example: After dinner, we shared a bottle of soju and sang karaoke until midnight.

words.tip: SOL + EMN. Silent N.
words.example: It was a solemn occasion.

words.commentary: From French sommelier, originally a pack animal driver who later became a wine steward. SOM-ME-LIER. Double m, and the 'ier' ending. Not 'somelier' or 'sommalier'. One of the most misspelled wine terms.
words.tip: Remember the French pack driver: double M for carrying wine loads.
words.example: The sommelier recommended a bold Cabernet to pair with our steak dinner.

words.commentary: From Greek sophistikos, of the Sophists. SOPHOS (wise). Sophistication was originally a negative term — the Sophists were accused of using wisdom for deception. The word now means refined complexity.
words.tip: SOPHISTIC + ATED. PH not F.
words.example: The design was elegant and sophisticated.

words.commentary: From French souffler, to blow or puff. SUFFLARE (Latin, to blow). Double f, the lé ending with an accent. A dish that puffs up with air — and deflates if you spell it wrong.
words.tip: Double F like French 'souffler' (to puff), plus accented é ending.
words.example: The chocolate soufflé collapsed when she opened the oven door too early.

words.commentary: From French souvenir, to remember. A souvenir is a memory object — a physical thing that carries an experience forward in time.
words.tip: SOUVEN + IR. French: to remember.
words.example: She bought a souvenir from the trip.

words.tip: SOVER + EIGN. EI not IE.
words.example: The sovereign ruled for decades.

words.commentary: From Old French soverain, supreme. Latin SUPERANUS (above). Sovereignty is being above — supreme authority that answers to no higher power.
words.tip: Remember: sovereign + ty. Think 'reign' - a sovereign reigns supreme.
words.example: The treaty recognized the nation's sovereignty over its territorial waters.

words.commentary: From the practice of speaking quietly (easy) about hidden bars during Prohibition. SPEAK-EASY. Two common words joined. Not 'speakesey' or 'speakeasie'. The 'easy' part is literally easy.
words.tip: Remember: speak + easy = speakeasy. Two simple words, no hyphen needed.
words.example: The jazz band played at a hidden speakeasy behind the bookstore.

words.tip: SPEC + IFIC. C not S.
words.example: Be more specific in your request.

words.commentary: From Latin specificare, to mention particularly. SPECIES (kind) + FACERE (to make). A specification makes something specific — requirements defined clearly enough to build from.
words.tip: SPECIFIC + ATION.
words.example: The specifications are in the manual.

words.commentary: From Latin specimen, a mark or example. SPECERE (to look). A specimen is something worth looking at — a sample that represents the whole.
words.tip: SPECIM + EN. Ends in EN.
words.example: The lab examined the specimen.

words.commentary: From Latin spectaculum, a show. SPECTARE (to watch). Something spectacular is worth watching — a performance that commands every eye in the room.
words.tip: SPECTACUL + AR. Think: spectacle.
words.example: The fireworks were spectacular.

words.commentary: Double E, not double E-A. The word comes from Old English spraec, and the modern spelling standardized in the early modern period.
words.tip: Double E. SP + EECH.
words.example: She delivered an inspiring speech.

words.commentary: From Greek sphygmos (pulse) + manometer (pressure gauge). The blood pressure cuff. Arguably the hardest common medical instrument to spell — 18 letters of pure Greek.
words.tip: Remember: sphygmo (pulse) + mano (thin) + meter - two m's, one in each part
words.example: The nurse wrapped the sphygmomanometer cuff around the patient's arm to check her blood pressure.

words.tip: SPIRIT + UAL.
words.example: It was a deeply spiritual experience.

words.commentary: From Latin spontaneus, of free will. SPONTE (of one's own accord). Something spontaneous arises of its own accord — action without external cause or prior planning.
words.tip: SPONTANE + OUS. Think: spontaneity.
words.example: The trip was completely spontaneous.

words.commentary: From spread plus sheet. Data spread across a sheet.
words.tip: From spread plus sheet.
words.example: She tracked expenses in a spreadsheet.

words.commentary: From Si Racha, a city in Thailand. Three syllables: SRI plus RA plus CHA.
words.tip: From Si Racha, a city in Thailand.
words.example: He added sriracha to the noodles.

words.commentary: From Greek staphyle (bunch of grapes) + kokkos (grain, seed). The bacteria cluster like grapes under a microscope. The 'ph' as 'f' and double-c create spelling challenges.
words.tip: Remember: staPHyle (grapes) + koKKos (grain) = staphylocoKKus with PH and double-C
words.example: The laboratory confirmed that the wound infection was caused by Staphylococcus aureus bacteria.

words.commentary: Stationary means not moving. Stationery is paper supplies.
words.tip: Stationary means not moving.
words.example: The car remained stationary at the lights.

words.commentary: STATION + ERY. The E distinguishes writing materials from the adjective stationary (standing still). Remember: E for envelope, A for standing still.
words.tip: STATION + ERY. E for envelope. A for standing still (stationary).
words.example: She bought new stationery for school.

words.commentary: From German Statistik, from Latin status (state). Statistics originally described data about the state — governance measured in numbers.
words.tip: STATIST + ICS. Think: state.
words.example: The statistics show a clear trend.

words.commentary: From Latin statutum, set up. STAT plus UTE. Not statue.
words.tip: From Latin statutum, set up.
words.example: The statute of limitations had expired.

words.commentary: From Greek stereos (solid) + typos (impression). A stereotype is a solid impression — an image so fixed it becomes rigid, losing the flexibility that truth requires.
words.tip: Remember: STEREO-TYPE (like stereo sound + type of impression)
words.example: The movie challenged the stereotype that all teenagers are irresponsible.

words.commentary: From Greek stethos (chest) + skopein (to examine). An instrument for examining the chest. The 'th' after 'ste' is the stumbling point — people write 'stethascope' or drop the first t.
words.tip: Remember Greek 'stethos' (chest) - keep the 'th' after 'ste' to examine correctly.
words.example: The doctor used her stethoscope to listen to the patient's heartbeat and breathing.

words.commentary: From Latin stimulare, to goad. STIMULUS (goad). To stimulate is to prod into action — the mind or body poked awake by external force.
words.tip: Remember the Latin root: STIMUL-ate, like a stimulus that prods you forward.
words.example: The teacher used interactive games to stimulate student interest in mathematics.

words.commentary: From Latin stimulare, to goad. STIMULUS (a pointed stick). Stimulation is being prodded — sensation that prods the mind or body into heightened response.
words.tip: Remember STIMULUS + ATION: the pointed stick (stimulus) gets the action ending.
words.example: The bright colors provided visual stimulation for the developing infant's brain.

words.tip: STIMUL + US. Ends in US.
words.example: The stimulus package boosted the economy.

words.tip: STOM + ACH. CH makes a K sound.
words.example: I have a stomach ache.

words.commentary: From Middle English stout, bold or strong. STOUT. Simple spelling but people occasionally write 'stowt'. The 'ou' makes the 'ow' sound — straightforward English.
words.tip: Think 'shout' but with 't' - the 'ou' makes the 'ow' sound.
words.example: The stout wooden door withstood the storm's fierce winds.

words.commentary: From STRAIGHT + FORWARD. Straightforward means moving directly ahead — no curves, no detours, no hidden paths.
words.tip: STRAIGHT + FORWARD.
words.example: The answer is straightforward.

words.tip: STRAT + EGY. E not A.
words.example: We need a better marketing strategy.

words.commentary: ENGTH without any extra vowels. The word packs six consonants around a single vowel — a remarkable consonant density for English.
words.tip: STRENG + TH. The G comes before TH.
words.example: He admired her inner strength.

words.commentary: STRENGTH + EN. The EN suffix turns the noun into a verb. To strengthen is to add strength — making something more capable of bearing weight.
words.tip: STRENGTH + EN.
words.example: Exercise can strengthen your bones.

words.commentary: From Greek streptos (twisted) + kokkos (grain). Bacteria that form twisted chains. Similar spelling pattern to staphylococcus — both end in -coccus.
words.tip: Remember: strepto (twisted) + coccus (grain) - double 'c' in middle, single at end
words.example: The throat culture tested positive for streptococcus bacteria, confirming the patient had strep throat.

words.tip: STRUCT + URE. Think: construct.
words.example: The structure was built in 1920.

words.tip: Two B's. STUBB + ORN.
words.example: He is too stubborn to change his mind.

words.commentary: From Latin sub (under) + cutis (skin). Under the skin — where certain injections are administered. The 'cut' in the middle literally means skin.
words.tip: Remember: sub-CUT-aneous - 'cut' means skin in Latin, so it's under the skin
words.example: The nurse administered the insulin via subcutaneous injection into the patient's thigh.

words.commentary: From Latin subordinare, to place in a lower order. SUB (under) + ORDINARE (to arrange). A subordinate is arranged under — positioned below in a hierarchy of authority.
words.tip: Remember: SUB-ORD-IN-ATE. Think 'in order' - the middle has ORD-IN like 'ordering'.
words.example: The manager assigned the project to her subordinate while she focused on strategic planning.

words.commentary: From Latin sub poena, under penalty. The OE makes an EE sound.
words.tip: From Latin sub poena, under penalty.
words.example: She received a subpoena to testify before the grand jury.

words.commentary: From Latin subscribere, to write beneath. SUB (under) + SCRIBERE (to write). A subscription is writing your name beneath — signing up by adding yourself to a list.
words.tip: SUBSCRIP + TION. Think: subscribe.
words.example: The magazine subscription costs twenty dollars.

words.commentary: From Latin subsequi, to follow closely. SUB (under) + SEQUI (to follow). Subsequent events follow under the shadow of what came before.
words.tip: SUB-SE-QUENT: remember SEQUI (to follow) - it's QU like 'queen' then ENT
words.example: The subsequent investigation revealed new evidence that changed the entire case.

words.commentary: From Latin substantialis, of substance. SUB (under) + STARE (to stand). Something substantial has substance standing under it — real weight and presence, not mere appearance.
words.tip: SUBSTANTI + AL. Think: substance.
words.example: The meal was substantial.

words.tip: SUBSTIT + UTE. Think: instead of.
words.example: There is no substitute for hard work.

words.tip: SUB + URB. Under the city.
words.example: They moved to the suburbs.

words.commentary: Two C's, two S's. SUCCESS + FUL. The word builds on success, which already has its own doubling challenges.
words.tip: Two C's, two S's, one L. SUCCESS + FUL.
words.example: The event was extremely successful.

words.commentary: From Latin succedere, to go under or follow after. SUB (under) + CEDERE (to go). A successor follows after — stepping into the place another has left.
words.tip: Two C's, two S's. SUCCESS + OR.
words.example: The successor was appointed immediately.

words.commentary: From Latin sufficere, to be enough. SUB (under) + FACERE (to make). Sufficiency is making enough from below — adequacy achieved from available resources.
words.tip: Remember: SUFFICIENT + CY ending. Double F, single C, then -IENCY.
words.example: The farm's water supply demonstrated remarkable sufficiency during the drought season.

words.commentary: From Latin sufficere, to put under. SUB (under) + FACERE (to make). Sufficient means enough has been made — the foundation is adequate.
words.tip: Two F's. SUFFICI + ENT.
words.example: Is the evidence sufficient?

words.tip: Two G's. SUGGEST + ION.
words.example: Thank you for your suggestion.

words.commentary: From Latin superintendere, to oversee. SUPER (over) + INTENDERE (to direct attention). A superintendent directs attention from above — oversight that ensures everything below runs properly.
words.tip: SUPER + INTEND + ENT.
words.example: The superintendent oversees the district.

words.commentary: SUPER (above, beyond) + MARKET. A supermarket goes beyond the ordinary market — larger, more comprehensive, more self-service.
words.tip: SUPER + MARKET.
words.example: I'll pick it up at the supermarket.

words.commentary: SEDE not CEDE. This is the only English word ending in SEDE. All others (precede, recede, concede) end in CEDE. It comes from Latin supersedere, to sit above.
words.tip: Ends in SEDE, not CEDE. It's the only English word that does.
words.example: The new policy will supersede the old one.

words.commentary: From Latin supplementum, a filling up. SUB (under) + PLERE (to fill). A supplement fills from below — adding what was missing to make something complete.
words.tip: Two P's. SUPPLE + MENT.
words.example: Take a daily vitamin supplement.

words.commentary: From Latin supremus, highest. Supremacy is the state of being highest — the position above all others.
words.tip: SUPREM + ACY.
words.example: The country established naval supremacy.

words.commentary: UR in the first syllable, not ER. From French surprendre, to overtake. The UR spelling preserves the French vowel sound.
words.tip: Two R's. SUR + PRISE. Not prize.
words.example: The party was a complete surprise.

words.commentary: From French surveiller, to watch over. SUR (over) + VEILLER (to watch). Surveillance is watching from above — observation maintained for security or control.
words.tip: SUR + VEILL + ANCE. French: watch over.
words.example: Security surveillance was increased.

words.commentary: From Latin susceptibilis, capable of receiving. SUB (under) + CAPERE (to take). Someone susceptible is capable of being taken — vulnerable to influence, disease, or persuasion.
words.tip: SUSCEPT + IBLE. Think: suspect.
words.example: Children are susceptible to colds.

words.tip: SUSPIC + ION. Think: suspect.
words.example: She was under suspicion.

words.commentary: From Latin suspicere, to look up at. SUB (under) + SPECERE (to look). Suspicion is looking from below — the upward glance of distrust, watching for threats from a position of vulnerability.
words.tip: SUSPIC + IOUS. Think: suspect.
words.example: The police found the behavior suspicious.

words.commentary: From Latin sustinere, to hold up. SUB (from below) + TENERE (to hold). Something sustainable can be held up from below — practices that support themselves without depleting their foundation.
words.tip: SUSTAIN + ABLE.
words.example: We need sustainable energy sources.

words.commentary: From Latin sutura, a seam. SUERE (to sew). Surgical stitching — sewing tissue together. The 'ture' ending is straightforward but the 'su' beginning can become 'sue'.
words.tip: Think 'su' as in 'sue' + 'ture' - like suing for bad stitches.
words.example: The surgeon carefully placed each suture to close the incision.

words.tip: SYLL + ABLE. Two L's.
words.example: The word has three syllables.

words.commentary: From Greek symbolon, a token. SYN (together) + BALLEIN (to throw). A symbol is something thrown together with meaning — an object or image paired with significance beyond itself.
words.tip: Remember: SYM-BOL-ISM - like the Greek 'thrown together' tokens, each part stays intact
words.example: The dove's symbolism as a peace messenger transcends cultural boundaries.

words.commentary: From Greek sympatheia, fellow feeling. SYN (together) + PATHOS (feeling). Sympathy is feeling together — your heart echoing another's emotion.
words.tip: SYM-PATH-ETIC: remember PATHOS (feeling) stays intact in the middle
words.example: The nurse was sympathetic to the patient's concerns about the surgery.

words.commentary: From Greek sympatheia, fellow feeling. SYN (together) + PATHOS (feeling). Sympathy is feeling together — sharing the emotional state of another through recognition, not experience.
words.tip: SYMPATH + Y. Think: same feeling.
words.example: She expressed her sympathy.

words.commentary: From Greek symphonia, sounding together. SYN (together) + PHONE (sound). A symphony is sounds coming together — individual instruments united in harmony.
words.tip: SYMPH + ONY. Think: same sound.
words.example: The symphony was performed beautifully.

words.commentary: From Greek symptoma, happening. SYN (together) + PIPTEIN (to fall). Things that fall together with a disease. The 'mpt' consonant cluster is unusual.
words.tip: SYMP + TOM. Silent P.
words.example: Fever is a common symptom.

words.commentary: From Greek synagoge, a bringing together. SYN (together) + AGEIN (to lead). A synagogue is where people are led together — assembly as worship.
words.tip: Remember: SYN-A-GOGUE - people gathering together, ends in -GOGUE like dialogue
words.example: The historic synagogue in the old quarter was built in the 18th century.

words.commentary: From Greek syndrome, a running together. SYN (together) + DRAMEIN (to run). A group of symptoms that run together. The 'yn' spelling rather than 'in' catches people.
words.tip: Remember SYN (together) + DROME (running) - symptoms running together, ends in -drome like hippodrome
words.example: The doctor explained that chronic fatigue syndrome involves multiple interconnected symptoms.

words.commentary: From a 1530 poem about a shepherd named Syphilus who angered Apollo. A literary name that became medical terminology. The y in place of an i is the common error.
words.tip: Remember Syphilus the shepherd - Y not I, and one L not two.
words.example: The doctor explained that syphilis is a sexually transmitted infection that requires antibiotic treatment.

words.commentary: The French name for the Shiraz grape. SY-RAH. Often confused with the spelling of Shiraz. Not 'sirah' or 'sirrah'. The y carries the sound.
words.tip: Remember the Y sound: SY-rah, like 'sigh-rah' - French origin keeps the Y
words.example: The sommelier recommended a bold Syrah from the Rhône Valley to pair with the grilled lamb.

words.commentary: From Greek syrinx, a tube. Originally a hollow tube or pipe. The 'yr' vowel combination in the middle and the soft g at the end create dual spelling traps.
words.tip: Remember the Greek 'syrinx' (pipe) - keep the 'y' and add silent 'e'
words.example: The nurse carefully drew the medication into the syringe before administering the injection.

words.commentary: From Greek systematikos, of a whole. SYN (together) + HISTANAI (to set up). Something systematic is set up together — organized into a coherent whole where every part relates to every other.
words.tip: Think 'system' + 'atic' - one 'm', one 't' in the middle
words.example: The scientist used a systematic approach to test each hypothesis in order.

words.commentary: From Greek tachys (fast) + kardia (heart). Fast heartbeat. The 'tachy' prefix meaning fast appears in many medical terms. The y-to-c transition trips spellers.
words.tip: Remember: TACHY (fast) + CARDIA (heart) - keep the Y in tachy
words.example: The patient's tachycardia was concerning, with a heart rate exceeding 100 beats per minute.

words.commentary: From Italian tagliare, to cut. The gli makes a 'lyee' sound in Italian. Double l, ending in e. Ribbon-shaped pasta named for being cut into strips.
words.tip: Remember: TAGLIA-TELL-E - like telling someone about cutting pasta strips
words.example: The restaurant's fresh tagliatelle with mushroom sauce was absolutely delicious.

words.commentary: From Arabic tahina, to grind. Ground sesame paste. Three syllables: ta-HEE-nee. Not 'tehini' or 'tahine'. The h is pronounced.
words.tip: Remember Arabic 'tahina' - keep the 'a' in both syllables and the 'i' ending.
words.example: She drizzled tahini over the roasted vegetables for extra flavor.

words.tip: TECHN + IQUE. French ending.
words.example: She mastered the painting technique.

words.commentary: From Greek tekhnologia, systematic treatment. TEKHNE (art/skill) + LOGOS (study). Technology is the study of skill — craft examined and systematized.
words.tip: Remember: TECH-NO-LOG-ICAL - technology needs logic, so keep the 'h' in tech and 'log' in logical
words.example: The rapid technological advancement in artificial intelligence has transformed many industries.

words.commentary: From Greek tekhnologia, systematic treatment. TEKHNE (art or skill) + LOGOS (study). Technology is the study of skill — knowledge applied to making and doing.
words.tip: TECHNO + LOGY. Think: techno + logic.
words.example: Technology is advancing rapidly.

words.commentary: From Greek tele (far) + Latin communicare (to share). Telecommunication is sharing across distance — human connection maintained despite physical separation.
words.tip: Remember: tele + communication = telecommunication (keep all letters from both parts)
words.example: The telecommunication tower provided cellular coverage to the entire rural valley.

words.commentary: From Latin temperamentum, a mixing. TEMPERARE (to mix). Temperament is your inner mixture — the blend of qualities that makes your emotional responses distinctive.
words.tip: Remember the Latin root: TEMPER-A-MENT — tempering requires mixing 'a' throughout
words.example: Her calm temperament made her perfect for handling difficult customers.

words.commentary: Don't skip the second E. TEMPER + ATURE. The word relates to tempering or moderating — a temperature is a measure of the degree of heat moderation.
words.tip: TEMPER + ATURE. Don't skip the second E.
words.example: The temperature dropped overnight.

words.commentary: From Latin temporarius, of a season. TEMPUS (time). Something temporary belongs to time — existing for a period, then passing away as all timed things must.
words.tip: TEMPOR + ARY. Ends in ARY.
words.example: This is a temporary solution.

words.commentary: From Portuguese tempero, seasoning. Adopted into Japanese cuisine from Portuguese missionaries. Not 'tempra' or 'timpura'. Three syllables: tem-PU-ra.
words.tip: Remember Portuguese 'tempero' - seasoning with u, not e or i
words.example: The restaurant's shrimp tempura was perfectly crispy and light.

words.tip: TEND + ENCY. Ends in ENCY.
words.example: He has a tendency to arrive late.

words.commentary: From Latin tendo (tendon) + Greek itis (inflammation). Also spelled 'tendonitis' — both are accepted, but tendinitis is technically preferred in medical literature.
words.tip: Remember: tendin-ITIS uses Latin 'tendin' not 'tendon' plus Greek 'itis'
words.example: The tennis player developed tendinitis in her elbow from repetitive motion.

words.commentary: From Tequila, a town in Jalisco, Mexico. TE-QUI-LA. The 'qui' is always the trouble spot. Not 'taquila' or 'tequilla'. One l, and the e comes before qui.
words.tip: Remember the Mexican town: Te-QUI-la with one L, like the place it's named after.
words.example: She ordered a margarita made with premium silver tequila and fresh lime juice.

words.commentary: From Greek teras, monster, plus byte. One trillion bytes.
words.tip: From Greek teras, monster, plus byte.
words.example: The server had two terabytes of storage.

words.commentary: From Japanese teri (luster, glaze) + yaki (grilled). Grilled with a sweet soy glaze. Four syllables: te-ree-YA-kee. Not 'terriyaki' — one r.
words.tip: Think 'teri' (one r) + 'yaki' - Japanese has no double consonants here
words.example: The chicken teriyaki was glazed to perfection and served over steamed rice.

words.commentary: From Latin terminus (boundary) + Greek logos (study). Terminology is the study of boundaries — the words that mark where one concept ends and another begins.
words.tip: Remember: TERMIN-OLOGY ends concepts, like terminal ends a journey.
words.example: The medical terminology course helped students understand complex anatomical terms.

words.commentary: From Latin territorium, land around a town. TERRA (land). Something territorial relates to land — defense of boundaries, whether geographical or personal.
words.tip: Remember TERRA (land) + TORI + AL - double R, double T
words.example: The dog became territorial when strangers approached his favorite spot in the yard.

words.commentary: From Latin territorium, land around a town. TERRA (earth). Territory is claimed earth — ground that belongs to someone or something.
words.tip: Two R's. TERRIT + ORY.
words.example: The animal defended its territory.

words.commentary: From Latin testimonium, evidence. TESTIS (witness). Testimony is what a witness provides — truth offered from direct experience under the weight of oath.
words.tip: TESTIMON + Y. Think: testify.
words.example: The witness gave her testimony.

words.commentary: Than is used in comparisons. Then refers to time or sequence.
words.tip: Than is used in comparisons.
words.example: She was taller than her brother.

words.tip: US: theater (ER). UK: theatre (RE).
words.example: We went to the theater.

words.commentary: From Greek theatron via French theatre. RE is the French original — ER is the American simplification.
words.tip: US flips to ER. UK, CA, AU, NZ keep RE.
words.example: The play opens at the theater on Friday.

words.commentary: From Greek theoretikos, speculative. THEORIA (contemplation). Theoretical knowledge comes from contemplation rather than practice — thinking before doing.
words.tip: THEORET + ICAL. Think: theory.
words.example: The concept is purely theoretical.

words.commentary: From Greek therapeutikos, of healing. THERAPEUEIN (to attend or treat). Something therapeutic heals — treatment that restores health or well-being through deliberate care.
words.tip: Remember the Greek root: THERAPY + EUTIC (healing practice)
words.example: The therapeutic massage helped reduce her chronic back pain significantly.

words.tip: THERAP + Y. Think: therapeutic.
words.example: Physical therapy helped his recovery.

words.commentary: From Greek therme (heat) + metron (measure). A thermometer measures heat — temperature made visible through the expansion and contraction of sensitive materials.
words.tip: Remember: THERMO (heat) + METER (measure) - two Ms in thermometer
words.example: The nurse checked the patient's fever with a digital thermometer.

words.commentary: OUGH makes an O sound here, one of the seven different sounds that OUGH can produce in English. Thorough shares its root with through.
words.tip: THOROUGH. Two O's, GH ending.
words.example: The inspection was thorough.

words.commentary: One H in the middle, despite how it sounds. The word originally referred to the threshing floor at a doorway, where grain was separated from chaff.
words.tip: One H in the middle. THRESH + OLD.
words.example: We are on the threshold of a new era.

words.commentary: THROUGH + OUT. The word means from one end to the other — completely through and extending outward in every direction.
words.tip: THROUGH + OUT.
words.example: It rained throughout the day.

words.commentary: From Italian tirami su, pick me up or lift me up. TIRARE (to pull) + MI (me) + SU (up). Three separate words merged into one. The caffeine in the espresso and cocoa literally picks you up.
words.tip: Remember Italian 'tirami su' - TIRA-MI-SU pulls me up with double 's'
words.example: The restaurant's homemade tiramisu was the perfect end to our Italian dinner.

words.tip: One B, two C's. TO + BACCO.
words.example: Tobacco use is harmful to health.

words.commentary: From Latin tolerantia, endurance. TOLERARE (to bear). Tolerance is bearing what is different or difficult — acceptance maintained through deliberate patience.
words.tip: TOLER + ANCE. Ends in ANCE.
words.example: Tolerance is an important value.

words.commentary: One M, two R's. TO + MORROW. The word literally means to the morning, and the double R comes from the Old English morgen.
words.tip: One M, two R's. TOM + OR + ROW.
words.example: We'll finish the project tomorrow.

words.commentary: From Old English tunge. The -gue ending is unusual and unintuitive — why not just 'tung'? The silent letters persist from historical spelling conventions that no longer match pronunciation.
words.tip: TONG + UE. The UE is at the end.
words.example: Bite your tongue before you say something rude.

words.commentary: From Latin tonsillae (tonsils) + Greek itis (inflammation). Double-l in tonsill-, then -itis. The double-l is essential.
words.tip: Remember the double-L in tonsill-: your tonsils need aLL the letters!
words.example: The doctor diagnosed her with tonsillitis and prescribed antibiotics for the throat infection.

words.commentary: From Spanish torta, cake. TORTA + ILLA (diminutive). A small flat cake. Double l makes a 'y' sound in Spanish. Not 'tortillia' or 'tortila'.
words.tip: Spanish 'tortilla' has double L making 'y' sound - remember the diminutive 'illa' ending
words.example: She warmed the tortilla on the griddle before filling it with beans and cheese.

words.commentary: From Latin totalis, entire. TOTUS (whole). A totalitarian system claims the whole — government that recognizes no limits to its authority, no private sphere beyond its reach.
words.tip: Remember TOTAL + ITARIAN: the system wants TOTAL control, not partial.
words.example: The novel depicted a dystopian totalitarian regime that monitored every citizen's movements.

words.commentary: From Old French torneiement, a jousting contest. TORNER (to turn). A tournament was originally a turning — knights wheeling their horses in combat, competition as circular battle.
words.tip: TOURNA + MENT. Think: tourney.
words.example: The tennis tournament starts Monday.

words.commentary: From French tourniquet, a turning device. TOURNER (to turn). A device turned tight to stop bleeding. The French -iquet ending is the trap — it sounds like 'ket' but is spelled with -quet.
words.tip: French TOURNER + iquet: turn tight, spell -quet not -ket
words.example: The paramedic quickly applied a tourniquet to stop the severe bleeding from the wound.

words.commentary: From Greek tracheia (windpipe) + stoma (mouth, opening). Creating a permanent opening in the windpipe. Distinguished from tracheotomy by the permanent stoma.
words.tip: Remember: trachea (windpipe) + ostomy (surgical opening) = tracheostomy
words.example: The patient required an emergency tracheostomy after the accident blocked his airway.

words.commentary: From Greek tracheia (rough artery, windpipe) + tome (cutting). Cutting into the windpipe. Not to be confused with tracheostomy (creating a permanent opening).
words.tip: Remember: trachea + otomy (cutting). The 'ea' stays together, then 'otomy' not 'ostomy'.
words.example: The emergency tracheotomy saved the patient's life when his airway became blocked.

words.commentary: From Latin traditio, a handing over. TRADERE (to hand over). A tradition is handed from one generation to the next — culture passed palm to palm.
words.tip: TRADIT + ION.
words.example: It's a family tradition.

words.tip: TRAG + EDY. Ends in EDY.
words.example: The earthquake was a terrible tragedy.

words.commentary: From Latin trajectoria, a crossing. TRANS (across) + JACERE (to throw). A trajectory is the path of something thrown across space — the arc that connects launch to landing.
words.tip: Remember: JECT like 'projectile' - something thrown across (trajectory).
words.example: The asteroid's trajectory will bring it close to Earth next month.

words.commentary: From Latin transigere, to drive through. TRANS (across) + AGERE (to drive). A transaction drives something across — value moved from one party to another.
words.tip: TRANSACT + ION.
words.example: The transaction was completed.

words.commentary: From Latin transformare, to change shape. TRANS (across) + FORMA (shape). Transformation is crossing from one shape to another — change so thorough that the original becomes unrecognizable.
words.tip: TRANSFORM + ATION.
words.example: The city has undergone a transformation.

words.tip: TRANSIT + ION.
words.example: The transition was smooth.

words.commentary: From Latin transmittere, to send across. TRANS (across) + MITTERE (to send). A transmission sends across — information, power, or disease crossing from source to receiver.
words.tip: Remember: TRANS + MISSION - it's a mission to send across, double S and I-O-N
words.example: The radio transmission from the space station was crystal clear.

words.commentary: From Latin transparere, to show through. TRANS (through) + PARERE (to appear). Transparency is appearing through — clarity that allows seeing past surfaces to what lies beneath.
words.tip: Remember PARENT in the middle: trans-PARENT-cy shows through clearly
words.example: The company's financial transparency helped build trust with investors.

words.commentary: From Latin transparere, to show through. TRANS (through) + PARERE (to appear). Something transparent lets appearance pass through — visibility unobstructed.
words.tip: TRANSPAR + ENT. Ends in ENT.
words.example: The glass was completely transparent.

words.commentary: British English doubles the L before adding -ING because the stress pattern doesn't matter in British spelling rules. American English only doubles when the stress falls on the final syllable, hence traveling.
words.tip: US: one L (traveling). UK/AU/NZ: two L's (travelling).
words.example: She enjoys travelling to new countries.

words.commentary: From Latin tremendus, causing trembling. TREMERE (to tremble). Something tremendous causes trembling — awe so great the body shakes in response.
words.tip: TREMEND + OUS.
words.example: It was a tremendous success.

words.commentary: From French trier, to sort. Sorting patients by severity. The French '-iage' ending pronounced 'ahzh' is the spelling trap — people write 'treage' or 'triaje'.
words.tip: Think 'tri-' (three) + French '-age' ending: medical triage sorts into priority groups.
words.example: The emergency room nurse performed triage to determine which patients needed immediate attention.

words.commentary: From Latin triumphus, a ceremonial procession. Roman triumphs were elaborate victory parades. The PH preserves the Greek-via-Latin spelling convention.
words.tip: TRI + UMPH. PH not F.
words.example: The team celebrated their triumph.

words.commentary: From Latin triumphalis, of a triumph. TRIUMPHUS (a ceremonial procession). Being triumphant means achieving the ancient Roman equivalent of a victory parade — success celebrated publicly.
words.tip: Remember the 'mph' from Roman TRIUMPH - like elephant but with triumph!
words.example: The triumphant team paraded through the streets after winning the championship.

words.tip: TROPIC + AL.
words.example: The tropical island was paradise.

words.commentary: From trouble plus shoot. To shoot at the source of a problem.
words.tip: From trouble plus shoot.
words.example: It took an hour to troubleshoot the network issue.

words.commentary: Drop the E from true. Unlike most LY additions (nicely, safely), true loses its final E. This is one of those exceptions you simply memorize.
words.tip: TRUE drops the E. TRU + LY.
words.example: I am truly grateful for your help.

words.commentary: From Japanese tsu, harbor and nami, wave — a harbor wave, named for where the destruction is most visible. The T is pronounced in Japanese and increasingly in English.
words.tip: From Japanese tsu (harbor) + nami (wave). The T is not silent.
words.example: The tsunami warning system gave coastal residents time to evacuate.

words.commentary: From Latin tuberculum, small swelling. TUBER plus CUL plus OSIS.
words.tip: From Latin tuberculum, small swelling.
words.example: Tuberculosis was once the leading cause of death in cities.

words.commentary: From Latin turbulentia, confusion. TURBA (a crowd or disturbance). Turbulence is the disturbance of smooth flow — whether air disrupting a flight or emotions disrupting a mind.
words.tip: Remember TURBU-LENCE: the Latin TURBA (crowd) creates L-E-N-C-E disturbance.
words.example: The airplane shook violently as it encountered severe turbulence over the mountains.

words.commentary: From Medieval Latin terra merita, merited earth. A golden spice. The first r is often dropped in speech ('tumeric'), causing the misspelling. Both r's are essential: tur-MER-ic.
words.tip: Remember 'terra merita' - merited earth needs both R's: TUR-MER-ic
words.example: She added a pinch of turmeric to the curry for its anti-inflammatory properties.

words.commentary: From Japanese taikun, great lord — a title for the shogun used with foreign diplomats. American naval officers brought it back and it shifted to mean a powerful businessman.
words.tip: From Japanese taikun, great lord. Entered English via American diplomats.
words.example: The media tycoon owned newspapers across three continents.

words.commentary: Two N's and a Y. The word comes from Greek tyrannos, an absolute ruler. The double N preserves the Greek original.
words.tip: TYRANN + Y. Two N's.
words.example: The people rose up against tyranny.

words.commentary: From Greek, possibly from Turkish cacik. A yogurt and cucumber sauce. The tz combination at the start is rare in English. Pronounced 'tsah-TSEE-kee'. Often misspelled 'tatziki' or 'tzaziki'.
words.tip: Remember the Greek double-tz pattern: tzaTZiki - two 'tz' sounds like pizza
words.example: The restaurant served warm pita bread with homemade tzatziki and olive oil.

words.commentary: From Japanese udon, thick wheat noodles. Two syllables: oo-DON. No tricks — straightforward spelling once you know the word.
words.tip: Think 'you-don' — just replace 'you' with 'u' for this Japanese noodle.
words.example: The restaurant serves steaming bowls of udon in rich miso broth.

words.commentary: From Latin ultimatus, last. ULTIMUS (farthest). Ultimately means at the farthest point — the final destination of logic or time.
words.tip: Remember ULTIMATE + LY: the ultimate spelling ends with -ate before -ly
words.example: The investigation ultimately revealed the truth about what happened that night.

words.commentary: From Latin ultra (beyond) + Old English sund (sound). Sound waves beyond human hearing used for imaging. One of the few medical terms that is phonetically transparent.
words.tip: Remember: ULTRA (beyond) + SOUND - no hyphen, keep the 'n' in sound
words.example: The doctor used ultrasound to check on the baby's development during the pregnancy.

words.commentary: From Latin unanimis, of one mind. UNUS (one) + ANIMUS (mind). A unanimous decision comes from one mind — complete agreement where every voice says the same thing.
words.tip: UN + ANIM + OUS. Think: one mind.
words.example: The vote was unanimous.

words.commentary: UNDER + ESTIMATE. To underestimate is to estimate below the truth — to calculate less than reality warrants.
words.tip: UNDER + ESTIMATE.
words.example: Don't underestimate the challenge.

words.commentary: From UNDER + GRADUATE. An undergraduate is under the level of graduation — still climbing toward the degree.
words.tip: Remember: UNDER + GRAD + U + ATE - you're under graduation, so keep the U!
words.example: As an undergraduate student, she worked part-time at the campus library.

words.tip: UN + EMPLOY + MENT.
words.example: Unemployment rates have dropped.

words.commentary: UN + FORGET + TABLE. Something unforgettable cannot be forgotten — it embeds itself in memory and refuses to leave.
words.tip: UN + FORGET + TABLE. Two T's.
words.example: It was an unforgettable experience.

words.commentary: From UN + FORTUNATE. Unfortunately means without fortune's favor — luck looking the other way when you needed it most.
words.tip: UNFORTUNATE + LY. Keep the E.
words.example: Unfortunately, the event was cancelled.

words.tip: UNI + FORM. One form.
words.example: Students must wear a uniform.

words.commentary: From Latin universalis, of the whole. UNUS (one) + VERSUS (turned). Universal turns everything into one — a single truth that applies everywhere.
words.tip: Remember UNI-VER-SAL: UNI (one) turns VERything into one SAL-ution.
words.example: The need for clean water is a universal concern that affects all communities worldwide.

words.tip: UNIVERS + ITY. Think: universe of study.
words.example: She studied at Oxford University.

words.commentary: UN + NECESSARY. Two N's where the prefix meets the root, and one C followed by two S's in the root itself.
words.tip: UN + NECESSARY. One C, two S's.
words.example: Avoid unnecessary expenses.

words.commentary: From UN (not) + PRECEDENTED. Something unprecedented has no precedent — an event with no prior example, standing alone in history.
words.tip: Remember: UN + PRECEDENT + ED. Think 'no precedent exists before this.'
words.example: The pandemic created unprecedented challenges for healthcare systems worldwide.

words.commentary: UN + PREDICT + ABLE. Something unpredictable cannot be predicted — it defies forecasting and planning.
words.tip: UN + PREDICT + ABLE.
words.example: The weather is unpredictable.

words.commentary: One L. Unlike untill in older English, the modern spelling has simplified to a single L. The word is a combination of un + til (to).
words.tip: One L at the end. UN + TIL.
words.example: Wait until the light turns green.

words.commentary: UP + HEAVAL (from heave). An upheaval heaves things up — it overturns the established order from below.
words.tip: UP + HEAVAL. Think: heave up.
words.example: The revolution caused social upheaval.

words.tip: US + AGE. No E before AGE.
words.example: Data usage has increased.

words.commentary: From user plus name. The identifier for a computer account.
words.tip: From user plus name.
words.example: She forgot her username and password.

words.tip: US: Z. UK: S. UTILIZ + ATION.
words.example: Better utilization of resources is needed.

words.commentary: From Latin vacca, a cow. Edward Jenner used cowpox to prevent smallpox — the word vaccination preserves the bovine origin of immunology's greatest triumph.
words.tip: Two C's. VACCINAT + ION.
words.example: Vaccination protects against disease.

words.commentary: From Latin vacuum, empty space. VACUUS (empty). Double-u is extremely rare in English — vacuum is one of the few words that has it. One c, two u's.
words.tip: One C, two U's. The vacuum sucks up U's.
words.example: Please vacuum the living room before guests arrive.

words.tip: VALU + ABLE. No E before ABLE.
words.example: The ring was very valuable.

words.tip: VARIAT + ION. Think: vary.
words.example: There is very little variation.

words.commentary: From Latin vegetabilis, animating. VEGERE (to enliven). Vegetables were originally anything that enlivened — the word narrowed from all growing things to what's on your plate.
words.tip: VEGET + ABLE. E in the middle.
words.example: Eat your vegetables every day.

words.commentary: From Latin vegetare, to enliven. VEGETUS (lively). Vegetation is living plant growth — the earth enlivened by green things reaching toward the sun.
words.tip: VEGETAT + ION. Think: vegetate.
words.example: The tropical vegetation was lush.

words.commentary: The H after VE is silent but etymologically important — it comes from Latin vehiculum, where the H represented an aspirated sound.
words.tip: VE + HICLE. Ends in CLE, not CAL.
words.example: The vehicle was parked in the driveway.

words.commentary: EANCE ending. The word comes from Old French vengeance, itself from Latin vindicare, to avenge. The G stays soft because of the E that follows.
words.tip: Remember: venge-ANCE (likerance, stance) - the A comes before the N
words.example: He sought vengeance against those who had wronged his family.

words.commentary: From Latin ventilare, to fan. VENTUS (wind). Ventilation is artificial wind — air moved through spaces to maintain the freshness that closed rooms lose.
words.tip: Think VENT + -ilation: one 'l' like the wind vents through
words.example: The hospital's ventilation system circulates fresh air throughout every ward.

words.tip: VENT + URE. Think: adventure.
words.example: The business venture was risky.

words.commentary: From Latin verbatim, word by word — from verbum, word. The IM ending is an ablative case marker meaning by word. Not verbatum.
words.tip: Latin for word for word. Ends in IM not UM.
words.example: She quoted the contract verbatim during the dispute.

words.commentary: From Italian verde, green. VER-DIC-CHIO. An Italian white wine grape. Double c, and the 'chio' ending. Not 'verdikio' or 'verdichio'. The double c before 'chio' is key.
words.tip: Remember Italian 'verde' (green) plus double-c before 'chio' ending
words.example: The sommelier recommended a crisp verdicchio from the Marche region to pair with the seafood.

words.commentary: From Latin verificare, to make true. VERUS (true) + FACERE (to make). Verification makes something true — or rather, confirms that it was true all along.
words.tip: VERIFIC + ATION. Think: verify.
words.example: Email verification is required.

words.commentary: From German Wermut, wormwood. VER-MOUTH. Despite sounding like 'ver-mooth', it is spelled with 'mouth'. Not 'vermuth' or 'vermoouth'.
words.tip: Remember: German Wermut became vermouth - it ends with 'mouth' not 'muth'
words.example: She stirred a splash of dry vermouth into the gin for a perfect martini.

words.commentary: From Latin versatilis, turning easily. VERTERE (to turn). Something versatile turns easily between different uses — adaptability as a form of fluid rotation.
words.tip: VERSAT + ILE. Think: versatility.
words.example: She is a versatile performer.

words.commentary: From Latin vertigo, whirling. VERTERE (to turn). A sensation of spinning. The word itself is straightforward Latin, but the soft g is sometimes written as j.
words.tip: Remember VERT (turn) + IGO - like 'I go' spinning when I turn
words.example: The medication helped reduce her episodes of vertigo that made climbing stairs difficult.

words.commentary: From Latin veterinarius, relating to beasts of burden. VETERINAE (draft animals). Veterinary medicine began with working animals — the horses and oxen that powered civilization.
words.tip: VETERIN + ARIAN. Think: vet.
words.example: The veterinarian examined the dog.

words.commentary: Named after Vichy, France. A cold potato-leek soup. The ss before oise and the silent e at the end follow French patterns. Pronounced 'vish-ee-SWAHZ'.
words.tip: Remember Vichy + ssoise: double 's' before the French ending like in bouillabaisse
words.example: The restaurant's signature vichyssoise was perfectly chilled and garnished with fresh chives.

words.tip: VICIN + ITY. C not S.
words.example: Is there a pharmacy in the vicinity?

words.commentary: From Latin vigilare, to be awake. VIGIL (watchful). Someone vigilant stays awake and watchful — alertness sustained through deliberate effort.
words.tip: VIGIL + ANT. Ends in ANT.
words.example: Security guards must remain vigilant.

words.commentary: From Spanish vigilante, watchman — itself from Latin vigilare, to watch. The final E is spoken, unlike most English words ending in E.
words.tip: From Spanish vigilar, to watch. Four syllables: vi-gi-LAN-te.
words.example: The vigilante took justice into his own hands.

words.tip: VILL + AIN. Ends in AIN not IAN.
words.example: The villain was finally caught.

words.tip: Vin (wine) plus aigre (sour) plus ette — wine gone sour.
words.example: She drizzled vinaigrette over the salad.
words.commentary: From French vinaigre, vinegar. Wine gone sour, then made into a dressing.
A French white wine grape. VI-OG-NIER. Not 'viogner' or 'veognier'. The i appears twice, and the 'gn' makes a French nasal sound.
words.tip: Remember: VI-OG-NIER has two i's and French 'gn' like 'champagne'
words.example: The sommelier recommended a crisp viognier from the Rhône Valley to pair with the seafood.

words.tip: VIOLAT + ION. Think: violate.
words.example: That is a violation of the rules.

words.tip: VIOL + ENCE. Ends in ENCE.
words.example: Violence is never the answer.

words.commentary: From virtual plus ization. Creating virtual versions of hardware.
words.tip: From virtual plus ization.
words.example: Virtualization reduced the number of physical servers needed.

words.commentary: From Latin visibilis, able to be seen. Visibility measures how far you can see — it quantifies the clarity of sight.
words.tip: VISIBIL + ITY. Think: visible.
words.example: Visibility was poor due to fog.

words.commentary: IBLE not ABLE. The word comes from Latin visibilis, and the IBLE ending has been preserved from the original Latin suffix.
words.tip: Ends in IBLE, not ABLE. VIS + IBLE.
words.example: The mountain was visible from the highway.

words.commentary: From Latin vocabulum, a name or word. VOCARE (to call). Vocabulary is the collection of names you can call things — your available words, the tools of expression.
words.tip: VOCABUL + ARY. Ends in ARY.
words.example: Reading expands your vocabulary.

words.tip: VOLCAN + O. Think: Vulcan.
words.example: The volcano erupted violently.

words.commentary: From Latin voluntarius, of free will. VOLUNTAS (will). Voluntary action comes from free will — chosen, not compelled.
words.tip: VOLUNT + ARY. Ends in ARY.
words.example: The work is voluntary.

words.commentary: From French volontaire, willing. Latin VOLUNTAS (will). A volunteer acts from will alone — service offered freely, without compulsion or compensation.
words.tip: Remember: volunteer has UN like 'unwilling' - but volunteers are willing!
words.example: She decided to volunteer at the local food bank every weekend.

words.commentary: From Latin vulnerabilis, capable of being wounded. VULNUS (wound). Vulnerability is the capacity to be wounded — openness to harm that is also the prerequisite for connection.
words.tip: Remember VULNER-ABIL-ITY: able to be wounded, with all vowels intact
words.example: The cybersecurity team conducted regular assessments to identify potential vulnerability points in the network.

words.commentary: From Latin vulnerabilis, able to be wounded. VULNUS (wound). The vulnerable can be wounded — openness that risks pain for the sake of connection.
words.tip: VULNER + ABLE. Don't skip the L.
words.example: Children are particularly vulnerable.

words.commentary: From Old English wandrian, to move about. A wanderer moves without fixed destination — the journey itself is the purpose.
words.tip: WANDER + ER. Not wonder.
words.example: The wanderer traveled far.

words.commentary: From German Wanderlust, hiking desire — wandern means to hike rather than to wander, making the etymology slightly misleading in English.
words.tip: From German wandern (to hike) + Lust (desire). One word.
words.example: Her wanderlust led her to visit forty countries before thirty.

words.commentary: From WARE + HOUSE. A warehouse houses wares — storage given a straightforward name for once.
words.tip: WARE + HOUSE.
words.example: The warehouse stores inventory.

words.commentary: From Old French warant, guarantee. WAR plus RANT.
words.tip: From Old French warant, guarantee.
words.example: The police obtained a search warrant.

words.tip: Two R's. WARRANT + Y.
words.example: The warranty covers two years.

words.commentary: Not whether or wether. Weather refers to atmospheric conditions, and the EA spelling distinguishes it from its homophones.
words.tip: WEA + THER. Don't confuse with whether (choice) or wether (sheep).
words.example: The weather forecast calls for rain.

words.commentary: From Old English Wodnesdaeg, Woden's day. Named after the Norse god Odin (Woden in Old English). The first d is silent in modern pronunciation, making this a perpetual spelling trap.
words.tip: WED + NES + DAY. Named after Woden (Norse god). The D is silent.
words.example: The meeting is scheduled for Wednesday.

words.commentary: EI not IE, breaking the I-before-E rule. The word originally meant fate or destiny in Old English, and its spelling has always been unusual — fittingly weird.
words.tip: WE are WEIRD. E before I. It breaks the rule, because it's weird.
words.example: That was a weird coincidence.

words.commentary: From German Weiss (white) + Bier (beer). WEISS-BIER. Double s. Not 'weisbier' or 'wiessbier'. The 'ei' makes 'eye' and the 'ie' makes 'ee' — classic German spelling pattern.
words.tip: Remember German 'ei' = 'eye' sound, double 's' like in 'weiss' (white)
words.example: The brewery's traditional weissbier had a cloudy appearance and citrusy flavor.

words.tip: WHEEL + CHAIR.
words.example: The venue is wheelchair accessible.

words.commentary: Whether introduces alternatives. Weather is atmospheric conditions.
words.tip: Whether introduces alternatives.
words.example: She was not sure whether to accept the offer.

words.commentary: From Irish Gaelic uisce beatha, water of life. UISCE (water) + BEATHA (life). The spelling 'whiskey' (with an e) is used in Ireland and the United States, while 'whisky' (without the e) is used in Scotland, Canada, and Japan. The difference traces back to regional transliterations of the Gaelic original.
words.tip: WHISKEY (US/Ireland) has an E. WHISKY (Scotland/Canada) does not.
words.example: Irish whiskey is smooth and rich.

words.commentary: The Scottish and Canadian spelling of the spirit, from Gaelic uisce beatha, water of life. While 'whiskey' (with an e) is Irish and American, 'whisky' (without) is the Scottish, Canadian, and Japanese standard. Both are correct — geography determines the spelling.
words.tip: Scottish whisky has no 'e' — remember 'Scotland' has no 'e' either
words.example: The single malt whisky from the Highlands had a smoky, peated flavor.

words.commentary: From WHOLE + SALE. Wholesale is selling in whole quantities — commerce at scale, where bulk replaces individual transaction.
words.tip: WHOLE + SALE.
words.example: They buy goods at wholesale prices.

words.commentary: WIDE + SPREAD. Something widespread has spread wide — extending across a broad area. The compound describes its own meaning.
words.tip: WIDE + SPREAD. One word.
words.example: The damage was widespread.

words.commentary: From Old English wilddeornes, place of wild animals. WILD + DEOR (animal). The wilderness belongs to wild animals — the land beyond human control.
words.tip: WILDER + NESS.
words.example: They hiked through the wilderness.

words.commentary: From wire plus less. Without wires.
words.tip: From wire plus less.
words.example: She switched to a wireless keyboard and mouse.

words.tip: WISD + OM. Think: wise.
words.example: With age comes wisdom.

words.commentary: From WITH (away) + DRAW. A withdrawal draws away — removing yourself or your resources from a situation.
words.tip: WITH + DRAW + AL. Ends in AL.
words.example: The withdrawal was processed.

words.tip: WIT + NESS. One T.
words.example: The witness testified in court.

words.commentary: Named after Worcester, England. Pronounced 'WUSS-ter-sher' despite having eleven letters. Possibly the most disproportionate spelling-to-pronunciation ratio in English. The trick: say 'Wooster-sher' and you are close enough.
words.tip: Remember: Worcester + shire. The city name keeps its tricky spelling intact.
words.example: She added a dash of Worcestershire sauce to enhance the marinade's flavor.

words.commentary: WORTH + WHILE. Something worthwhile is worth the time spent — the reward justifies the investment of attention.
words.tip: WORTH + WHILE. One word.
words.example: The effort was worthwhile.

words.commentary: One T. WRITE drops the E and adds ING without doubling. The silent W at the start of write is another Old English relic.
words.tip: One T. WRITE drops the E, adds ING.
words.example: She is passionate about creative writing.

words.commentary: From Greek xenos (stranger) + phobos (fear). Xenophobia is fear of strangers — the instinct to reject what is unfamiliar.
words.tip: XENO + PHOBIA. Fear of strangers.
words.example: Xenophobia leads to discrimination.

words.commentary: From Greek xylon (wood) + phone (sound). A xylophone makes sound from wood — music created by striking wooden bars, each cut to a different pitch.
words.tip: Remember: XYLon (wood) + PHONe (sound) - keep the Y and PH
words.example: The child's first music lesson began with learning to play simple melodies on the xylophone.

words.commentary: From Dutch jacht, a fast ship. JAGEN (to hunt). A hunting ship. The ach is completely silent — one of the most phonetically misleading words in English.
words.tip: YACHT. The CH is silent.
words.example: They sailed the yacht across the harbor.

words.commentary: YEAR + BOOK. A book that captures a year — a time capsule between covers.
words.tip: YEAR + BOOK.
words.example: She signed everyone's yearbook.

words.commentary: From Old English giestran (yester) + daeg (day). The YESTER prefix is one of the oldest time-words in English, surviving only in yesterday and a few archaic forms.
words.tip: YESTER + DAY.
words.example: I saw her yesterday.

words.tip: I before E. Y + IELD.
words.example: The investment will yield a good return.

words.commentary: YOUTH + FUL. Full of youth — carrying the energy and appearance of early life. One L at the end, following the standard suffix rule.
words.tip: YOUTH + FUL. One L.
words.example: She has a youthful appearance.

words.commentary: From Italian, possibly from Late Latin sabaia (an Illyrian drink). A custard of egg yolks, sugar, and wine. The gli makes a 'lyee' sound. Often simplified to 'zabaione' — both spellings exist.
words.tip: Remember the Italian 'gli' makes 'lyee' sound - like 'million' but with 'gl'
words.example: The chef prepared a rich zabaglione with Marsala wine for dessert.

words.commentary: From Greek zelos, eager rivalry. Zealousness is eager devotion — passion so intense it borders on obsession, commitment that knows no moderation.
words.tip: ZEAL + OUS. Think: zeal.
words.example: She was a zealous supporter of the cause.

words.commentary: From German Zeit, time and Geist, spirit. The intellectual and cultural climate of an era — another concept English borrowed wholesale because no English word captured it.
words.tip: From German Zeit (time) + Geist (spirit). The spirit of the age.
words.example: The film perfectly captured the zeitgeist of the 1980s.

words.commentary: Probably from German Zickzack. The word looks like what it means — the Z's zigzag across the spelling.
words.tip: ZIG + ZAG. One word.
words.example: The road zigzags up the mountain.

words.commentary: From Greek zoion (animal) + logos (study). Zoology is the study of animals — the branch of biology that observes, classifies, and seeks to understand every creature that moves.
words.tip: Zoo + logy: animals at the zoo need logical study
words.example: She decided to major in zoology after volunteering at the wildlife sanctuary.

words.commentary: From Italian zucchino, a small squash. ZUCCA (squash). The double C and the INI ending are authentically Italian.
words.tip: Two C's, one H. ZUCCH + INI. Italian word.
words.example: Add diced zucchini to the soup.
